output: Your toddler needs foods from the same four basic nutrition groups: 1) Meat, fish, poultry, eggs, beans; 2) A source of calcium (dairy products, beans, dark green vegetables, seeds, tofu); 3) Fruits and vegetables; 4) Whole-grain cereal, bread, and pasta; potatoes; rice.

instruction: My child isn’t walking yet — when should I be concerned?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your child cannot walk by eighteen months, this is a sign of possible developmental delay and you should alert your pediatrician.

instruction: Can I speak more than one language at home with my child?
output: Yes. If you speak more than one language, use both at home. Speak with your child in whatever language(s) you feel comfortable in.

instruction: How can I teach my toddler about hot things like the stove?
output: Begin to explain safety in simple terms. For example, feeling the heat from the stove without touching it can teach the meaning and danger of hot objects.

instruction: What should I do when stressful things happen in my child’s life?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. When stressful things happen, take extra time to cuddle with and reassure your child, and consider discussing them with your pediatrician as well. Also, avoid adverse childhood experiences and other causes of chronic stress when at all possible.

instruction: Is it important for caregivers to form a loving relationship with my child?
output: Yes. Make sure other people who provide care and supervision for your child understand the importance of forming a loving and comforting relationship with him.

instruction: How can I help my child talk about feelings?
output: Help your child use words to describe emotions and to express feelings such as happiness, joy, anger, and fear. Describe your own emotions with words and help your child use simple labels like “glad,” “mad,” and “sad.”

instruction: What are some simple ways to stimulate language and thinking during daily routines?
output: Talk or sing to your child during dressing, bathing, feeding, playing, walking, and driving. Speak slowly and give time for your child to respond. Listen and expand on your child’s phrases (for example, when your child says “Milk?” you might say “That’s right, that is milk. Do you want some milk? We can put it in a blue cup.”). Ask questions and listen to and answer your child’s questions. Play fun, calm, melodic music and read every day.

instruction: What childcare should I look for and how often should I visit?
output: Choose quality childcare that is affectionate, responsive, educational, and safe. Visit your childcare provider frequently and share your ideas about positive caregiving.

instruction: My toddler is acting defiant and wanting more independence — is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Before two years of age children demonstrate increasing independence and begin to show defiant behavior, particularly with adults with whom they feel comfortable. You may also see increasing episodes of separation anxiety toward midyear that then fade.

instruction: Should I restrict cholesterol and other fats in my baby's diet?
output: No. Cholesterol and other fats should not be restricted for babies and young toddlers because they are very important for normal growth and development. Babies and young toddlers should get about half of their calories from fat, and fat intake can be gradually decreased once your child reaches age two to about one-third of daily calories by ages four to five. Healthy fats include avocado, olive oil, fish, nut butters, and dairy; unhealthy fats include fried foods, fast foods, and many packaged foods.

instruction: If I keep my toddler's daily calories around 1,000, do I need to worry about overfeeding and weight gain?
output: If you keep your child’s daily caloric intake at about 1,000 calories, you needn’t worry about overfeeding and risk of weight gain.

instruction: Can my one-year-old eat the same foods as the rest of the family? What precautions should I take?
output: By his first birthday your child should be able to handle most foods served to the rest of the family, but take precautions: make sure food is cool enough (test the temperature yourself), avoid heavily spiced, salted, buttered, or sweetened foods, and make sure anything you give is mashed or cut into small, easily chewable pieces. Never offer whole nuts, whole grapes (cut in half or quarters are OK), cherry tomatoes unless cut in quarters, raw carrots, popcorn, seeds, whole or large sections of hot dogs or meat sticks, hard or gummy candies, or chunks of peanut butter (thinly spread peanut butter on a cracker or bread is OK). Hot dogs and carrots in particular should be quartered lengthwise and sliced into small pieces. Also have the toddler eat only while seated and supervised and teach him to finish a mouthful before speaking.

instruction: Is it safe to give my one-year-old peanut butter?
output: Do not offer chunks of peanut butter. Thinly spread peanut butter on a cracker or bread is OK. Also avoid whole nuts because they can be a choking hazard.

instruction: How should I serve grapes and cherry tomatoes to my toddler?
output: Do not give whole grapes; cut them in half or quarters. Do not give cherry tomatoes whole; cut them in quarters before offering them to your toddler.

instruction: When do children learn to chew with a grinding motion?
output: Children don’t learn to chew with a grinding motion until they’re about four years old.

instruction: Should my toddler eat only while seated and supervised?
output: Yes. Make sure your toddler eats only while seated and supervised by an adult. Eating on the run or while talking increases the risk of choking, so teach him to finish a mouthful before speaking.

instruction: Do I have to wean my baby from breastfeeding at one year? I'm worried about continuing to nurse.
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. You do not have to wean at one year. The AAP recommends breastfeeding in combination with complementary foods until at least twelve months of age and continuing breastfeeding for as long as it is mutually desired by mother and baby. Many babies continue to breastfeed into toddlerhood, and breast milk continues to be a good source of nutrition and retains its disease-fighting properties. Feedings around sleep times are often the last to go, and remember to brush teeth after those feedings.

instruction: How should I prepare hot dogs and raw carrots to reduce choking risk?
output: Hot dogs and carrots should be quartered lengthwise and then sliced into small pieces. Also avoid offering whole or large sections of hot dogs and make sure all foods are mashed or cut into small, easily chewable pieces.

instruction: Should I give my one-year-old heavily spiced, salted, buttered, or sweetened foods?
output: No. Don’t give heavily spiced, salted, buttered, or sweetened foods; they mask the natural taste of foods and may be harmful to long-term health. Young children seem to be more sensitive than adults to these flavorings and may reject heavily spiced or seasoned foods.

instruction: What toys are appropriate for children under two years old?
output: Appropriate toys before two years include board books with large pictures and simple stories, books and magazines with photos of babies, blocks, nesting toys, simple shape sorters and pegboards, beginner’s jigsaw puzzles, toys that encourage make-believe play (child lawnmower, kitchen sets, brooms), digging toys (bucket, shovel, rake), dolls of all sizes, cars, trucks, trains, unbreakable containers, bath toys (boats, containers, floating squeak toys), balls of all shapes and sizes except balls that could possibly fit in the mouth, push and pull toys, outdoor toys (slides, swings, sandbox), beginner’s tricycle, connecting toys (links, large stringing beads, S-shapes), stuffed animals, children’s musical instruments, large crayons, toy telephone, unbreakable mirrors, dress-up clothes, and safe household items like wooden spoons, old magazines, baskets, and cardboard boxes.

instruction: Can my toddler play with balls? Are there any size concerns?
output: Yes, balls of all shapes and sizes are appropriate before two years, except balls that could possibly fit in the mouth should be avoided.

instruction: When should my child start drinking from a cup and drink less milk?
output: Around his first birthday your toddler should drink liquids from a cup. He’ll need less milk then, since he’ll get most of his calories from solid foods.

instruction: I'm worried my two-year-old doesn't imitate actions, won't follow simple instructions, and can't push a wheeled toy. What does that mean?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. The items you describe are noted indicators for concern by two years: not imitating actions or words, not following simple instructions, and not being able to push a wheeled toy are all mentioned as signs to watch for by two years of age.

instruction: What toys are appropriate for a child under two years old?
output: Board books with large pictures and simple stories; books and magazines with photographs of babies; blocks; nesting toys; simple shape sorters and pegboards; beginner’s jigsaw puzzles; make-believe toys (child lawnmower, kitchen sets, brooms); digging toys (bucket, shovel, rake); dolls of all sizes; cars, trucks, trains; unbreakable containers of all shapes and sizes; bath toys (boats, containers, floating squeak toys); balls of all shapes and sizes except ones that could fit in the mouth; push and pull toys; outdoor toys (slides, swings, sandbox); beginner’s tricycle; connecting toys (links, large stringing beads, S-shapes); stuffed animals; children’s musical instruments; large crayons; toy telephone; unbreakable mirrors of all sizes; dress-up clothes; and safe, unbreakable household items like wooden spoons, old magazines, baskets, cardboard boxes, pots and pans.

instruction: Can I start giving my baby a sippy or straw cup yet?
output: Yes. Cup feeding (sippy or straw cup) can be introduced as early as six months, and by one year it should be mastered.

instruction: I'm worried my toddler still uses a bottle at night — will that harm his teeth?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Infants who sleep with a bottle containing anything other than water are at high risk of tooth decay. It is recommended the bottle be given up entirely at around age one and almost certainly by eighteen months. If you must give a bottle, limit the contents to plain water.

instruction: How should I wean my child off the bottle?
output: Eliminate the midday bottle first, then the evening and morning ones, saving the bedtime bottle for last. If the baby won’t take a bottle with plain water at first, gradually dilute the formula or other contents with water over a week or two until the bottle contains only water.

instruction: How much vitamin D does a toddler need each day?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends toddlers consume at least 600 IU (15 mcg) of vitamin D a day. Since most children’s diets don’t supply this much, many pediatricians recommend a daily vitamin supplement. After age one, vitamin D drops of 600 IU (15 mcg) per day are mentioned.

instruction: We eat mostly vegetarian at home — could my toddler need supplements?
output: If your family’s dietary practices limit food groups (for example, a strictly vegetarian household or one with no eggs or dairy), your child may need supplements such as vitamins B12 and D, riboflavin, and calcium. Consult your pediatrician about which supplements are needed and their amounts.

instruction: How much iron do toddlers need and what if my child drinks a lot of milk?
output: Toddlers need at least 15 mg of daily iron in their food. Drinking 16 ounces (480 ml) of milk or less each day is little cause for concern if the child is also eating a healthy diet full of iron-rich foods. If he drinks much more than that and you can’t get him to eat more iron-rich foods, consult your pediatrician about adding an iron supplement. Combining iron-rich foods with fruits high in vitamin C can help absorb more iron, and cooking in a cast-iron skillet can add iron to the diet.

instruction: When should my child’s lead level be checked?
output: It is important to have your child’s lead level checked at the one- and two-year check-ups.

instruction: Are high doses of vitamins or minerals safe for toddlers?
output: High doses of some vitamins, like the fat-soluble vitamins A and D, may pose risks because they are stored in the tissues and at very high levels could make a child sick. Large doses of minerals such as zinc and iron taken over time can have negative effects as well. Always discuss vitamins and supplements with your pediatrician to ensure appropriate amounts.

instruction: My baby cries for the bottle at night and won’t settle—what can I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your baby cries for only a short time, try letting him cry back to sleep; after a few nights he’ll probably forget about the bottle. If that doesn’t work, consult your pediatrician. For comfort, let him use a cuddly toy, blanket, or his thumb—but never a bottle or cup containing anything but plain water.

instruction: Can my toddler have a small snack before bedtime?
output: A short breastfeeding, a drink of cow’s milk or other liquid, or some fruit or another nutritious food is acceptable before bedtime. If the snack is in a bottle, gradually phase in use of a cup. Whatever the snack, have your child finish it and then clean his teeth using a small amount (the size of a grain of rice) of fluoridated toothpaste on a soft cloth, gauze, or toothbrush.

instruction: Are stuffed animals and children’s musical instruments okay for a one-year-old?
output: Yes. Stuffed animals and children’s musical instruments are listed among toys appropriate before two years of age.

instruction: Are bath toys and unbreakable mirrors safe for toddlers?
output: Yes. Bath toys (boats, containers, floating squeak toys) and unbreakable mirrors of all sizes are listed as appropriate for children under two.

instruction: What household items are safe for my toddler to play with?
output: Safe, unbreakable items around the house include wooden spoons, old magazines, baskets, cardboard boxes, and pots and pans; these are listed as appropriate playthings.

instruction: When should I stop giving my baby a bottle?
output: It’s recommended to give up the bottle entirely at around one year and almost certainly by eighteen months. The sooner you start removing it, the easier the task. If you are breastfeeding, bottles may be avoided altogether.

instruction: Can I introduce a sippy or straw cup now? My baby is 7 months old.
output: Yes. Cup feeding (sippy or straw cup) can be introduced as early as six months and by one year should be mastered. As long as your baby is drinking from a cup, he doesn’t need to take liquids from a bottle.

instruction: Is it OK to give my toddler a bottle at bedtime with milk to help him sleep?
output: It’s best to avoid bottles at bedtime with anything other than plain water. Infants who sleep with a bottle containing anything other than water are at high risk of tooth decay. If you must give a bottle, limit the contents to plain water.

instruction: My baby refuses a bottle of plain water—how can I transition?
output: If your baby will not initially take a bottle with plain water, gradually dilute the formula or other contents with water over a short time. After a week or two the bottle should contain only water.

instruction: I still feed my baby at night. Is nighttime feeding necessary at this age?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. At this age he no longer needs anything to eat or drink at night. Nighttime feedings are a comfort rather than a nutritional necessity and can prevent his learning to fall back to sleep on his own.

instruction: How should I phase out bottles during the day?
output: Eliminate the midday bottle first, then the evening and morning ones. Save the bedtime bottle for last, but remember to limit the contents to plain water.

instruction: If my child cries for a bottle at night, should I let him cry it out?
output: If he cries for only a short time, try letting him cry back to sleep. After a few nights he’ll probably forget all about the bottle. If this doesn’t happen, consult your pediatrician.

instruction: Can I give my toddler a small snack or milk before bedtime?
output: A short breastfeeding, a drink of cow’s milk or other liquid, or some fruit or another nutritious food before bedtime is acceptable—provided his teeth are brushed afterward.

instruction: How should I clean my toddler's teeth after a bedtime snack or drink?
output: Have your child finish the snack then clean his teeth using a small amount (the size of a grain of rice) of fluoridated toothpaste on a soft cloth, gauze, or toothbrush. Not removing food or liquid allows it to remain on the teeth all night and can result in tooth decay.

instruction: Is it okay to let my toddler use a bottle or cup to soothe himself to sleep?
output: If he needs comfort to fall asleep, let him use a cuddly toy, blanket, or his thumb—but never a bottle or cup containing anything but plain water. The bottle can become a crutch and prevent him learning to fall back to sleep on his own.

instruction: My 15-month-old throws food and dishes. What should I do about that behavior?
output: Discourage the behavior with a firm reprimand and replace the utensils in the proper location. Use unbreakable dishes, cups, and glasses. If the behavior persists, consider stopping the meal and waiting until the next.

instruction: When will my toddler be able to use utensils and a cup consistently?
output: By fifteen months he’ll have much more control and can fill his spoon and get it to his mouth most of the time. By eighteen months your toddler can use a spoon, fork, and unbreakable glass or cup, though he may not always want to.

instruction: How can I cut down on my toddler’s sweets?
output: It’s your responsibility to limit access to sweets and provide a diet primarily of nutritious foods. Either don’t bring sweets into the house or keep them hidden, avoid adding sugar to his food, and don’t make dessert an everyday event. Offer healthy snacks like fruit, whole-grain bread and crackers, and cheese. Make sure your toddler eats only while seated and supervised by an adult.

instruction: Is there a sample one-day menu I can follow for a one-year-old?
output: Yes. A sample menu for a one-year-old (about 21 pounds/9.5 kg) includes items such as ½ cup iron-fortified cereal or 1 cooked egg and ½ cup milk at breakfast; a snack of toast with cream cheese or yogurt; lunch with ½ sandwich, ½ cup cooked green vegetables, and ½ cup milk; another snack of cheese or fruit; and dinner with 2–3 ounces cooked meat, ½ cup cooked yellow or orange vegetables, ½ cup whole-grain pasta, rice, or potato, and ½ cup milk.

instruction: What unit conversions does the sample menu use for a one-year-old?
output: The menu notes 1 cup = 8 ounces = 240 ml; 1 ounce = 2 tablespoons = 30 ml; ½ ounce = 1 tablespoon = 15 ml = 3 teaspoons; 1 teaspoon = ¹⁄³ tablespoon = 5 ml.

instruction: Is this one-day menu meant for a one-year-old who weighs about 21 pounds?
output: Yes. This sample one-day menu is for a one-year-old child who weighs approximately 21 pounds (9.5 kg). It lists specific foods and portions for breakfast, two snacks, lunch, and dinner appropriate for that child.

instruction: How much milk is included at each meal in the sample menu?
output: The sample menu lists 1/2 cup of whole or 2 percent milk at breakfast, 1/2 cup at lunch, 1/2 cup at dinner, and either water or 1/2 cup whole or 2 percent milk with snacks.

instruction: What are good sources of iron for my toddler?
output: Excellent sources listed include red meats, iron-fortified whole-grain cereal, and blackstrap molasses. Other good sources include hamburger, lean beef, chicken, tuna, ham, shrimp, frankfurter, eggs and egg yolks, spinach, mustard greens, asparagus, baked potato with skin, navy beans, kidney beans, soybeans, split peas, dried apricots, raisins, prunes and prune juice, strawberries, and tomato juice.

instruction: I'm worried my toddler might be gaining too much weight. What do pediatricians do to monitor this?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Your pediatrician should monitor your child’s weight and rate of weight gain from infancy onward. The pediatrician compares your toddler’s weight to his length to check that the child is at a healthy weight for age, sex, and length, using weight-to-length ratios and growth charts endorsed by the AAP and the World Health Organization.

instruction: What weight-to-length percentiles define overweight and obese for infants and toddlers?
output: A weight-to-length ratio at or above the 85th percentile is considered overweight. A weight-to-length ratio at or above the 95th percentile defines the obese category.

instruction: Can infants be put on diets if they seem chubby?
output: Infants aren’t put on diets. Growth charts can provide confidence that an infant or toddler is growing well and so may not need extra formula or supplementation, which can be reassuring if you are concerned about fussiness due to not eating enough.

instruction: How many times might it take for my child to learn to like a new food?
output: Taste preferences can change over time, and it can take your child ten times of trying a new food before he enjoys it.

instruction: What snack options does the sample menu suggest?
output: Suggested snacks include one slice of toast or a whole-wheat muffin with 1–2 tablespoons of cream cheese or peanut butter, or 1/2 cup yogurt with cut-up fruit. Another snack option is 1–2 ounces of cubed or string cheese, or 2–3 tablespoons of fruit or berries, with water or 1/2 cup whole or 2 percent milk.

instruction: I'm worried my child watches a lot of TV; can that affect their weight?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Studies show that children who watch too much TV are more likely to have overweight. The text also suggests turning off the TV and having your child sit at the table during meals and enjoying meals together without TV or phone distractions.

instruction: What are the measurement conversions listed in the menu?
output: The conversions given are: 1 cup = 8 ounces = 240 ml; 1 ounce = 2 tablespoons = 30 ml; 1/2 ounce = 1 tablespoon = 15 ml = 3 teaspoons; 1 teaspoon = 1/3 tablespoon = 5 ml.

instruction: What does the dinner in the sample menu include for a one-year-old?
output: Dinner includes 2–3 ounces of cooked meat (ground or diced), 1/2 cup cooked yellow or orange vegetables, 1/2 cup whole-grain pasta, rice, or potato, and 1/2 cup whole or 2 percent milk.

instruction: My child is fussy and not eating much; can the growth charts tell me if they need extra formula?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Growth charts can provide more confidence that your infant or toddler is growing well and so may not need extra formula or supplementation; this can be helpful reassurance if you are concerned your child is fussy due to not eating enough.

instruction: How much peanut butter or cream cheese should I put on a slice of toast for a snack?
output: The menu suggests 1–2 tablespoons of cream cheese or peanut butter on a slice of toast or a whole-wheat muffin as a snack option.

instruction: Should I minimize juice for my infant to help prevent obesity?
output: The text recommends developing healthy eating habits that begin in infancy, such as minimizing or eliminating juice and offering a variety of healthy foods, especially vegetables and fruits.

instruction: When should I start thinking about toilet training for my child?
output: As your child approaches age two, you’ll begin to think about toilet training. The text notes that you might consider it if a childcare or preschool program requires toilet training.

instruction: My baby’s weight seems high — how will the doctor judge whether it’s a problem?
output: Your pediatrician will monitor your child’s weight and rate of weight gain from infancy onward. They compare your toddler’s weight to his length to ensure a healthy weight for age, sex, and length. A weight-to-length ratio at or above the 85th percentile is considered overweight, and a ratio at or above the 95th percentile is considered obese. These categories predict risk for current and future medical problems, with higher ratios above the 85th percentile indicating greater risk.

instruction: What do overweight and obese mean for a toddler?
output: Overweight is defined by a weight-to-length ratio at or above the 85th percentile, and obese is defined by a weight-to-length ratio at or above the 95th percentile. These categories predict risk for current and future medical problems, and the higher the weight-to-length ratio above the 85th percentile, the greater the risk.

instruction: I’m worried my fussy baby isn’t getting enough to eat — should I add extra formula or start a diet?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Infants aren’t put on diets. Growth charts endorsed for infants can reassure you that an infant or toddler is growing well and may not need extra formula or supplementation.

instruction: Our family tends to gain weight easily. Can we still help our toddler if genetics run in the family?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Some children are prone to extra weight gain because of family history, which includes genetics, metabolism, and family customs. In almost all cases, making healthy food changes and increasing physical activity can help improve your child’s weight.

instruction: How should we start healthy eating habits for our infant?
output: Talk to your pediatrician about developing healthy eating habits that begin in infancy. Suggestions include minimizing or eliminating juice, offering a variety of healthy foods especially vegetables and fruits, and continuing these habits throughout childhood. Early on, allow your child to decide when he is full.

instruction: My toddler refuses new foods — how many times should I offer them?
output: Taste preferences can change over time, and it can take your child about ten times of trying a new food before he enjoys it.

instruction: What are good snack choices for my toddler and how should we eat meals?
output: Choose nutritious snacks such as vegetables, fruits, low-fat dairy foods, and whole grains. Turn off the TV and have your child sit at the table during meals. Enjoy meals together as a family whenever possible, without distractions from phones or the television.

instruction: I’m worried my toddler watches a lot of TV — does TV increase the chance of overweight?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Studies show that children who watch too much TV are more likely to have overweight.

instruction: When should I think about toilet training my two-year-old?
output: As your child approaches age two you can begin to think about toilet training, and some childcare or preschool programs may require it. Training generally becomes easier and is accomplished faster when a child is older. Many children are ready after their second birthday (boys often slightly later than girls), though some children may be ready earlier. Early training is possible but not necessarily preferred because it may place unnecessary pressure and the child may not yet have bowel or bladder control or the motor skills to remove clothes quickly.

instruction: My toddler isn’t ready for potty training yet — what can I do to prepare him?
output: Keep his potty chair or a toilet seat insert handy and explain in simple terms how it works. Choose the words you’ll use at home, such as “pee-pee” and “poop,” to familiarize him with what’s taking place. Showing him where stool from diapers goes and allowing him to flush the toilet can help. These steps help him become familiar so the process will be less scary and confusing; being able to tell you after he has gone is a first step but not a sign he is completely ready.

instruction: He keeps falling asleep everywhere — how do we get a better sleep routine?
output: Don’t let your child fall asleep where he drops off, as that makes establishing a regular sleep schedule harder. Note when he shows signs of sleepiness and make that his regular bedtime. Establish a bedtime ritual (bath, story, or song) that ends with him quiet but awake in his crib, ready for your goodnight kiss before you leave the room. If he cries continuously, use methods for teaching him to fall asleep on his own. Using a transitional object (lovey) may be helpful at this age.

instruction: My toddler was sleeping through the night but now wakes up a lot — what might cause that and what should I do?
output: Changes in routine are a common cause of nighttime awakening: switching rooms or beds, losing a favorite cuddly toy or blanket, taking a trip, or having an illness. These are valid reasons for waking, but they are not reasons to pick him up or take him to your room. He needs to put himself back to sleep, even if it means crying a bit first. If he’s used to getting lots of nighttime attention, retrain gradually. If you’ve been giving milk when he wakes, change to water and then stop the practice. If you’ve been picking him up, restrict yourself to verbally calming him from a distance. Don’t get angry; show him kindness while remaining firm.

instruction: My toddler has big tantrums and I feel like a failure — what does this mean about his behavior?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. At this age your toddler has a limited idea of “good” and “bad” and doesn’t fully understand rules or warnings. He often acts on impulses, so tantrums and unexpected behavior are common and don’t mean you’ve failed as a parent.

instruction: How should I think about discipline with my toddler?
output: Discipline means to teach or instruct. While punishment may be part of discipline, a much more important aspect is love. Affection and caring form the core of your relationship and play a powerful role in shaping his behavior. Your daily example of honesty, dedication, and trust will teach him the same.

instruction: My toddler started waking up a lot after we switched rooms. Can I bring him into my bed to help him sleep?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Changing rooms can disrupt sleep, but moving him into your bed or taking him to your room is not recommended. He needs to put himself back to sleep, even if he cries a bit. Retrain gradually if he’s used to lots of nighttime attention; if you’ve been giving milk when he wakes, change to water and then stop that practice. If you’ve been picking him up, restrict yourself to verbally calming him from a distance. Stay kind but firm.

instruction: He cries when he wakes at night — should I pick him up or just let him cry?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Though his crying is understandable, pick-ups are not an appropriate response. He needs to learn to put himself back to sleep, and that may mean crying briefly. If you’ve been picking him up, limit yourself to verbal calming from a distance and retrain him gradually.

instruction: We’ve been giving milk at night when our toddler wakes. What should we do now?
output: If you’ve been giving milk when he wakes up, change that to water and soon afterward stop the practice entirely.

instruction: Is it okay to soothe him by talking to him from across the room when he wakes up?
output: Yes. If you’ve been picking him up, restrict yourself to verbally calming him from a distance rather than picking him up or taking him to your room.

instruction: My toddler keeps hitting and biting other kids. How should I respond?
output: It makes sense to feel worried about this. Be certain he understands that hitting, biting, and kicking are not OK. Alert him to your displeasure with your facial expression and tone of voice, then move him to a different place. Use firm but gentle guidance and, when appropriate, briefly isolate him with no attention, no toys, and no fun (time-out).

instruction: Is spanking an effective way to stop misbehavior?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Physical punishment is harmful and is the least effective way to discipline. It teaches that hitting and yelling are acceptable, can lead children to imitate hitting, can cause physical harm, makes the child angry and resentful, gives the child extreme attention, and is associated with later aggression, conduct problems, and depressive symptoms. Avoid physical or emotionally hurting punishments.

instruction: When my toddler breaks rules in public I get embarrassed and lose my temper. What should I do in the moment?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. When he violates rules, show your displeasure with your facial expression and tone of voice, then move him to a different place. Prepare your responses while you are calm so you’re less likely to lose your temper in the moment. Never resort to punishments that physically or emotionally hurt your child.

instruction: How often should I praise my toddler when he behaves well?
output: Displays of affection should greatly outnumber punishments and criticisms. Catch him being good as often as you can; praise and attention are powerful motivational rewards for obeying reasonable rules.

instruction: I’m a single parent and sometimes feel overwhelmed managing behavior. Where can I find support?
output: It makes sense to feel alone at times. Resources include family members, friends, faith communities, and parent support groups. Your pediatrician’s office can also help connect you with resources if you’re not sure where to start.

instruction: Should I make a long list of rules for my 18-month-old?
output: No. If you load too many rules on your child at this early age he’ll be hurt and bewildered and you’ll be frustrated. Establish priorities and build your list of rules gradually; the most important rules should keep him safe.

instruction: If I tell my toddler not to do something once, should I expect him to stop?
output: Telling him once won’t get the message across. He has to learn by trial and error, often several times, before he understands a rule.

instruction: Is yelling at my child a harmless way to discipline him sometimes?
output: Many parents worry about this. Yelling and other harsh verbal discipline are harmful — they cause aggression, conduct problems, and depressive symptoms later, and are emotionally harmful to both parent and child.

instruction: What exactly is a time-out for a toddler?
output: Time-out means isolating the child briefly with no attention, no toys, and no fun. It’s an approach to discipline that gives no attention for a short period.

instruction: How should I handle safety as my child learns to walk?
output: Discipline around safety should be paramount. Give him freedom to explore in safe ways and ensure childproof locks are in place where they’re needed. Prioritize rules that keep him safe.

instruction: Is it ever okay to spank or hit my toddler when I'm really mad?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Never resort to punishments that physically or emotionally hurt your child. Spanking, slapping, beating, shaking, and screaming at children of any age does far more harm than good. Physical punishment can harm your child, teaches that hitting and yelling are acceptable, makes the child angry and resentful, and is the least effective way to discipline.

instruction: What should I do if my toddler keeps opening the oven door after I told him not to?
output: Without raising your voice, firmly say, “No. Don’t open the oven door,” and pick him up with his back toward you. Begin time-out training in your lap with your child facing away from you and hold him until he is quiet and still.

instruction: Why shouldn't I yell or scream at my child when he misbehaves?
output: Yelling and other harsh verbal discipline cause aggression, conduct problems, and depressive symptoms in later years. Harsh discipline is emotionally harmful to both parent and child and is the least effective way to discipline.

instruction: How can I make time-out work instead of hitting or yelling?
output: The key is consistency and calmness. Respond immediately every time your child breaks an important rule, remain calm, and hold the child facing away until he is quiet and still. If you lose your temper, take a few deep breaths, count to ten, and if possible leave the room and get someone else to step in.

instruction: I'm worried I'll lose my temper and slap my toddler. Any quick strategies to avoid that?
output: Many parents wonder about this. When you feel yourself losing your temper, take a few deep breaths, count to ten, and, if possible, leave the room and get someone else to step in. Remind yourself you are older and should be wiser than your toddler.

instruction: My toddler screams “no” to everything. Should I punish him for saying no?
output: Don’t punish him for saying “no.” He may be asserting control and often says “no” automatically. Instead, repeat your request calmly and clearly and don’t overreact.

instruction: How can I prevent temper tantrums before they start?
output: You can plan in advance. Use a friendly tone and phrase requests like invitations, offer limited choices when possible, choose your battles carefully, and make sure everyone who cares for him follows the same policies consistently.

instruction: Is it okay to offer my toddler a cookie if he cooperates with bath time?
output: Don’t make deals or offer choices where none exists. Issues like bathing and bedtime are nonnegotiable, and bribery will teach him to break the rule whenever you forget the reward.

instruction: Why does physical punishment sometimes make misbehavior worse later on?
output: Physical punishment makes the child angry and resentful of the parent, so instead of developing self-discipline the child is more likely to try to get back at the parent by continuing to misbehave without getting caught.

instruction: What do you mean when you say toddlers imitate everything you do?
output: Toddlers will imitate everything that you do. If parents hit or yell, children often learn that violence is an acceptable way to express anger and to resolve conflicts and may become hitters themselves.

instruction: Should I always insist my child eats what I ask, even if he resists?
output: Choose your battles carefully. Don’t push unless there’s something worth pushing. Safety items like staying buckled in a car seat are priorities; things like making him eat peas before applesauce are not.

instruction: How should caregivers handle a toddler who uses tantrums to get attention?
output: Physical punishment gives a child an extreme form of attention; even unpleasant attention can tell the child he’s gotten through to a busy parent. Make sure caregivers follow consistent policies and use strategies like ignoring misbehavior and using time-out rather than giving attention for bad behavior.

instruction: If a little spank doesn’t stop bad behavior, will parents often do more?
output: Physical punishment can escalate: if a little spank doesn’t work, many parents will slap even harder as they become angrier and more frustrated.

instruction: How should I phrase requests to reduce power struggles with my toddler?
output: Use a friendly tone and phrase your request like an invitation instead of a command. It also helps to say “please” and “thank you.”

instruction: Can offering limited choices help when my toddler resists chores or routines?
output: Yes. Offering limited choices when possible—like which pajamas to wear or which story to read—encourages independence and makes him more likely to comply when it counts.

instruction: How can I reduce the number and intensity of my toddler's temper tantrums at home?
output: Plan prevention in advance and make sure everyone who cares for your child follows the same approach. Use a friendly tone and phrase requests like invitations, saying please and thank you. Don’t overreact when he says “no”; calmly repeat your request. Choose battles carefully and say no only when necessary (safety is a priority). Offer limited choices where possible but don’t offer choices where none exists. Anticipate likely triggers and avoid them when you can. Reward good behavior with praise and attention, and keep your sense of humor when appropriate.

instruction: What should I do when my toddler says "no" to everything I ask?
output: Don’t overreact — many toddlers say “no” to assert control. Repeat your request calmly and clearly and don’t punish him for saying “no.” Choose when to insist and say “no” only for truly necessary things like safety.

instruction: Which battles are worth fighting and which should I let go of?
output: Prioritize safety items — for example, keeping him buckled into his car safety seat is worth insisting on. Low-priority matters, like ordering peas before applesauce, are not worth pushing. Say no only when it’s absolutely necessary.

instruction: Can offering choices help prevent tantrums, and how should I do it?
output: Yes. Offer limited choices whenever possible, such as which pajamas to wear, which story to read, or which toy to play with. This encourages independence and makes him more likely to comply when it counts. Do not offer choices where none exists, and don’t make deals or bribe him.

instruction: My child always loses it in the grocery store — what can I do so we don’t have a scene?
output: If the grocery store reliably triggers tantrums, arrange for the child to stay with your spouse or partner or get a sitter for the next few trips. If that isn’t possible when you’re out, calmly carry him to a restroom or the car so he can finish his performance away from onlookers; sometimes a big, immobilizing hug and a calming voice will soothe him.

instruction: When should I call a time-out during a tantrum?
output: Think of tantrums as performances and eliminate the audience by leaving the room. If he follows you, call time-out and put him in his play yard. If he kicks or bites at any time during the tantrum, call time-out immediately.

instruction: After a tantrum or time-out is over, how should I act?
output: Don’t dwell on the tantrum. If your request triggered the outburst, calmly repeat it. Stay composed and determined so he learns that acting out is a waste of his and your time.

instruction: My toddler sometimes holds his breath during tantrums and once passed out briefly. Is that normal and what should I do?
output: It can happen that a child holds his breath during a severe temper tantrum and may faint for a very short period. He will awaken in thirty to sixty seconds. Keep him safe and protected during this brief episode and try not to overreact, because overreaction tends to reinforce tantrum breath-holding.

instruction: I get so embarrassed when my child screams in public — I’m worried I’ll lose control. What can I do without making things worse?
output: It makes sense to feel embarrassed. Rather than lashing out or giving in, calmly carry him to a restroom or the car so he can finish away from onlookers. If possible, use a big, immobilizing hug and a calming voice to soothe him. Avoid spanking, yelling, or snapping, because those approaches don’t work better than calm responses.

instruction: Is laughing at my toddler during a tantrum ever okay?
output: Don’t laugh at your toddler while he kicks and screams, because that plays to his performance. It’s fine to laugh and talk about it with friends or family when he’s out of earshot to relieve stress.

instruction: My child’s temperament seems very intense. Does that mean tantrums are my fault?
output: No. A child’s general temperament sets the tone for tantrums. Active, intense, and persistent children may channel that intensity into tantrums. This is neither good nor bad and has nothing to do with your parenting skills; it’s a normal stage that will pass.

instruction: Are there alternatives to spanking when I feel I’m about to lose control?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics strongly opposes striking a child; spanking is never recommended because infants and children may be physically harmed. Alternatives include putting your baby in the crib or another safe place while you get control of yourself, calling a friend, relative, or partner for support or advice, and if those don’t help, reaching out to your child’s pediatrician for advice.

instruction: If I spank my child impulsively, how should I handle it afterward?
output: If a spanking is spontaneous, explain calmly afterward why you did it, the specific behavior that provoked it, and how angry you felt. You might apologize for your loss of control; this helps the child understand and models how to remediate a wrong.

instruction: One of my child’s playmates always makes him irritable and leads to tantrums. What should I try?
output: Anticipate that playmate interactions may trigger tantrums and avoid them when possible. Separate the children for a few days or weeks and see if the dynamics improve later.

instruction: How should caregivers handle rules like bathing, bedtime, or crossing the street when a toddler protests?
output: Don’t offer choices where none exists and don’t make deals for nonnegotiable issues like bathing, bedtime, and staying out of the street. These are not subjects for bribery or rewards for compliance.

instruction: My toddler is having a tantrum in the supermarket and I feel trapped and embarrassed. What should I do right now?
output: It’s common to feel trapped and embarrassed. Calmly carry him to a restroom or to the car so he can finish away from onlookers. Avoid spanking, yelling, or letting him have his way, since those responses encourage tantrums and won’t work better. In some cases a big, immobilizing hug and a calming voice will soothe and quiet him.

instruction: During a severe tantrum my child held his breath and fainted for a moment. He woke up after about 45 seconds. What does that mean and what should I do?
output: That can be frightening. A child may hold his breath during a severe temper tantrum and sometimes faint for a very short period; he will awaken in thirty to sixty seconds. Keep him safe and protected during the brief episode and try not to overreact, because overreacting tends to reinforce tantrum breath‑holding.

instruction: Is spanking ever acceptable when my toddler is out of control in public?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics strongly opposes striking a child for any reason. Spanking is never recommended; infants and children may be physically harmed by a parent who strikes them, and whenever a parent strikes a child it may undermine the trust the child needs to thrive.

instruction: I sometimes lose control and spank my baby. What should I do afterward?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If a spanking is spontaneous, later explain calmly why you did it, the specific behavior that provoked it, and how angry you felt. You might apologize to your child for your loss of control; this usually helps the youngster understand and accept what happened and models how to remediate a wrong.

instruction: What are some alternatives to spanking when I feel frustrated with my infant or toddler?
output: First, put your baby in the crib or another safe place while you get control of yourself. Call a friend, relative, or partner to get support or advice. If these don’t help, reach out to your child’s pediatrician for advice.

instruction: After a timeout or tantrum is over, should I keep talking about it or move on?
output: When the tantrum or time‑out is over, don’t dwell on it. If a request from you triggered the outburst, calmly repeat that request, remain composed and determined, and your child will soon realize acting out is a waste of his and your time.

instruction: My toddler keeps invading my older child's toys and they get into fights. How can I keep the peace between them?
output: Your toddler is self‑centered and may deliberately invade siblings’ territory and possessions, which can lead to tantrums. Enforce rules that guard older children’s privacy and set aside time to spend just with the older children. All your children want your affection and attention.

instruction: Which vaccines does my toddler need between 12 and 15 months?
output: Between twelve and fifteen months your toddler will need booster doses of the Hib and pneumococcal conjugate vaccines; these help prevent meningitis, pneumonia, and joint infection caused by Haemophilus influenzae type b and several strains of Streptococcus pneumoniae. Your toddler also should receive his first measles, mumps, rubella (MMR) vaccine, chickenpox vaccine, and hepatitis A vaccine.

instruction: What additional immunizations are required between 12 and 18 months?
output: At twelve to eighteen months your toddler will require the fourth dose of DTaP (which can be given as early as twelve months but is recommended at fifteen to eighteen months), the third dose of polio vaccine if not already given, the fourth dose of pneumococcal vaccine, the fourth dose of Hib vaccine, and the second dose of hepatitis A vaccine given at least six months after the first dose.

instruction: What blood tests should be done at the twelve-month well-child exam?
output: At the twelve‑month well‑child exam your child should have a hemoglobin and a lead level drawn to assess for anemia and lead poisoning, respectively. Iron‑deficiency anemia can be common in infants and toddlers, and iron supplementation may be needed.

instruction: What are common sources of lead that toddlers might put in their mouths?
output: Toddlers may chew on paint chips, toys painted with lead paint, jewelry, and other objects. Homes being renovated may have lead dust that can be ingested, and soil—especially near roadways—may have lead from car exhaust from the days when gasoline contained lead.

instruction: My toddler can climb out of his crib. What sleep‑safety steps should I take?
output: Keep the crib mattress at the lowest setting and keep the crib free of any objects your toddler could stack and climb on to get out. If your toddler can climb out of his crib, move him to a low bed. Keep the crib away from all windows, drapery, and electrical and other cords, and be sure all crib gyms, mobiles, and other hanging toys have been removed.

instruction: Why can time‑out be an effective discipline for toddlers?
output: To a toddler there is no such thing as negative attention— all attention is good. At times toddlers would rather have you angry than feel ignored, which is why time‑out can be so effective.

instruction: What vaccines does my one-year-old need at the 12-month well-child visit?
output: At the 12-month visit your child should get: the fourth dose of DTaP (can be given as early as 12 months but is recommended at 15–18 months), the third dose of polio if not already given, the fourth dose of pneumococcal vaccine, the fourth dose of Hib vaccine, and the second dose of hepatitis A given at least six months after the first dose.

instruction: When should the fourth DTaP dose be given?
output: The fourth DTaP can be administered as early as twelve months, but it is recommended at fifteen to eighteen months of age.

instruction: Does my child need the second hepatitis A vaccine now?
output: The second dose of hepatitis A should be given at least six months after the first dose.

instruction: What blood tests are done at the one-year visit?
output: At the twelve-month well-child exam your child should have a hemoglobin and a lead level drawn to assess for anemia and lead poisoning.

instruction: I'm worried my toddler might be chewing things that have lead — what items commonly contain lead?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Items that may contain lead include paint chips, toys painted with lead paint, jewelry, and other objects. Homes being renovated can have lead dust that may be ingested, and soil near roadways can have lead from car exhaust from the days when gasoline contained lead.

instruction: How can I make my toddler's crib safer?
output: Keep the crib mattress at the lowest setting; keep the crib free of any objects your toddler could stack and climb on to get out; if your toddler can climb out of the crib, move him to a low bed; keep the crib away from windows, drapery, and electrical and other cords; and be sure all crib gyms, mobiles, and other hanging toys have been removed.

instruction: Can my toddler have toys that plug into the wall or run on batteries?
output: Do not give your toddler any toy that requires an electrical outlet. If the toy is battery operated, make sure the battery case is secured.

instruction: What should grandparents avoid giving my toddler as gifts?
output: Do not give a motorized riding toy and do not give toys with any small parts or sharp edges. Stick with toys intended for toddlers and check the recommended age label to be sure.

instruction: Is it ever OK to step away from the bathtub for a few seconds while my toddler is in it?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Never leave your toddler, even for a few seconds, in or near any body of water without supervision; this includes a bathtub, toilet, wading pool, swimming pool, fishpond, whirlpool, hot tub, lake, or ocean.

instruction: How should my one-year-old ride in the car?
output: All children this age should ride rear-facing in their car safety seat, appropriately installed in the backseat, with a five-point harness.

instruction: What are some activities grandparents can do to help my child's motor and cognitive development?
output: Help your grandchild practice skills that involve your own likes, such as involving him in physical activities like sweeping, preparing food, or arranging items while you lend a helping hand. Devise and initiate outdoor games and exercises. Read special books with him, play music and sing songs, assist as he begins to learn his numbers, and play hiding games like hide-and-seek and peekaboo. Mix fantasy play with real play.

instruction: My toddler is starting to climb out of the crib — what should I do next?
output: If your toddler can climb out of his crib, move him to a low bed. Also be sure the crib mattress is at the lowest setting and remove any objects he could stack and climb on.

instruction: Why is a lead level checked at 12 months?
output: A lead level is drawn to assess for lead poisoning because toddlers have high hand-to-mouth activity and may chew on objects that contain lead; common sources include paint chips, toys painted with lead paint, jewelry, and other objects. Renovation dust and soil near roadways can also be sources of lead.

instruction: What should I check when choosing toys for my one-year-old?
output: Choose toys intended for toddlers and check the recommended age label to be sure. Do not give toys with small parts or sharp edges, do not give a motorized riding toy, do not give toys that require an electrical outlet, and if a toy is battery operated make sure the battery case is secured.

instruction: Is it normal that my toddler seems selfish and doesn’t think about other children’s feelings?
output: Yes. Don’t overreact to this self-centered behavior; it typically tapers off by age three. Reinforce that he should be sensitive to other children’s feelings and nurture his self-esteem without putting others at a disadvantage.

instruction: My one-year-old has big mood swings, clinging and then pushing me away—should I be worried?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Don’t overreact to the mood swings—clinging one moment, independent the next, then defiant is common at this age.

instruction: What should I do if my toddler becomes aggressive toward others?
output: Do not reinforce aggressiveness if it becomes abusive. Set limits, but do not physically restrain or punish him.

instruction: Can I give my one-year-old a motorized riding toy for their birthday?
output: Do not give him a motorized riding toy.

instruction: How do I choose safe toys for a toddler?
output: Choose toys intended for toddlers, not for older children. Do not give toys with any small parts or sharp edges, and check the recommended age label to be sure.

instruction: Is it okay to step out of the bathroom for a few seconds while my toddler is in the bathtub?
output: It makes sense to worry about this. Never leave your toddler, even for a few seconds, in or near any body of water without supervision, including the bathtub.

instruction: How should my child ride in the car at this age?
output: All children this age should ride rear-facing in their car safety seat, appropriately installed in the backseat, with a five-point harness. Never let your toddler climb out of his car safety seat while the car is moving.

instruction: Is it okay to leave my toddler alone in the car for a minute while I run into the house?
output: It makes sense to feel worried about this. Never leave him alone in the car, even if it is locked and in your driveway.

instruction: Can I rely on child safety caps to keep my toddler from getting into medicine bottles?
output: Do not depend on child safety caps to protect your child from getting into medicine bottles. Keep all medications, including those you might carry in your purse, out of reach.

instruction: How can I prevent my toddler from falling out of a window?
output: Protect any open windows with window guards or barriers your toddler cannot possibly push out or open. Screens do not prevent falls from windows.

instruction: Do I need a fence around our pool, and what kind should it be?
output: Drowning is the number one cause of death in children ages one to four years. Pools should be made entirely inaccessible by a four-sided fence completely separating the pool from the house and the rest of the yard.

instruction: What should I do with guns in the home if I have a toddler?
output: The safest home for a child is one without a gun. If you do have a gun, store it unloaded in a locked case, with the ammunition locked in a separate location. Many states hold parents liable for accidental firearm injuries involving children.

instruction: Are window screens enough to stop my toddler from falling out?
output: No. Screens do not prevent falls from windows; protect open windows with guards or barriers your toddler cannot push out or open.

instruction: How closely should I supervise my toddler around family pets, especially dogs?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Always supervise toddlers around animals, especially dogs; even the friendliest dog has the potential to bite.

instruction: Should I lock my parked car so my toddler can’t get inside when I’m not using it?
output: Keep your vehicle locked when not in use so your child cannot get in. Even without starting the engine, he could set the car in neutral, allowing it to roll, or he could suffer heatstroke.

instruction: How can I prevent my child from getting to the pool when I'm not looking?
output: Install door locks, barriers, and alarms to prevent your child from accessing pools without your knowledge. Make the pool entirely inaccessible by using a four-sided fence that completely separates the pool from the house and the rest of the yard.

instruction: Is drowning really the number one cause of death for children ages one to four?
output: Yes. Drowning is the number one cause of death in children ages one to four years.

instruction: Should I hold my toddler whenever we're near a driveway or street?
output: Yes. Hold on to your toddler whenever you’re near streets, parking lots, and driveways, even in quiet neighborhoods.

instruction: What kind of surface should be under our backyard playset?
output: Make sure there is sand, wood chips, or another soft surface under outdoor play equipment.

instruction: I'm worried my child might get into the car and hurt themselves — could they make the car roll or suffer heatstroke if they get inside?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Even without starting the engine, your child could set the car in neutral, allowing it to roll, or he could suffer heatstroke.

instruction: How should I secure my vehicle when it's not being used?
output: Keep your vehicle locked when not in use so your child cannot get in.

instruction: Does a pool fence need to separate the pool from the house, or is one side enough?
output: The pool should be made entirely inaccessible by a four-sided fence completely separating the pool from the house and the rest of the yard.

instruction: What should I watch for when I'm backing the car out of the garage?
output: It's common to have questions like this. Pay special attention anytime you or someone else is backing out of the garage or down the driveway. Make sure you know where your child is and that he cannot run into the path of the vehicle.

instruction: Should I set up fences around our yard to keep my toddler safe?
output: Yes. Set up fences or other barriers to make sure he stays within his outside play area and away from the street, pools, and other hazards.

instruction: Are door locks and alarms useful for keeping a toddler away from streets and driveways?
output: Yes. Install door locks, barriers, and alarms to prevent your child from accessing pools, driveways, and streets without your knowledge.

instruction: If my toddler gets into a parked car, what could happen to them?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Even without starting the engine, he could set the car in neutral, allowing it to roll, or he could suffer heatstroke.

instruction: Do I need to keep holding my toddler near streets even if our neighborhood is very quiet?
output: Yes. Hold on to your toddler whenever you’re near streets, parking lots, and driveways, even in quiet neighborhoods.

instruction: Where should outdoor play equipment be placed and what should be under it?
output: Make sure there is sand, wood chips, or another soft surface under outdoor play equipment. Also set up fences or other barriers so he stays within his outside play area and away from streets, pools, and other hazards.

instruction: Can fencing and barriers keep my child away from hazards like pools and streets?
output: Yes. Set up fences or other barriers to make sure he stays within his outside play area and away from the street, pools, and other hazards.

instruction: How many hours of sleep does a 5-month-old generally need in 24 hours?
output: Infants 4 to 12 months generally need 12 to 16 hours of sleep per 24 hours, including naps.

instruction: When will my baby sleep through the night? I'm worried he never will.
output: It varies from baby to baby; some develop regular sleep rhythms in the first 6 to 8 weeks while others remain unpredictable for many months or longer. In most cases babies learn to stretch out nighttime feeds over the first few months, and by 4 to 6 months they will likely no longer need a feeding in the middle of the night. Many parents worry about this.

instruction: What can I do to help my infant stop needing night feedings?
output: A consistent bedtime routine can help: bath, breast or bottle, book, and bed (placed on his back to sleep). Also allow him to soothe himself to sleep at night, keep the room dark and quiet, make sure he gets plenty of time outdoors (weather permitting), and that he feeds well during the day.

instruction: I kept my 4-month-old up late so his dad could play with him and now he's overtired and inconsolable. Is keeping him up for family time a problem?
output: Yes, for a typical 4- to 8-month-old a healthy nightly bedtime is between 6 and 8 p.m. Keeping the child up longer to greet a parent can leave him overtired and out of sync with his biological rhythms. When he feels the need to sleep, he should be allowed to do so; artificial disruption of his schedule can make him moody and less attentive.

instruction: What's a healthy bedtime for a 6-month-old?
output: For a typical 4- to 8-month-old, a healthy nightly bedtime is between 6 and 8 p.m.

instruction: Does my baby's genetics affect how he sleeps?
output: Yes. An infant’s unique genetics can influence sleep, for example whether he takes long naps or short ones.

instruction: If I put my baby to bed earlier, will that change how much night sleep he gets?
output: If he goes to bed earlier, he may get up a little earlier, but his total night sleep will be longer.

instruction: Will my child cope if we change the bedtime routine for a night, like sleeping at another house?
output: If you have a good sleep routine, your child is more likely to adjust on evenings where the routine changes, such as sleeping at a different house or being woken early.

instruction: How much does our family schedule affect when and how well our baby sleeps?
output: Family circumstances and parents’ busy schedules can affect when, how long, and how well a child sleeps; parents can sometimes unknowingly disrupt their child’s sleep by their schedules and decisions.

instruction: What exactly is the bedtime routine you recommend?
output: A consistent bedtime routine described here is: bath, breast or bottle, book, and bed; place the baby on his back to sleep.

instruction: My baby becomes cranky and can't be consoled in the evening—could he be overtired from our schedule?
output: Yes. Keeping a baby up longer than his evolving biological rhythm can make him overtired and difficult to console. When a sleep-wake schedule is artificially disrupted, the child will probably become moody and less attentive. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: How much sleep does a 2-year-old need in 24 hours including naps?
output: Children ages 1 to 2 years generally need 11 to 14 hours of sleep per 24 hours, including naps.

instruction: Do naps count toward the recommended total sleep hours?
output: Yes. The recommended amounts of sleep are given per 24 hours and explicitly include naps.

instruction: Is it okay to let my baby soothe himself to sleep at night?
output: Yes. Allowing him to soothe himself to sleep at night is listed as a helpful approach to promote longer nighttime sleep.

instruction: My newborn slept fine the first week and then his sleep became erratic—should I be worried?
output: There are normal variations in sleep patterns. Some babies sleep with regular rhythms early on while others have unpredictable sleep behaviors for many months or longer. Many parents have this concern.

instruction: Is it okay to keep my 4-month-old up until 8:30 p.m. so his father can play with him, even though he gets overtired and cries before then?
output: It’s understandable to feel unsure about this. For a typical four- to eight-month-old, a healthy nightly bedtime is between 6 and 8 p.m. Keeping him up later can leave him overtired and out of sync with his biological rhythms. If he goes to bed earlier he may get up a little earlier but his total night sleep will be longer, which can give more time for a working parent in the morning.

instruction: Which matters more for my baby: when he sleeps or how long he sleeps?
output: Timing is probably more important than how long he sleeps. The quality of his sleep, which restores alertness and helps maintain an even temperament, depends largely on when sleep occurs. A consistent bedtime in sync with his biological clock typically results in restful, prolonged sleep.

instruction: My baby has drowsy times during the day. Should I let him nap then or try to keep him awake?
output: You should encourage sleep during his drowsy periods. If he sleeps during those times the quality of that sleep will be better than sleep that occurs out of phase with his biological cycles. Waiting until after drowsiness can make him overtired and make it harder for him to fall asleep.

instruction: What is a healthy bedtime for a four- to eight-month-old baby?
output: A healthy nightly bedtime for a typical four- to eight-month-old is between 6 and 8 p.m.

instruction: My one-month-old cries a lot when put in his crib at night. Should I let him cry himself to sleep or comfort him?
output: Many parents wonder about this. During the first month, try to avoid letting him cry. Respond to his tears and soothe him by singing or talking quietly, playing soft music, keeping the lights dim, and/or rocking him gently. Pick him up if necessary and place him back in the crib five to ten minutes later.

instruction: How long can a newborn stay awake before needing to sleep?
output: In general, after being awake for one to two hours a first-month infant needs sleep. Sometimes he may need to fall asleep even before an hour goes by, and rarely he may stay awake for three hours.

instruction: Should I put my baby into the crib fully asleep or when he’s drowsy but still awake?
output: Place him in his crib when he’s drowsy but still awake, especially for daytime napping. This approach can help him fall asleep more easily; if you wait too long he’s likely to become cranky and have more difficulty falling asleep.

instruction: Our toddler is whiny and irritable every evening. Could this be because he’s not getting enough sleep?
output: It makes sense to feel concerned. Signs that an older child may not be getting enough quality sleep include being whiny, crabby, excitable, wired, and irritable. An earlier bedtime can eliminate unpleasant behavior near the end of the day; if an earlier bedtime shows no improvement it may need to be moved even earlier.

instruction: What happens if I keep my baby up past his drowsy time because I want family time in the evening?
output: Keeping a baby up past his drowsy times can make him overtired, moody, and less attentive when he is awake. If his sleep-wake schedule is artificially disrupted, he will probably become moody and less attentive, and it may be harder for him to fall asleep.

instruction: If I move my baby’s bedtime earlier, will he wake up earlier in the morning?
output: If he goes to bed earlier he may get up a little earlier, but his total night sleep will be longer. That longer total sleep can allow more time for a working parent to spend with the child in the morning.

instruction: How should parents change their schedules if they need to make time for the baby when he’s awake and alert?
output: If adjustments in schedules are needed, they should begin with both parents, who should find a way to be available to their child when he’s awake and alert.

instruction: What specific ways can I soothe a crying newborn at night?
output: You can soothe a crying newborn by singing quietly, talking to him quietly, playing soft music, keeping the lights dim, and/or rocking him gently. Pick him up if necessary and place him back in the crib five to ten minutes later.

instruction: My newborn cries every time I lay him in the crib. Should I let him cry himself to sleep?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. During the first month, try to avoid letting him cry: respond and soothe him by singing or talking quietly, playing soft music, keeping lights dim, and/or rocking him gently. Pick him up if necessary and place him back in the crib five to ten minutes later; minimize his discomfort to maximize his sleep time and quality.

instruction: How long can a newborn usually stay awake before needing sleep?
output: After he has been awake for one to two hours, he generally needs sleep. Sometimes he may need to fall asleep even before an hour has passed, and rarely he may stay awake for three hours.

instruction: What are simple ways I can soothe my baby when he’s crying at bedtime?
output: Try singing or talking quietly, playing soft music, keeping the lights dim, rocking him gently, and picking him up if needed, then placing him back in the crib five to ten minutes later.

instruction: Should only one parent do the bedtime routine or should we get others involved?
output: Get other adults involved in bedtime rituals (spouse/partner, grandparent, sitter, or nanny). The more people who participate, the less likely your baby will associate falling asleep with just one person; this idea is sometimes called “many hands.”

instruction: My six-week-old seems ready for bed much earlier than before. Is that normal and how long will he sleep?
output: Yes. At about six weeks his sleep-wake schedule often starts to settle and he may need sleep earlier, perhaps between 6 and 8 p.m. His longest sleep period at that age is typically in the late evening and may last three to five hours.

instruction: If my baby wakes too early in the night, should I pick him up or feed him to get him back to sleep?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If he awakens too early, you can try encouraging him to go back to sleep by soothing him and keeping the lights off and shades drawn. Avoid picking him up or feeding him if possible.

instruction: My older baby takes long late-afternoon naps and then won’t sleep well at night. What should I do?
output: Many parents wonder about this. From four months on, most infants need at least two naps (about 9 a.m. and 1 p.m.) and possibly a late afternoon nap. If late and lengthy naps interfere with nighttime sleep, you might consider adjusting the afternoon nap; you can talk to your pediatrician about awakening him from his afternoon nap a little earlier than he would wake on his own.

instruction: By three months how many hours will my baby sleep at night without waking parents?
output: By about three months of age, most babies sleep six to eight hours during the night without waking their parents up.

instruction: My eight-month-old cries at the sight of his bed. What could be causing this and what can I do?
output: It’s common to feel unsure about this. If a child of this age seems overtired and cries at the sight of his bed, possible reasons include naps that are too short (less than thirty minutes), naps not occurring with his sleep rhythms, or a bedtime that is too late. You can place him in bed much earlier temporarily (perhaps around 5:30 or 6 p.m.). If he cries, check on him and console him with a few comforting words, change his diaper if needed, make sure he is comfortable, keep the lights dim, don’t pick him up and walk with him, and then leave the room quietly. Gradually give him less attention at night so he can learn self-soothing techniques.

instruction: How should my partner and I agree on a sleep plan for our baby?
output: Both you and your spouse/partner should agree on a sleep plan. Decide together whether to adopt new steps gradually or quickly. Many pediatricians advise small, simple steps first. Whenever you make a change, wait several days to see how successful it is rather than evaluating night by night.

instruction: I’m a primary caregiver and totally sleep deprived. What should we do so I can rest?
output: It makes sense to feel exhausted. Support each other and provide the primary caregiver with additional periods when she can take a break to catch a nap or otherwise recharge her batteries.

instruction: When I put my baby down for a daytime nap, should he be fully asleep or drowsy?
output: Place him in his crib when he’s drowsy but still awake; this approach will be particularly helpful for daytime napping. If you wait too long he is likely to become cranky and have more difficulty falling asleep.

instruction: If my six-week-old fusses a little when I put him to bed, will that harm him?
output: If he fusses a little, it won’t cause any harm. To minimize crying, put him to sleep earlier if needed and spend some time soothing him; he may drift off to a short nap or a longer sleep period.

instruction: How do I help my baby learn to soothe himself so there’s less crying at night?
output: As you and your baby get in tune with his rhythms, he will gradually learn to soothe himself when you put him down; as that happens there will be little or no crying. At night, gradually give him less attention so he stops anticipating that you’ll always respond when he cries and is more likely to use self-soothing behaviors such as sucking on his hand, rocking his head, or rubbing the sheet. If he cries when first put to bed, check briefly, console with a few words, change his diaper if needed, keep lights dim, and then leave quietly.

instruction: My 2-month-old still wakes a lot at night. When do babies usually start sleeping longer stretches at night?
output: Many parents wonder about this. By about three months of age, most babies sleep six to eight hours during the night without waking their parents up.

instruction: What should I do if my baby wakes up too early in the morning?
output: Sooth him and keep the lights off and the shades drawn. Avoid picking him up or feeding him, if possible, to encourage him to go back to sleep.

instruction: How many naps does a baby between four and twelve months usually need, and when should they be?
output: From four months through the rest of the first year, most infants need at least two naps: one midmorning and one midday; some children may nap a third time later in the afternoon. A common schedule is about 9 a.m., then 1 p.m., and a late-afternoon nap if needed.

instruction: My five-month-old only naps 35–40 minutes and then wakes up crying. What can we try to extend her naps?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A pediatrician might suggest responding promptly when she first makes noises or calls out, gently patting her or giving a short massage so she may fall back asleep for another 20 to 30 minutes. Also check that the room is dark and quiet and that nap timing is in sync with her natural rhythms, and be patient as rhythms can take a month or two to settle.

instruction: When should I stop giving my baby a late-afternoon nap?
output: By about nine months of age, try to dispense with late afternoon naps so he’ll be ready for bedtime earlier in the evening.

instruction: My baby cries at the sight of his bed and seems overtired. What might be causing this and what can I do?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Possible causes include naps that are too short (less than thirty minutes), naps not occurring with his sleep rhythms, or putting him to bed too late. Temporarily place him in bed earlier (perhaps 5:30 or 6 p.m.). If he cries, check on him, console with a few comforting words, change his diaper if needed, keep the lights dim, avoid picking him up and walking with him, then leave the room quietly.

instruction: Is it okay to let my baby cry himself to sleep at night?
output: Many parents wonder about this. There are times when you may need to let your baby cry himself to sleep; according to guidance, it won’t cause harm and it can help him learn to soothe himself. However, if the crying is prolonged, do check on your child — the goal is to teach him to fall asleep on his own, not to make him more upset.

instruction: How long should a baby’s nighttime sleep be by eight months?
output: By about eight months old, nighttime sleep should last from ten to twelve hours without him awakening for a nighttime feeding.

instruction: How can I make a bedtime routine actually help my baby fall asleep?
output: Begin routines early, before the child is already overtired and crabby. Be consistent using the same bedtime routines day after day, and be persistent — changes don’t happen overnight and routines need time to have a positive effect.

instruction: Sometimes my baby has a bowel movement shortly after I put her in the crib and then cries. How can I handle that to get a good nap?
