output: Prepared formula has vitamin D added; if your baby drinks about 32 ounces per day, he will get about the same amount of vitamin D as a 400 IU (10 mcg) supplement per day.

instruction: How much vitamin D would I need to take for my breast milk to give my baby 400 IU per day?
output: For a baby to receive 400 IU (10 mcg) of vitamin D through breast milk, a mother would need to take 6,400 IU (160 mcg) of vitamin D per day.

instruction: I'm vegan and breastfeeding — should I worry about my baby's vitamin needs?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A vegan diet lacks vitamin D and vitamin B12; a B12 deficiency in babies can lead to anemia and nervous system abnormalities, so discuss your nutritional needs with your pediatrician.

instruction: When should I start breastfeeding after delivery for the best chance of success?
output: Breastfeeding is generally most successful when started immediately after delivery, in the first hour. Keeping the baby with you (rooming in) and responding to hunger cues (demand feeding) also supports breastfeeding.

instruction: How often do breastfed newborns usually eat?
output: Breastfed newborns generally eat more frequently than formula-fed infants, typically eight to twelve (or more) times in twenty-four hours; tally feeds over the day to make sure there are at least eight feeds per 24-hour period.

instruction: Should I expect my breastfed baby to sleep through the night in the first months?
output: Breastfeeding babies will typically need to feed at night and not sleep through the night; rooming in at night for at least the first six months facilitates cue-based feeding and promotes safe sleep to decrease the risk of SIDS.

instruction: How long does a baby usually nurse on the first breast?
output: A baby will generally feed for about ten to fifteen minutes on the first breast; allow your baby to nurse on the first breast as long as desired and burp him when he stops for a prolonged period or withdraws.

instruction: What are common hunger cues for a breastfed baby?
output: Hunger cues include waking and looking alert, putting hands toward the mouth, making sucking motions, whimpering, flexing arms and hands, becoming more active, and nuzzling against your breast; crying is a late sign of hunger.

instruction: What is engorgement and how long does it last?
output: Engorgement lasts only a few days while lactation is getting established; breasts can still become firm or overfilled any time feedings are skipped and the breast is not emptied.

instruction: How much milk do newborns take in the first days and by one month?
output: A baby may take as little as 1 teaspoon (5 ml) at each feeding the first couple of days, up to 1 ounce (30 ml) by the fourth or fifth day, 2 to 6 ounces (60–180 ml) per feeding by the end of the week, and an average of 24 ounces (720 ml) per day by the end of the first month.

instruction: Is it possible to breastfeed twins at the same time, or do I have to feed them one at a time?
output: At first it may be easier to feed twins one at a time, but after lactation is established, feeding them simultaneously is often more convenient, can save time, and can increase milk supply; you can use the football hold or cradle them with their bodies crossing.

instruction: My baby was born premature — might he need vitamin or iron supplements?
output: Some babies born prematurely or with certain medical problems may need vitamin or iron supplements; discuss the need for vitamins or minerals with your doctor.

instruction: How often should I expect my breastfed newborn to feed in a day?
output: Breastfed newborns usually eat more often than formula-fed babies — generally eight to twelve (or more) times in a 24-hour period. It’s best to tally feeds over the day to make sure there are at least eight feeds in each 24-hour period.

instruction: What signs show my baby is hungry so I can feed before he cries?
output: Watch for early hunger signals: waking and looking alert, putting hands toward the mouth, making sucking motions, whimpering, flexing arms and hands, becoming more active, and nuzzling against your breast (babies can smell the breast even through clothing). It’s best to nurse when you see these cues rather than waiting for crying, which is a late sign of hunger.

instruction: Is it okay to wait until my baby cries to start nursing?
output: No. Crying is a late sign of hunger. It’s best to start nursing at the earliest hunger cues — before the baby starts crying — so he’s truly hungry and stimulates the breast more efficiently.

instruction: When should breastfeeding be started after delivery for best success?
output: Breastfeeding is generally most successful when started immediately after delivery, ideally in the first hour. Keeping the baby with you (rooming in) and responding promptly to hunger cues — demand feeding — also supports success.

instruction: My baby feeds a lot at night. Should he sleep in a different room so I can get rest?
output: Breastfeeding babies typically need to feed at night and often won’t sleep through the night. Keeping the baby in the same room with you at night for at least the first six months helps cue-based feeding and also promotes safe sleep to decrease the risk of sudden infant death syndrome.

instruction: How long will my baby usually nurse on one breast before switching?
output: Your baby will generally feed about ten to fifteen minutes on the first breast. Allow him to nurse on that breast as long as he wants; when he stops for a prolonged period or withdraws, burp him and then offer the other breast.

instruction: My baby seems sleepy after nursing on the first side. Should I switch him to the other side right away?
output: If he seems sleepy after the first breast, you may want to wake him by changing his diaper or playing with him a little before offering the second side.

instruction: How should I decide which breast to offer first at each feeding?
output: Since your baby suckles more efficiently on the breast he uses first, alternate which breast you offer first from feeding to feeding. You can mark the side last nursed with a safety pin or start on the breast that feels fuller.

instruction: My baby flails and screams at the breast, grabbing and losing it over and over. What should I do?
output: Many parents wonder about this. This describes an "excited ineffective" pattern. Feed him as soon as he wakes, before he becomes desperately hungry, and calm him during the feeding. If milk sprays from the breast as he struggles, manually express a few drops first to slow the stream.

instruction: My newborn hardly wants to nurse until my milk “comes in.” Can I give a bottle of water or formula to help?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Babies described as "procrastinators" often don’t nurse much until the milk supply increases. You should not give bottles of water or formula, because that may make it harder for them to nurse at the breast. Continue to put the baby to the breast regularly whenever he appears alert or makes mouthing movements.

instruction: My baby resists latching for the first few days. Are there things I can try at home?
output: Try placing the baby naked on your reclining bare abdomen and chest for a while; he may spontaneously move toward the breast or you can place him on the breast after a time. You may also find advice on positioning and attachment from a lactation specialist helpful. For a baby who resists nursing for the first few days, using an electric pump between feedings can help stimulate milk production. Contact your pediatrician’s office for assistance or referral to a lactation specialist if needed.

instruction: My baby likes to play with the nipple before nursing and smacks his lips. Is that a problem?
output: Some babies called "gourmets" or "mouthers" taste and play with the nipple before nursing. The best approach is patience — after a few minutes they usually settle down and nurse well. Make sure the lips and gums are on the areola and not on the nipple.

instruction: My baby nurses a bit, rests, then nurses again and sometimes falls asleep on the breast. What should I expect?
output: This is a common pattern called a "rester." Resters prefer to nurse for a few minutes, rest, and then resume nursing; some fall asleep on the breast, nap, then awaken ready to continue. These feedings can take longer, so it’s best to schedule extra time and be flexible.

instruction: How can I handle nighttime feedings so the baby resettles easily afterward?
output: Keep the room dark, warm, and quiet for nighttime feedings and avoid using a bright light. If the diaper is soiled or wet, change it quickly and without fanfare before feeding and put the baby right back to sleep afterward.

instruction: When can I expect my breastfed baby to sleep longer stretches at night?
output: Long stretches of uninterrupted sleep are not typical until about four months of age and generally when the baby is over twelve pounds. By six to eight weeks many babies have one sleep period of four to five hours; by four months many—but not all—babies may sleep six hours or more at night without awakening, though some breastfed babies continue to awaken more frequently for night feedings.

instruction: My newborn grasps the areola and sucks very energetically for 10–20 minutes, then seems less eager. Is that a normal feeding pattern?
output: Some babies grasp the areola and suck energetically for ten to twenty minutes and then become less eager as time goes on. This is a common feeding pattern.

instruction: What should I do when my baby gets frantic at the breast, grabs it, loses it, and screams in frustration?
output: It helps to calm the baby several times during feedings and to offer the breast as soon as he wakes up, before he becomes desperately hungry. If milk sprays from the breast while he struggles, manually expressing a few drops first may slow the stream and help. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: My baby won’t nurse until my milk “comes in.” Can I give bottles of water or formula to get him to take something?
output: Babies who won’t nurse until the milk supply increases should not be given bottles of water or formula, because feeding them bottles may make it more difficult to get them to nurse at the breast. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: How can I help a reluctant nurser who resists nursing for the first few days?
output: You can try placing the baby naked on the reclining mother’s bare abdomen and chest for a period of time; the baby may spontaneously move toward the breast or can be placed on the breast after a while. Advice on improved positioning and attachment from a lactation specialist may be helpful. For a baby who resists nursing for the first few days, you can use an electric pump between feedings to stimulate milk production, and contact your pediatrician’s office for assistance or referral to a lactation specialist. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: My baby plays with the nipple and tastes milk before really nursing. What should I do?
output: Some babies play with the nipple, taste the milk first, and smack their lips before nursing. The best solution is tolerance: after a few minutes of playing they often settle down and nurse well. Be sure the lips and gums are on the areola and not on the nipple.

instruction: My infant nurses a bit, rests, then nurses again or falls asleep on the breast. Is that okay?
output: Yes. Some babies prefer to nurse for a few minutes, rest, and then resume nursing; others fall asleep on the breast, nap, and then awaken ready to nurse again. These babies cannot be hurried, so it’s best to schedule extra time for feedings and remain flexible.

instruction: How will I know if my baby is eating enough during the first week after birth?
output: In the first week, doctors check that weight loss is not excessive (more than 10 percent of birth weight), there is no excessive jaundice, and the baby is producing at least a few wet diapers and stools per day. Wet diapers and stooling become reliable signs of feeding two days after birth. By the third day there should be four to eight wet diapers and three to four loose, seedy stools per day. You should schedule a follow-up visit with your pediatrician within forty-eight hours of going home. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: When should I schedule a follow-up visit after we go home from the hospital?
output: You should schedule a follow-up visit with your pediatrician within forty-eight hours of going home to help ensure your milk supply is increasing and your baby is getting milk from breastfeeding.

instruction: How many wet diapers and bowel movements should my baby have during the first month?
output: Once your milk supply increases, an adequate diet should produce six or more wet diapers a day and generally three to four or more bowel movements per day during the first month. Later, bowel movements may be less frequent and there may be a day or more between them; if the stools are soft and the baby is otherwise thriving, this is typical.

instruction: My baby is very sleepy and has been feeding less than eight times in 24 hours. Should I be worried?
output: A baby who is excessively sleepy or feeds less frequently than eight times in a twenty-four-hour period should be seen by the pediatrician to make sure he is gaining weight as expected. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: How much weight should my baby gain once my milk supply is established?
output: Once your milk supply is established, your baby should gain between ½ and 1 ounce (14–28 g) a day during the first three months. Between three and six months weight gain will taper to about ½ ounce (14 g) a day, and after six months daily gains will be even less.

instruction: If I need to be away from my newborn in the hospital, what should I do about feeding and milk production?
output: If circumstances keep you away from the baby, you will need to express breast milk manually or mechanically to stimulate continued milk production. Hospital staff will work with you to express milk in a way that enables transitioning back to the breast, including feeding techniques that avoid bottles and teats such as syringe or cup feeding, paced bottle-feeding, or feeding through a supplemented nursing system. Always check with your pediatrician or other expert before you stop giving your breast milk to your baby or begin supplementing with formula.

instruction: Is it better for my baby to room in with me in the hospital or sleep in the nursery for one night so I can rest?
Can I ask the nursery to take my baby one night so I can get an uninterrupted sleep in the hospital?
output: Most hospitals now provide mother–infant care so you can sleep in the same room while a nurse cares for your newborn. Research shows mothers often sleep better when their baby is in the same room rather than a nursery. If your baby is in the room, follow safe sleep guidelines: place the newborn on her back in a bassinet, not in your bed.

instruction: What are ways the hospital can feed my baby without giving a bottle or a regular teat?
output: Hospital staff may use feeding techniques that avoid bottles and teats, such as syringe or cup feeding, paced bottle-feeding, or feeding through a supplemented nursing system. They will work with you to express milk in a way that enables transitioning back to the breast.

instruction: When is it okay to start giving my baby expressed breast milk in a bottle?
output: Once your milk supply is established, usually three or four weeks after delivery, you may decide to offer expressed milk by bottle so you can be away for some feedings. An occasional bottle at this stage probably won’t interfere with nursing habits, but it can contribute to engorgement if you do not express milk in a timely way.

instruction: How should I store milk I express so caregivers can use it while I am away?
output: Transfer expressed milk into a clean bottle, a rigid plastic container, or a specially made plastic storage bag for the freezer so caregivers can use it while you are away. Store enough so you can replace the breast milk used by other caregivers.

instruction: I'm worried that using bottles will make my breasts get painful and swollen — what can cause engorgement and how do I prevent it?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Engorgement can happen if you don’t breastfeed or express your milk regularly throughout the day. Milk stasis from not expressing can lead to decreased milk production or clogged ducts. To avoid engorgement, either breastfeed or express milk regularly and express milk in a timely way when you are away.

instruction: What is the basic method for hand-expressing breast milk?
output: Wash your hands and use a clean container. Place your thumb above the areola and your fingers underneath. Gently but firmly roll the thumb and fingers toward each other while compressing the breast tissue and pushing toward the chest wall. Do not slide your fingers toward the nipple. Transfer the milk to a clean bottle, rigid plastic container, or a special freezer storage bag.

instruction: Do I need a let-down reflex for milk to come out when I express?
output: Yes. You must have the let-down reflex for milk to flow when expressing, whether by hand or with a pump.

instruction: Are electric breast pumps better than manual pumps?
output: Good-quality electric pumps can stimulate the breast more effectively than manual expression; they have regulated pressures and are self-cycling for efficient milk removal. Electric pumps are used primarily to induce or maintain lactation when a mother is unable to breastfeed directly for several days or more, such as with a hospitalized infant or when returning to work or school. They can be costly but can sometimes be rented or obtained through insurance or WIC.

instruction: My baby is in the hospital — can the hospital help me collect and store milk?
output: Yes. If your baby is hospitalized, the hospital may give specific instructions about milk collection and storage and may lend you a hospital-grade breast pump. Staff will work with you to express milk in ways that support returning to the breast.

instruction: How can I avoid causing nipple soreness when hand-expressing?
output: Do not slide your fingers toward your nipple, as that can cause soreness. Instead, place thumb above the areola and fingers underneath, then gently but firmly roll them toward each other while compressing the breast tissue and pushing toward the chest wall.

instruction: Are nursing pads useful for new mothers who are leaking milk?
output: Yes. Wearing nursing pads will help manage leakage. Some women wear nursing pads constantly during the first month or two to avoid milk stains.

instruction: Should I check with anyone before I stop giving breast milk or start formula?
output: Always check with your pediatrician or another expert before you stop giving your breast milk to your baby or begin supplementing with formula.

instruction: If I plan to be away from my baby, should I express milk while I'm gone?
output: Yes. If circumstances keep you away from your baby, you should express breast milk manually or with a pump to stimulate continued milk production. Expressing milk in advance and storing it allows your baby to receive your milk from caregivers while you are away and helps maintain your full milk production.

instruction: What are the risks of using a poor-quality breast pump?
output: A poor-quality breast pump may not remove milk effectively, which can result in engorgement or a gradually lower milk supply over time. Poor-quality pumps also may irritate the nipples or be painful.

instruction: Is there another simple hand technique for expressing milk you can describe?
output: Hold the breast with your thumb and index finger at the edge of the areola on opposite sides, then press toward the chest wall with a rhythmic motion. Rotate the position of your fingers so all parts of the breast are emptied. It helps to massage the breast gently first to stimulate expression.

instruction: Can I rent an electric breast pump if I only need one temporarily?
output: Yes. You can rent a pump from a medical supply store, a hospital, or a lactation rental agency. Electric pumps are efficient but can be costly to buy outright.

instruction: How can I use hand expression to get colostrum or milk out?
output: Hold the breast with your thumb and index finger at the edge of the areola on opposite sides, press toward the chest wall with a rhythmic motion, and rotate the finger position so all parts of the breast are emptied. It’s easier if you first stimulate the breast by massaging it gently.

instruction: Where can I get a basic hand pump?
output: Hand pumps are available at most drugstores and baby stores.

instruction: What should I look for when buying an electric breast pump?
output: Choose a pump that creates a steady milking action with variable pressure rather than simple suction. Consider a double electric pump if you want to express both breasts at once to increase milk volume and save time. Also make sure all parts that touch skin or milk can be removed for proper cleaning.

instruction: Can my insurance or WIC help me get a good pump?
output: Yes. In many states you can obtain a good-quality pump through your insurance or your state’s WIC program. You can upgrade an insurance-supplied pump by asking your obstetrician or pediatrician for a prescription and talking to your insurance provider about where to purchase a covered pump.

instruction: Do I need to sterilize pump parts and containers for a healthy baby?
output: No. Sterilization is not required for pumps and containers for a healthy baby; washing well with hot soapy water or using the dishwasher is fine. Also wash your hands immediately before pumping.

instruction: What kinds of containers should I use for storing pumped milk?
output: Use clean containers, preferably glass or rigid plastic, or special plastic bags designed for breast milk. Baby bottle insert bags are not strong or thick enough to protect the milk from contamination.

instruction: How long can I keep expressed breast milk at room temperature, in the fridge, or in the freezer?
output: Expressed breast milk can be stored at room temperature for three to five hours, in the refrigerator for up to three to five days, or in a freezer (0 degrees Fahrenheit / -20 degrees Celsius) for up to nine months. Store frozen milk in the back of the freezer and write the date on each container.

instruction: How much milk should I freeze for one feeding?
output: It’s useful to freeze about 3 to 4 ounces (90–120 ml) for a single feeding. You can also freeze some 1-to 2-ounce (30–60 ml) portions for small extras.

instruction: How should I thaw frozen breast milk and can I use a microwave to warm it?
output: Thaw frozen milk in the refrigerator, under warm running water, or immersed in a container of warm water. Don’t use the microwave to warm milk because it causes uneven heating and a risk of scalding.

instruction: Once breast milk is thawed, how long can I keep it and can I refreeze it?
output: Thawed milk should be refrigerated and used within twenty-four hours. Never refreeze thawed milk.

instruction: The thawed milk smells a bit different — is it still okay to give to my baby?
output: Stored breast milk may have an altered smell or taste due to fat breakdown, but this is not harmful to the baby. Swirl the container gently if the fat has separated until it looks uniform.

instruction: If my baby doesn’t finish a bottle of thawed breast milk, how long can it sit before I need to throw it away?
output: If the infant does not finish all the thawed breast milk in the bottle, it should be consumed within one to two hours or discarded.

instruction: How can I tell if my newborn is getting enough breast milk?
output: Many parents wonder about this. A well-fed newborn should lose no more than 10 percent of birth weight in the first few days before starting to gain. He should have one or two bowel movements per day in the first two days (blackish, tarry stools), at least two greenish-to-yellow stools on days three to four, and by days five to seven stools that are yellow, loose, with small curds, numbering at least three to four per day. By days five to seven he should also have six or more wet diapers per day with nearly colorless or pale yellow urine, appear content and happy for an average of one to three hours between feedings, and nurse at least eight to twelve times every twenty-four hours.

instruction: Will using a double electric pump really save time and increase milk?
output: Yes. A double electric pump expresses both breasts at the same time; this will increase your milk volume and save time. There are also bras and tops that help hold both cones in place.

instruction: Are the costs of pumps eligible as a deductible item if I use a flexible spending plan?
output: The costs related to breastfeeding supplies, such as pumps, may be a deductible item if you participate in a flexible spending plan.

instruction: Can I warm breast milk in the microwave to save time?
output: Don’t heat breast milk, formula, or bottles in a microwave. Microwaving overheats the milk in the center and can scald your baby’s mouth; the bottle can even explode if left in the microwave too long. Use a container of warm water instead.

instruction: How should I warm frozen breast milk safely?
output: Thaw or warm breast milk by placing the container in a container of warm water. If the fat has separated, gently swirl the container until the milk is uniform. Thawed milk should be refrigerated and used within twenty-four hours and should never be refrozen. If a bottle of thawed milk isn’t finished, it should be used within one to two hours or discarded.

instruction: My stored breast milk smells a bit different — is that harmful?
output: Stored breast milk may have an altered smell or taste because of fat breakdown. This change in smell or taste is not harmful to the baby. If the fat has separated, gently swirl the milk until it returns to a uniform consistency.

instruction: How long can I keep thawed breast milk in the refrigerator?
output: Thawed milk should be refrigerated and used within twenty-four hours. Never refreeze thawed milk. If an infant does not finish a bottle of thawed milk, it should be consumed within one to two hours or discarded.

instruction: How can I tell if my breastfed baby is getting enough to eat?
output: Many parents worry about this. Guidelines include: your newborn should lose no more than 10 percent of birth weight in the first few days before starting to gain weight; have one to two bowel movements per day with blackish, tarry stools in the first two days, then at least two greenish to yellow stools on days three and four, and by days five to seven have yellow, loose stools with small curds numbering at least three to four per day; have six or more wet diapers per day with nearly colorless or pale yellow urine by days five to seven; appear content and happy for an average of one to three hours between feedings; and nurse at least eight to twelve times every twenty-four hours.

instruction: What should my newborn’s bowel movements look like in the first week?
output: In the first two days expect one or two bowel movements per day that are blackish and tarry. On days three and four have at least two stools that are greenish to yellow. By days five to seven stools should be yellow, loose, with small curds and should number at least three to four per day. When milk production increases, your baby will often have a bowel movement with each feeding during the first month.

instruction: When should my baby be having six or more wet diapers each day?
output: By days five to seven your baby should have six or more wet diapers per day, with urine that is nearly colorless or pale yellow.

instruction: My breastfed baby refuses the bottle — what can I try?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Some breastfed babies accept a bottle easily, while others take an occasional bottle only if someone other than the mother offers it and the mother is out of sight. Once the baby is familiar with the bottle, he may take it in the mother’s presence. If your baby refuses a bottle, try offering milk in a cup or sippy cup instead; even premature newborns are able to cup-feed, and some breastfed babies go from breast to cup without ever using a bottle.

instruction: What should I do about sore or cracked nipples from breastfeeding?
output: It makes sense to feel worried about sore nipples. Mild soreness can occur in the first week, but breastfeeding should not cause sustained pain, discomfort, or open cracks. Proper latch-on is the most important factor in preventing sore and cracked nipples. If nipples or other breast areas are painful, seek advice from your lactation expert. During bathing, wash breasts with mild soap and use only water on the nipple; creams, lotions, and vigorous rubbing are not necessary and may aggravate the problem. Try varying the baby’s position at each feeding. You may use purified lanolin on cracked or dry nipples to protect and allow healing; if this does not solve the problem, consult your doctor or lactation consultant because you might have a yeast or bacterial infection of the nipple or a skin condition such as dermatitis that requires treatment.

instruction: My breasts are very engorged — how can I get relief?
output: It makes sense to be concerned about engorgement. Some engorgement is expected when lactation starts, but severe engorgement can occur if the baby doesn’t nurse often or efficiently in the first few days after your milk comes in. The best treatment is to breastfeed frequently, express milk between feedings manually or with a pump, and offer both breasts at every feeding. If the baby feeds from only one side, manually express milk from the other side. Warmth encourages milk flow, so standing in a warm shower while manually expressing may help. You may get relief with warm compresses during nursing and cool compresses between nursing. Relieve engorgement as soon as it is recognized to avoid a decrease in milk production.

instruction: What is mastitis and what should I do if I think I have it?
output: Mastitis is an infection of the breast tissue caused by bacteria. It causes flu-like symptoms such as fever, chills, headache, nausea, dizziness, and lack of energy, along with localized redness, tenderness, swelling, heat, and pain in and around the breast. If you experience any of these symptoms, call your doctor at once. Treatment includes continued milk removal by feeding or pumping, rest, fluids, antibiotics, and pain medicine if needed. Your doctor will prescribe an antibiotic that is safe during breastfeeding and you should take all the antibiotics even if you feel better. Do not stop nursing, because stopping will worsen the mastitis and cause increased pain. The milk itself is not infected, your baby will not be harmed by nursing during mastitis, and mastitis or the antibiotics will not change milk composition. Bed rest, sleep, and decreased activity will help recovery. If it’s too painful to nurse on the infected breast, let the milk flow onto a clean towel while you nurse from the opposite side so the baby can finish on the infected side with less discomfort. Some women find pumping more comfortable for a few feedings; pumped milk can be stored or fed to the baby immediately. A mother’s return to work is a peak time for developing mastitis, so it is important to express milk regularly, approximately on the same schedule as the baby would feed, to try to prevent infection.

instruction: Should I stop breastfeeding if I have mastitis?
output: Do not stop nursing; stopping will worsen the mastitis and cause increased pain. Milk removal by feeding or pumping is part of mastitis treatment.

instruction: Can I pump milk if nursing the painful breast is too uncomfortable during mastitis?
output: Yes. Some women with severe pain find it more comfortable to pump the breast than to feed the baby directly, even if just for a few feedings. Pumped milk can be stored or fed to the baby immediately after.

instruction: Will antibiotics for mastitis harm my baby or change my milk?
output: Many parents worry about this. Your doctor will prescribe an antibiotic that is safe during breastfeeding. The milk itself is not infected, your baby will not be harmed by nursing during mastitis, and mastitis or the antibiotics will not change milk composition. Be sure to take all the antibiotics even if you feel better.

instruction: How can I try to prevent mastitis when I go back to work?
output: A mother’s return to work is a peak time for development of mastitis. It is important to express milk regularly, approximately on the same schedule as the baby would feed, to try to prevent the infection.

instruction: My breasts are very swollen and painful after my milk came in. What should I do to relieve engorgement?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Try to breastfeed frequently and express milk between feedings manually or with a pump. Offer both breasts at every feeding; if the baby feeds only from one side, manually express milk from the other side. Standing in a warm shower while you manually express may help. You may also use warm compresses during nursing and cool compresses between nursing. Relieve engorgement as soon as you recognize it to avoid problems with milk supply.

instruction: Can severe engorgement cause my milk supply to drop?
output: Yes. If engorgement persists and milk is not removed, breasts have a mechanism to turn off milk production and this can lead to a decrease in milk supply.

instruction: My baby only nurses from one breast. How can I help the other breast?
output: If the baby feeds from only one side, manually express milk from the other side. Offer both breasts at every feeding; expressing between feedings by hand or pump can also help.

instruction: Should I use warm or cold compresses for engorged breasts?
output: Use warmth to encourage milk flow: warm compresses during nursing. Use cool compresses between nursing for relief.

instruction: What are the signs of mastitis and what should I do if I think I have it?
output: Mastitis causes flu-like symptoms such as fever, chills, headache, nausea, dizziness, and lack of energy along with localized redness, tenderness, swelling, heat, and pain in and around the breast. If you experience any of these symptoms, call your doctor at once. Treatment includes milk removal by feeding or pumping, rest, fluids, antibiotics, and pain medicine if needed.

instruction: Is it safe to keep nursing if I have mastitis and need antibiotics?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Do not stop nursing; stopping will worsen the mastitis and cause increased pain. The milk itself is not infected and your baby will not be harmed by nursing during mastitis. Your doctor will prescribe an antibiotic that is safe during breastfeeding, and you should take all the antibiotics even if you feel better.

instruction: It's very painful to nurse on the infected breast. What can I do so my baby can still get milk?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If it’s too painful to nurse on the infected breast, let the milk flow from the painful breast onto a clean towel or absorbent cloth while you nurse from the opposite side; then the baby can finish feeding on the infected side with less discomfort. Some women find pumping the breast more comfortable for a few feedings; the pumped milk can be stored or fed to the baby immediately.

instruction: Does returning to work affect my risk of mastitis?
output: A mother’s return to work is a peak time for the development of mastitis. It is important to express milk regularly, approximately on the same schedule as the baby would feed, to try to prevent the infection.

instruction: How does a supplemental nurser (infant feeding device) work when used at the breast?
output: A supplemental nurser is hung from your neck and the container holding formula or expressed milk has a thin flexible tube aligned along the breast with its tip adjacent to the nipple and placed in the corner of the baby’s mouth. The baby’s suction draws the formula or breast milk from the container so she gets supplemental milk while at the breast.

instruction: When might a supplemental nurser be used?
output: A supplemental nurser can be used if your milk supply is not meeting the baby’s needs, if you miss a number of feedings because of illness, if the baby is unable to nurse, for premature infants, to train babies with feeding problems, to stimulate lactation in adoptive mothers, or in mothers who stopped breastfeeding and wish to start again.

instruction: Can a breast pump fully replace baby suckling to maintain milk production?
output: No. Pumps do not stimulate or empty the breasts nearly as efficiently as your baby’s sucking, so expressing milk during missed feedings may not prevent a decrease in production.

instruction: How often should I nurse to try to reestablish my milk supply quickly?
output: To try to reestablish milk supply, nurse more frequently — every hour for twenty-four hours.

instruction: Where can I get a supplemental nurser and how long will it take to learn to use it?
output: Supplemental nursers are available from lactation specialists, medical supply stores, some pharmacies, or online. If possible, purchase the device from someone who can show you how to use and clean it. Most mothers and babies need a few days of practice to get comfortable, and using one requires commitment because it may take weeks or months to rebuild the milk supply and the breastfeeding relationship.

instruction: My breastfed baby seems constantly fussy and wants to nurse all the time. Could this be a growth spurt and what should I do?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Rapid growth phases often happen at two to three weeks, around six weeks, and again at about three months; during these spurts babies will want to nurse constantly. This frequent nursing is normal and temporary — when you nurse often for several days your breasts will make more milk. Do not give your baby other liquids. If the frequent feeding does not return to a more regular pattern after four to five days, call your pediatrician’s office for assistance so the baby can be checked.

instruction: My newborn seems to nurse constantly and is never satisfied after feedings. What should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel worried about this. If your baby feeds constantly and is never satisfied, your breastfeeding needs to be evaluated by an experienced healthcare provider. The provider will weigh and examine the baby, examine your breasts and nipples, and observe an entire breastfeeding session. The problem might be fixed by improving positioning and latch, or it may be more complicated if the baby has lost too much weight or is not gaining well.

instruction: My baby has been nursing every hour for several days. Could this be normal and how long might it last?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Rapid growth phases commonly happen at two to three weeks of age, again around six weeks, and again at about three months. During these spurts babies will want to nurse constantly; frequent nursing for several days signals the breasts to make more milk and is normal and temporary. Do not give other liquids. If the frequent feeding does not return to a more regular pattern after four to five days, or you are tempted to start bottles, call your pediatrician’s office so the baby can be seen, weighed, and the feeding process evaluated (or referred to a lactation specialist).

instruction: My 5-week-old has at least one long period each day of intense crying, turns red, and draws up her legs. Could this be colic?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Colic begins after four weeks of age. Colicky babies generally have at least one period each day when they appear to be in pain, cry hard, turn red, and draw their legs up. They may act hungry and then pull back and refuse the breast. Your baby’s doctor can provide suggestions for managing colic.

instruction: What are the signs of a hyperalert or high‑needs infant and what helps calm them?
output: Hyperalert or high‑needs infants require more holding, carrying, and motion and are often irregular in their eating, sleeping, and reactions. They seem to cry around the clock, tend to “snack” at the breast frequently, and sleep in brief fifteen‑ to thirty‑minute catnaps while being held. Slings or other baby carriers and swings are helpful to calm these babies. Despite the fussiness, they should be gaining weight normally.

instruction: My baby gulps milk, coughs, and seems uncomfortable after feeding. Could my milk flow be too strong?
output: It makes sense to feel concerned. Oversupply or an overactive let‑down can cause very full breasts, lots of leaking and spraying. The baby may gulp milk very fast, pull away to catch his breath, cough or sputter, and swallow lots of air and milk; later gas bubbles can form and cause discomfort. Your pediatrician may guide you to a lactation consultant to help with this problem.

instruction: My newborn spits up after every feeding. Is that normal or a problem?
output: Most newborns spit up after feeding; this is normal and requires no treatment. Physiological reflux is normal in all infants until twelve months of age and may be more prominent in preterm infants. Medication (antacid) is not required as long as your infant is gaining normal weight. If the spitting up leads to fussiness, becomes more like vomiting with much larger amounts, or is associated with weight loss, the baby should be evaluated by your pediatrician.

instruction: I think a food I’m eating is making my breastfed baby fussy. What should I try?
output: It makes sense to worry about this. If you suspect a particular food, avoid it for one week to see if the symptoms go away. Then you may try the food again carefully to see if the symptoms return.

instruction: How common are food allergies in breastfed babies and what should I watch for?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Allergies are less common than other reasons for fussiness but occur more often in babies from families where the mother, father, or siblings have asthma, eczema, or other allergic diseases. In the breastfed baby the mother’s diet may be the source. Allergic symptoms can linger for more than a week after the food has been removed from the mother’s diet. Sometimes food allergies, such as to milk protein, can result in blood in the stool. Severe food allergies can cause wheezing, hives, or shock and require attention from your pediatrician.

instruction: My baby has only blood in the stool but is otherwise well. Do I need to treat this?
output: If blood in the stool is the only symptom, generally no treatment is necessary.

instruction: My baby started wheezing and got hives after I ate dairy. What should I do now?
output: It makes sense to be worried. Wheezing, hives, or collapse are signs of a severe food allergy. Severe food allergies definitely require the attention of your pediatrician.

instruction: I’m thinking about using a supplemental nurser to give formula at the breast. What should I know?
output: Supplemental nursers are available from lactation specialists, medical supply stores, some pharmacies, or online. If possible, purchase the device from someone who can show you how to use and clean it. Most mothers and babies need a few days of practice to get comfortable with the device. Using a supplemental nurser requires commitment and dedication, as it may take weeks or months to rebuild the milk supply and the breastfeeding relationship.

instruction: I had breast implants. Will that stop me from breastfeeding?
output: Breast enlargement surgery usually does not interfere with breastfeeding provided the breasts were previously normal, the nipples were not moved, and no ducts were cut.

instruction: I had breast reduction surgery before pregnancy. Can I still breastfeed and what should I watch for?
output: It makes sense to feel uncertain about this. Breastfeeding after breast reduction surgery is highly individual because the surgery typically involves at least some disruption of breast tissue and often movement of the nipple and areola. Your baby’s weight should be checked more frequently, at least twice a week for the first few weeks, until the baby is gaining well. You can still breastfeed and supplement with formula if you aren’t able to produce enough milk, and your pediatrician needs to be aware of previous breast surgery so your infant can be followed closely.

instruction: I had cancer in the past but I’m no longer on chemo or radiation. Is breastfeeding safe for me?
output: Breastfeeding should be safe if you have been diagnosed with cancer or had a malignant tumor removed but are no longer on chemotherapy or radiation therapy; check with your physician.

instruction: My baby suddenly began crying nonstop and cannot be comforted. What should I do?
output: It makes sense to be alarmed. If endless crying occurs suddenly or seems unusually severe and the baby cannot be comforted, call your pediatrician or seek emergency care immediately.

instruction: My breastfed baby cries a lot — is it likely a food allergy from my diet?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Food allergies are less common than many other reasons for fussiness. Allergies occur more often when family members have asthma, eczema, or other allergic diseases. In breastfed babies, the mother’s diet can be the source, but it can be hard to pinpoint the exact food and symptoms can linger for more than a week after removing the food.

instruction: I noticed a little blood in my breastfed baby’s stool — do they need treatment?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If blood in the stool is the only symptom, generally no treatment is necessary. However, food allergies can sometimes be serious in other ways.

instruction: What are signs that a baby has a severe food allergy?
output: Severe food allergies can cause wheezing, hives, or shock (collapse). Such severe food allergies require the attention of your pediatrician.

instruction: My baby suddenly began crying nonstop and can’t be comforted — what should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Sudden or unusually severe, endless crying can be caused by serious illnesses not related to feeding; in that situation you should call your pediatrician or seek emergency care immediately.

instruction: Can I breastfeed if I had cancer in the past?
output: Most studies indicate breastfeeding offers some protection against breast cancer. If a woman has been diagnosed with cancer or had a malignant tumor removed but is no longer on chemotherapy or radiation, breastfeeding should be safe, though you should check with your physician. Breastfeeding is also safe after removal of a benign lump or cyst.

instruction: Will breast enlargement surgery stop me from breastfeeding?
output: Plastic surgery to enlarge the breasts usually does not interfere with breastfeeding provided the breasts were previously normal, the nipples were not moved, and no ducts were cut.

instruction: I had breast reduction surgery — can I still breastfeed and how will my baby be followed?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Breastfeeding after breast reduction is highly individual because surgery typically disrupts breast tissue and often moves the nipple and areola. Each mother–baby pair must be helped and followed individually. Your baby’s weight should be checked more frequently, at least twice a week for the first few weeks, until the baby is gaining well. You can still breastfeed and supplement with formula if you aren’t able to produce enough milk, and your pediatrician should be aware of your previous breast surgery so the infant can be followed closely. Make sure you discuss all concerns with your doctor.

instruction: If I can’t make enough milk, is it okay to supplement with formula?
output: Yes. You can still breastfeed and supplement with formula if you aren’t able to produce enough breast milk; this will provide your baby with some of the benefits of receiving breast milk.

instruction: Why can’t I give my infant cow’s milk before they’re one year old?
output: It makes sense to feel worried about this. Infants cannot digest cow’s milk as completely or easily as formula. Cow’s milk has high concentrations of protein and minerals that can stress a newborn’s immature kidneys and can cause severe illness in times of heat stress, fever, or diarrhea. Cow’s milk also lacks proper amounts of iron and vitamin C, may lead to iron-deficiency anemia because its protein can irritate the stomach and intestine causing blood loss in the stools, and does not contain the healthiest types of fat for growing babies. Your baby should not receive any cow’s milk or other non-human animal milk or milk substitute for the first twelve months after birth.

instruction: When can my child start drinking cow’s milk and how much is okay?
output: Once over one year old, your baby may consume whole cow’s milk or reduced-fat (2 percent) milk provided he has a balanced diet of solids. Intake should be limited to about 2 cups (approximately 16 ounces) per day or less. More than 24 ounces a day has been associated with iron deficiency if toddlers aren’t getting enough other healthy iron-rich foods. Whole vitamin D–fortified milk is recommended for most infants after one year; if a child is at risk for overweight or there is family history of obesity, high blood pressure, or heart disease, a pediatrician may recommend 2 percent milk. Do not give 1 percent or nonfat milk before the second birthday. After two years, discuss milk choice with your pediatrician.

instruction: Are homemade baby formulas okay to use?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics does not recommend homemade baby formulas because they can be deficient in vitamins and other important nutrients and may be contaminated with potentially harmful bacteria.

instruction: How are cow’s milk–based infant formulas altered to be safe for babies?
output: Cow’s milk–based formulas are treated by heating and other methods to make the protein more digestible, have more milk sugar (lactose) added to match breast milk concentration, have butterfat removed and replaced with vegetable oils and other fats that infants can more easily digest, and have additional iron added.

instruction: Should I use iron-fortified formula for my baby if I’m not breastfeeding?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics currently recommends iron-fortified formula for all infants who are not breastfed, or who are only partially breastfed, from birth to one year of age. Low-iron formulas should not be used.

instruction: Do I need clean water to prepare formula at night?
output: Yes. Formula must be bought and prepared or otherwise managed, and you’ll need a reliable source of clean water for mixing the formula, which means trips to the kitchen at night as well as extra bottles and equipment.

instruction: Who can give my baby a bottle if we choose bottle-feeding?
output: Dad, grandparents, sitters, and even older siblings can feed an infant breast milk or formula in a bottle, which may give some mothers more flexibility.

instruction: Can I give my 9-month-old cow's milk instead of formula?
output: No. Your baby should not receive any cow’s milk (or other non-human animal milk or milk substitute) for the first twelve months after birth.

instruction: When can my baby start drinking cow's milk and how much is OK each day?
output: Once your baby is over one year old, he may consume whole cow’s milk or reduced-fat (2 percent) milk provided he has a balanced diet of solid foods. Intake should be limited to 2 cups (about 16 ounces) per day or less. More than 24 ounces a day has been associated with iron deficiency if toddlers aren’t getting enough other healthy iron-rich foods. At this age, whole vitamin D–fortified milk is recommended for most infants because children still need a higher fat content; if your child is or is at risk for overweight or there is a family history of obesity, high blood pressure, or heart disease, your pediatrician may recommend 2 percent milk. Do not give 1 percent or nonfat milk before his second birthday.

instruction: My toddler drinks about 28 ounces of milk every day — should I be worried?
output: It makes sense to feel concerned. More than 24 ounces a day has been associated with iron deficiency if toddlers aren’t getting enough other healthy iron-rich foods. If your child is not yet eating a broad range of solids, talk to your pediatrician about the best nutrition for him.

instruction: Is it OK to give my 18-month-old 1% or skim milk?
output: Do not give 1 percent (lowfat) or nonfat (skim) milk before his second birthday, because it does not contain enough fat for brain development.

instruction: What are cow's milk–based infant formulas and how are they changed for babies?
output: Cow’s milk–based formulas account for about 80 percent of formula sold. The milk is treated by heating and other methods to make the protein more digestible, more milk sugar (lactose) is added to match breast milk concentration, and the butterfat is removed and replaced with vegetable oils and other fats that infants can more easily digest and that are better for infant growth. Cow’s milk formulas have additional iron added, and most formulas also have DHA and ARA added; some are fortified with probiotics or prebiotics.

instruction: I've heard iron in formula causes constipation — is that true?
output: Many parents wonder about this. The amount of iron in infant formula does not contribute to constipation.

instruction: What is an extensively hydrolyzed (predigested) formula and who needs it?
output: Extensively hydrolyzed formula is often called “predigested,” since the protein content has already been broken down into smaller proteins that can be digested more easily. Ask your pediatrician to recommend a brand of hypoallergenic formula if one is needed for allergies or other conditions. These formulas tend to be costlier than regular formulas.

instruction: Can I use soy formula if my baby can't digest lactose or if I'm a vegan parent?
output: Soy formulas contain soy protein and a carbohydrate (either glucose or sucrose) different from milk-based formulas. They are sometimes recommended for babies unable to digest lactose, although lactose-free cow’s milk–based formula is also available. Some strict vegetarian and vegan parents choose to use soy formula because it contains no animal products, but breastfeeding is the best option for vegetarian and vegan families. The American Academy of Pediatrics believes there are few circumstances when soy formula should be chosen instead of cow’s milk–based formula.

instruction: My baby had diarrhea and now seems to be bothered by milk — should I switch to lactose-free formula?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Many infants have brief periods when they cannot digest lactose, particularly following bouts of diarrhea; this is usually only a temporary problem and does not require a change in your baby’s diet. While lactose-free formulas are fine sources of nutrition, check with your pediatrician before starting a lactose-free formula, since whatever problem she may be having is likely due to something else.

instruction: Could colic and bloody diarrhea be caused by a milk allergy, and what would be needed then?
output: I’m sorry you’re worried about this. A true milk allergy can cause colic, failure to thrive, and even bloody diarrhea; the allergy is to the protein in cow’s milk formula. In that case, soy formulas may seem like a good alternative, but up to half of infants with a milk allergy are also sensitive to soy and thus must be given specialized formula (such as amino acid–based or elemental) or breast milk.

instruction: What is galactosemia and how is it checked in newborns?
output: Galactosemia is a rare disorder in which children have an intolerance to galactose, one of the two sugars in lactose; children with this condition cannot tolerate breast milk and must be fed a lactose-free formula. All states include a blood test for galactosemia in routine newborn screening after birth.

instruction: What are probiotics in infant formula and what do they do?
output: Probiotics are live bacteria added to some formulas or given as drops or powders; common types are strains of bifidobacteria and lactobacilli. In formula-fed babies, probiotics promote a balance of bacteria in the intestines and offset the growth of “unfriendly” organisms. Some research has shown probiotics may prevent or treat infectious diarrhea and atopic dermatitis, and other possible benefits are being studied. The evidence is limited, benefits appear to occur only as long as probiotics are being taken, and intestinal bacteria levels return to previous levels once probiotic-fortified formula is stopped. Before giving your child infant formula fortified with probiotics, discuss the issue with your pediatrician.

instruction: Do any formulas have prebiotics to help the baby's gut?
output: Yes. Some formulas are fortified with prebiotics in the form of manufactured oligosaccharides, intended to mimic the natural breast milk oligosaccharides that promote a healthy intestinal lining.

instruction: Are DHA and ARA added to infant formulas?
output: Most formulas have docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) and arachidonic acid (ARA) added; these are fatty acids believed to be important for the development of a baby’s brain and eyes.

instruction: Who regulates the safety and contents of infant formula in the United States?
output: US law and the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) govern the contents and safe production and distribution of all infant formulas.

instruction: Should I use a low-iron formula because I'm worried about iron levels?
output: Low-iron formulas should not be used. The American Academy of Pediatrics currently recommends iron-fortified formula be used for all infants who are not breastfed, or who are only partially breastfed, from birth to one year of age. Additional iron is available in many foods, especially in meats, egg yolks, and iron-fortified cereals.

instruction: What are probiotics in infant formula and what do they do?
output: Probiotics are live "good" bacteria (the word means "for life"). Some formulas are fortified with these bacteria, and doctors may recommend probiotic drops or powders for breastfed infants. In formula-fed babies, probiotics help promote a balance of bacteria in the intestines and can offset growth of "unfriendly" organisms that could cause infections and inflammation. The most common types are strains of bifidobacteria and lactobacilli. It may help to discuss probiotic use with your pediatrician.

instruction: My baby was born by C-section. Could probiotics help her?
output: Many pediatricians are starting to use probiotics for infants delivered by C-section or whose mothers received antibiotics during labor. The common probiotic strains in formulas and supplements are bifidobacteria and lactobacilli. It makes sense to feel unsure about this; before giving probiotics, discuss the option with your pediatrician.

instruction: How do I prepare one bottle from concentrated formula?
output: Wash your hands and measure the concentrate. Pour in an equal amount of water, shake, and use promptly. Any unused concentrate may be covered and refrigerated for no more than forty-eight hours.

instruction: How should I mix powdered formula safely?
output: Powdered formula usually requires one level scoop of powder for every 2 ounces (60 ml) of water—mix thoroughly to avoid clumps and always follow the label instructions exactly. Powder is not commercially sterile and has been linked to a very rare severe illness from Cronobacter bacteria; the CDC and other US organizations recommend using water heated to at least 158°F (70°C) before mixing powder to reduce this risk. It’s understandable to be concerned about safety.

instruction: Can I prepare formula in advance and how long can I store it?
output: Store formula prepared in advance in the refrigerator to discourage bacterial growth. Unused refrigerated formula should be discarded after twenty-four hours. If you prepare formula from concentrate and have unused concentrate, it may be covered and refrigerated for no more than forty-eight hours.

instruction: Is it okay to warm a bottle in the microwave?
output: Do not use a microwave. Refrigerated formula can be left out for an hour or warmed in a pan of hot water. Test the temperature by shaking a few drops onto the inside of your wrist before feeding.

instruction: How should I clean bottles, nipples, and utensils used for formula?
output: Make sure all bottles, nipples, and utensils are clean. If your home has chlorinated water, you can use a dishwasher or hot tap water with dishwashing detergent and then rinse in hot tap water. For nonchlorinated water, boil the utensils for five to ten minutes.

instruction: Does breastfeeding affect a baby’s risk of cavities?
output: Breast milk contains lactose, but breastfeeding until up to twelve months of age decreases the risk of dental caries by half, most likely because of immune-modulating effects and a protective microbiome despite the lactose. If an infant breastfeeds to sleep, the gums and erupting teeth should be wiped to minimize the risk of caries.

instruction: What types of infant formula are available and how do they differ?
output: Most infant formulas come as ready-to-feed liquid, concentrate, or powder. Ready-to-feed is very convenient but the most expensive. Concentrate is prepared by adding water as the manufacturer directs. Powder is the least expensive, light and portable, and does not spoil if it stays in the can or bottle before adding water.

instruction: If my baby stops formula with probiotics, will the benefit remain?
output: Benefits from probiotic-fortified formula appear to occur only as long as the probiotics are being taken. Once your baby stops consuming probiotic-fortified formula, intestinal bacteria levels return to previous levels. By contrast, bacteria in the gut resulting from breastfeeding are more resilient.

instruction: My well water might be unsafe—what should I do before using it to make formula?
output: If you use well water or are concerned about tap water safety, boil it for approximately one minute before adding it to formula and let it return to room temperature before feeding. If there is any concern, it may be a good idea to have your well water tested for bacteria or other contaminants. You can also use bottled water.

instruction: How much powdered formula should I use per amount of water?
output: Most powdered formula requires one level scoop of powder for every 2 ounces (60 ml) of water. Mix thoroughly to ensure there are no clumps and always read the label instructions for proper mixing.

instruction: Why is it important to follow the manufacturer’s directions when mixing formula?
output: Follow the manufacturer’s directions exactly because too much water means your baby won’t get the calories and nutrients needed for proper growth, while too little water makes extra-concentrated formula that could cause diarrhea or dehydration and gives your infant more calories than needed.

instruction: What are the most common probiotic strains found in infant products?
output: The most common types of probiotics in infant formulas and supplements are strains of bifidobacteria and lactobacilli.

instruction: Can breastfed babies get probiotic supplements too?
output: Yes. Doctors may recommend probiotic drops or powders for breastfed infants, and parents can find probiotic supplements outside of formula for breastfed babies. Research on benefits is ongoing and the evidence for many effects is limited.

instruction: My pediatrician says my premature baby needs a specialized formula—what should I do?
output: Specialized formulas exist for infants with specific disorders or for premature babies. If your pediatrician recommends a specialized formula, follow their guidance about feeding requirements (amounts, scheduling, and any special preparations), since these may be different from regular formulas. It makes sense to feel unsure about special instructions.

instruction: How long can I keep formula I prepare in the refrigerator?
output: Store formula prepared in advance in the refrigerator to discourage bacterial growth. Unused refrigerated formula should be discarded after twenty-four hours.

instruction: Can I warm a bottle in the microwave?
output: Do not use a microwave. Refrigerated formula doesn’t necessarily have to be warmed, but if you warm it you can leave the bottle out for an hour or warm it in a pan of hot water. Test the temperature by shaking a few drops onto the inside of your wrist before feeding.

instruction: What are the basic steps for mixing powdered formula from powder?
output: Wash your hands, then add powder. Measure water and stir to mix thoroughly so there are no clumps. Fill clean bottles and place them in the refrigerator. Always read the label instructions and follow the manufacturer’s directions exactly.

instruction: Is powdered infant formula sterile and safe to use?
output: Powdered infant formula is not commercially sterile and has been associated with a severe illness attributed to Cronobacter bacteria, although the illness is very rare. It’s understandable to be concerned. The CDC and other U.S. organizations recommend heating the water to at least 158°F (70°C) before mixing with powder to reduce the possibility of infection; the WHO has issued guidelines to improve safety.

instruction: How much powder do I use per amount of water?
output: Most powdered formula requires one level scoop of powder for every 2 ounces (60 ml) of water. Always read the label instructions for mixing the formula properly.

instruction: Can I leave a warmed bottle sitting out for an hour before feeding?
output: Yes. Refrigerated formula can be warmed by leaving the bottle out for an hour or by warming it in a pan of hot water. Always test the temperature before feeding.

instruction: How should I clean bottles, nipples, and other feeding utensils?
output: Make sure all bottles, nipples, and other utensils used in preparation and feeding are clean. If your home has chlorinated water, you can use a dishwasher or hot tap water with dishwashing detergent and then rinse in hot tap water. For nonchlorinated water, boil the utensils for five to ten minutes.

instruction: How do I know if a nipple hole is the right size?
output: Check the size of the hole. If it’s too small your baby may suck hard and swallow too much air; if it’s too big the formula may flow so fast he might choke. Ideally, formula should flow from an upended bottle at a rate of one drop per second and stop dripping after a few seconds.

instruction: Are bottles that promote self-feeding a good idea?
output: Bottles designed to promote self-feeding are not recommended because they may contribute to early childhood caries (ECC) by promoting constant feeding and overexposing the teeth to sugars. Self-feeding while lying on the back has also been shown to contribute occasionally to ear infections.

instruction: When does early childhood caries (ECC) most commonly occur?
output: ECC is most commonly seen in children six months and older who bottle or breastfeed to fall asleep or who have demand at-will feedings throughout the night.

instruction: Should I use glass bottles once my baby starts holding the bottle?
output: As your baby begins holding the bottle herself, avoid using breakable glass bottles.

instruction: Can you give me a simple checklist for preparing powdered formula from start to finish?
output: Wash your hands, add powder, measure water and stir to mix thoroughly so there are no clumps, fill clean bottles, and place them in the refrigerator. Follow the manufacturer’s directions exactly.

instruction: What should I do if I use well water for infant formula?
output: If you use well water or are concerned about your tap water, boil it for approximately one minute before adding it to the formula and let it return to room temperature before feeding. You also can use bottled water, and it may be a good idea to have your well water tested for bacteria or other contaminants.

instruction: How can I test if warmed formula is too hot?
output: Test warmed formula by shaking a few drops onto the inside of your wrist to make sure it’s not too hot before feeding.

instruction: Is it okay to give my baby a bottle to suck on overnight?
output: A bottle should not be given to suck on overnight. If you give your infant a bedtime feeding, take away the bottle and brush his teeth before he falls asleep to help prevent early childhood caries (ECC).

instruction: Can I give my baby a bottle to suck on all night?
output: It’s best not to give a bottle to suck on overnight. If you give a bedtime feeding, take the bottle away and brush the baby’s teeth before he falls asleep to help prevent early childhood tooth decay (ECC).

instruction: How should I hold and position my baby during bottle feedings to reduce choking and ear infection risk?
output: Cradle your baby in a semi-upright position and support his head. Don’t feed him when he’s lying flat, because lying flat increases the risk of choking and may allow formula to flow into the middle ear, which can lead to infection. Hold the bottle so formula fills the neck and nipple to prevent the baby from swallowing air as he sucks.

instruction: What should I look for when choosing a bottle nipple?
output: You may need to try several nipples to find the one your baby prefers. Options include standard silicone or rubber nipples, orthodontic ones, and special designs for premature infants or babies with cleft palates. Always check the size of the hole: if it’s too small the baby may suck so hard he swallows too much air; if it’s too big the formula may flow too fast and he might choke.

instruction: How fast should formula flow from an upended bottle?
output: Ideally, formula should flow from an upended bottle at a rate of about one drop per second and should stop dripping after a few seconds.

instruction: How much formula should a newborn eat in the first week?
output: In the first week after birth, babies should be eating no more than 1 to 2 ounces (30–60 ml) per feed.

instruction: How often do formula-fed babies usually feed?
output: Formula-fed babies typically feed on a more regular schedule, often every three to four hours.

instruction: If my baby falls asleep and sleeps more than four to five hours in the first weeks, should I wake her to feed?
output: During the first few weeks, if your baby sleeps longer than four to five hours and starts missing feedings, wake her up and offer a bottle.

instruction: My baby gets fidgety and stops sucking during a feed — is he full?
output: If your baby becomes fidgety or easily distracted during a feeding, he’s probably finished. If he drains the bottle and continues smacking his lips, he might still be hungry.

instruction: How much formula will my baby likely take by six months?
output: By six months, your baby will typically consume about 6 to 8 ounces (180–240 ml) at each of four or five feedings in twenty-four hours.

instruction: How do I know how much formula my baby needs each day based on weight?
output: On average, a baby should consume about 2½ ounces (75 ml) of formula a day for every pound (453 g) of body weight, though babies often regulate their own intake day to day.

instruction: Should I give my infant vitamin D or other supplements?
output: Many parents wonder about this. The American Academy of Pediatrics believes healthy children receiving a normal, well-balanced diet do not need vitamin supplements beyond the recommended dietary allowances, which include 400 IU (10 mcg) of vitamin D a day for infants less than one year of age and 600 IU (15 mcg) a day for children over one year of age. Megadoses of vitamins can cause toxic symptoms. Talk with your pediatrician before giving vitamin supplements.

instruction: What is paced feeding and why is it recommended for bottle-fed babies?
output: Paced feedings are recommended to avoid overfeeding, a problem commonly seen with bottle-fed babies.

instruction: How can I reduce the chance of ear infections related to bottle feeding?
output: Avoid feeding your baby when he’s lying flat because that can allow formula to flow into the middle ear, leading to possible infection. Also avoid letting an infant self-feed while lying on the back, since that has been shown to contribute occasionally to ear infections.

instruction: My baby finishes the bottle but keeps trying to suck — is that normal?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Some babies have higher needs for sucking and may want to suck on a pacifier after feeding even when they’ve finished their bottle.

instruction: When do most babies stop needing middle-of-the-night feedings?
output: Between two and four months of age (or when they weigh more than 12 pounds [5.4 kg]), most babies no longer need middle-of-the-night feedings, because they’re consuming more during the day and their sleeping patterns have become more regular, though this varies from baby to baby.

instruction: How much formula should my one-month-old be drinking at each feeding?
output: By the end of the first month, she should be up to at least 3 to 4 ounces (120 ml) per feeding, with a fairly predictable schedule of feedings about every three to four hours.

instruction: What should my six-month-old be eating per feeding and how often?
output: By six months, your baby will consume about 6 to 8 ounces (180–240 ml) at each of four or five feedings in twenty-four hours.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby has had enough at a bottle feeding?
output: If she becomes fidgety or easily distracted during a feeding, she’s probably finished. If she drains the bottle and continues smacking her lips, she might still be hungry. Babies usually regulate their intake from day to day to meet their own needs.

instruction: Is there a daily maximum amount of formula my baby should drink?
output: Your baby should usually drink no more than an average of about 36 ounces (960 ml) of formula in twenty-four hours, although this is not a strict maximum. Some babies have higher sucking needs and may want a pacifier after feeding.

instruction: When do most babies stop needing middle-of-the-night feedings?
output: Between two and four months of age (or when they weigh more than 12 pounds [5.4 kg]), most babies no longer need middle-of-the-night feedings because they’re consuming more during the day and their sleeping patterns have become more regular.

instruction: What are signs my baby might be overfed?
output: Some signs of overfeeding are: if bottle-fed, consuming more than 4 to 6 ounces (120 to 180 ml) per feeding; vomiting most or all of the food after a complete feeding; and having loose, very watery stools eight or more times a day.

instruction: What are signs my baby might not be getting enough to eat?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Signs of underfeeding include: if breastfed, falling asleep after a short time and not seeming satisfied; wetting fewer than four diapers per day (especially if sleeping through the night) which may mean feeding inadequately and urinating less often; infrequent or very hard stools in the first month; appearing hungry and searching to suck shortly after feedings; becoming more yellow instead of less during the first week; and seeming excessively sleepy or lethargic.

instruction: What signs suggest a feeding allergy or digestive problem?
output: Signs of a feeding allergy or digestive disturbance include vomiting most or all food after a complete feeding; producing loose and very watery stools eight or more times a day or having blood in the stools; and having a severe skin rash.

instruction: Do breastfed babies need vitamin D drops?
output: Breastfed infants need supplemental vitamin D. The Academy recommendation is a minimum intake of 400 IU (10 mcg) of vitamin D per day beginning soon after birth. Sunscreen and keeping children out of direct sun limit skin production of vitamin D, so talk to your pediatrician about supplemental vitamin D drops.

instruction: Does formula provide vitamin D or do formula-fed babies need supplements?
output: Prepared formula has vitamin D added to it, so if your baby is drinking formula, vitamin D supplementation is usually not needed.

instruction: Should I give my baby vitamin supplements in general?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics believes that healthy children receiving a normal, well-balanced diet do not need vitamin supplementation over and above the recommended dietary allowances, which include 400 IU of vitamin D for infants under one year and 600 IU for children over one year. Megadoses of vitamins can produce toxic symptoms such as nausea, rashes, headaches, and sometimes more severe effects. Talk with your pediatrician before giving vitamin supplements to your child.

instruction: Should I take vitamins while nursing?
output: Pediatricians recommend that nursing mothers continue taking a daily multivitamin supplement to ensure proper nutritional balance. If you follow a strict vegetarian diet, you need to take an extra B-complex supplement because certain B vitamins come mainly from meat, poultry, or fish products.

instruction: My baby still seems to want to feed a lot and sometimes just sucks — what can I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your baby still seems to feed frequently or consume larger amounts, try distracting him with play or offer a pacifier, since some babies have higher sucking needs. If your baby consistently seems to want more or less, discuss this with your pediatrician.

instruction: How can I estimate how much formula my baby needs per day based on weight?
output: On average, your baby should consume about 2½ ounces (75 ml) of formula a day for every pound (453 g) of body weight, though babies often adjust their intake day to day to meet their own needs.

instruction: Do breastfed babies need vitamin D supplements?
output: Yes. Breastfed infants need supplemental vitamin D because breast milk does not provide enough, and the American Academy of Pediatrics recommends discussing vitamin D drops with your pediatrician.

instruction: My baby is drinking prepared formula — do I still need to give vitamin D drops?
output: Usually not. Prepared infant formula has vitamin D added, so if your baby is drinking formula, vitamin D supplementation is usually not needed. You can talk with your pediatrician if you have questions.

instruction: How much vitamin D should infants and young children get each day?
output: The Academy recommends a minimum intake of 400 IU (10 mcg) of vitamin D per day beginning soon after birth, and 600 IU (15 mcg) per day for children over age one.

instruction: My baby was born at about 4 pounds and is breastfed — when should iron supplements start?
output: It makes sense to feel concerned. Premature or low-birth-weight infants have lower iron stores and usually begin iron supplementation within two weeks after birth.

instruction: When should a healthy, term breastfed baby start iron supplements?
