output: For breastfeeding infants who weighed more than about 5½ pounds (2.5 kg) at birth and did not require NICU care, no additional iron is necessary until four months of age. The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends beginning iron supplementation at four months for breastfeeding infants.

instruction: Should I give my baby water before six months?
output: No. Until your baby starts solid foods, he gets all the water he needs from breast milk or formula. In the first six months, additional water or juice is not needed for breastfed or bottle-fed infants.

instruction: Is it okay to give my 8-month-old juice?
output: Juice is not recommended for babies in the first year. It is healthier for infants to eat fruit and drink water instead of drinking juice.

instruction: We use well water at home — should I worry about fluoride for my baby?
output: It makes sense to want to know. Have your well tested to determine the amount of natural fluoride. After six months, infants need appropriate fluoride supplementation only if local drinking water contains less than 0.3 ppm fluoride, and your pediatrician or pediatric dentist can advise about fluoride drops.

instruction: Can babies under six months receive fluoride supplements?
output: No. Babies under six months should not receive fluoride supplementation.

instruction: I'm a strict vegetarian and breastfeeding — do I need extra vitamins?
output: Pediatricians recommend that mothers continue taking a daily multivitamin supplement. If you are on a strict vegetarian diet, you need to take an extra B‑complex supplement because certain B vitamins are available only from meat, poultry, or fish products.

instruction: How long should I use iron-fortified formula for my bottle-fed baby?
output: If bottle-feeding, it is recommended to use iron-fortified formula from birth through the entire first year.

instruction: Should I delay clamping the umbilical cord to prevent iron deficiency?
output: The best way to help prevent iron deficiency is to allow the umbilical cord to pulsate for at least thirty to sixty seconds before clamping and cutting. You should speak with your obstetrician about this practice before delivery.

instruction: My breastfed baby has a fever and vomiting — what fluids should I give?
output: I’m sorry you’re dealing with this. Your baby may need extra fluids when ill, and the best fluid for a breastfed infant who is ill is breast milk. Ask your pediatrician what liquids are best and how much to give at these times.

instruction: When can I start offering my baby water in a cup?
output: After six months you may offer small amounts of water in a cup. Don’t force it or worry if he rejects it; he may prefer to get extra liquid from more frequent feedings.

instruction: How long should we consider fluoride supplements for our child?
output: Appropriate fluoride supplementation is based on each child’s unique needs. A supplement should be considered by you and your doctor until all of a child’s permanent teeth are present in the mouth, and your pediatrician or pediatric dentist can advise on necessity and dosage.

instruction: Should I give my 3-month-old fluoride drops?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Babies under six months should not receive fluoride supplementation.

instruction: At what age do babies need fluoride supplements?
output: After six months, breastfed and formula-fed infants need appropriate fluoride supplementation if local drinking water contains less than 0.3 parts per million (ppm) of fluoride.

instruction: How can I find out if our home water has enough fluoride for my baby?
output: If you have well water, have the well tested to determine the amount of natural fluoride. If your baby consumes bottled water or your home has a municipal water supply, check to see if the water is fluoridated.

instruction: We use only bottled water to mix formula. Should we use nursery water with fluoride?
output: If your family prefers bottled water, you should consider purchasing water marketed for babies with fluoride added (sometimes called “nursery water”). It is available in the baby food aisle and can be used when mixing formula.

instruction: Do formula-fed infants get any fluoride from their formula?
output: Formula-fed infants receive some fluoride from their formula if the drinking water is fluoridated.

instruction: How long should we consider fluoride supplements for our child?
output: Appropriate fluoride supplementation is based on each child’s unique needs. A supplement should be considered by you and your doctor until all of a child’s permanent teeth are present in the mouth.

instruction: Can I give my 6-month-old fruit juice?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends fruit juice not be given to infants under twelve months of age because it offers no nutritional benefit in this age group and may increase risk of tooth decay and preference for sweeter flavors instead of plain water.

instruction: How much fruit juice can a toddler aged 2 have each day?
output: For children ages one to six years old, limit fruit juice consumption to 4 to 6 ounces (120–180 ml) each day. For children older than twelve months, 4 ounces of juice per day given as part of a meal may be a reasonable option.

instruction: My baby swallows a lot of air and gets fussy during bottle feeds — what should I do?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Stop the feeding rather than letting your infant fuss and feed at the same time, and try burping frequently. Pausing and changing position slows gulping and reduces swallowed air. If bottle-feeding, burp after every 2 to 3 ounces (60–90 ml).

instruction: When should I burp my breastfed baby?
output: Burp the baby when she switches breasts. Some breastfed babies don’t swallow very much air and therefore may not need to burp.

instruction: What should I do if my baby gets hiccups during a feeding?
output: If hiccups occur during a feeding, change his position, try to get him to burp, or help him relax and wait until the hiccups are gone to resume feeding. If they don’t disappear in five to ten minutes, try to resume feeding for a few minutes; doing this usually stops them.

instruction: My infant spits up a lot. Is this dangerous and should I change how I put him to sleep?
output: Spitting up is common and usually no cause for concern; it almost never involves choking, coughing, discomfort, or danger even if it occurs while sleeping. The best position for sleep is on his back and with the mattress flat, not elevated.

instruction: How can I burp my baby—what techniques work?
output: Try holding the baby upright with his head on your shoulder and gently patting his back; sitting the baby on your lap supporting chest and head with one hand while patting his back with the other; or laying the baby on your lap on his back with his head higher than his chest and gently patting or rotating your hand on his back. If he still hasn’t burped after several minutes, continue feeding and burp him again when he’s finished, then keep him upright for ten to fifteen minutes.

instruction: How can I tell the difference between spitting up and vomiting, and when should I contact the pediatrician?
output: Vomiting is forceful, usually causes great distress and discomfort, generally occurs soon after a meal, and produces a much greater volume than spitting up. If your baby vomits on a regular basis (one or more times a day) or if you notice blood or a bright green color in your baby’s vomit, consult your pediatrician.

instruction: How should I hold my baby when trying to burp him over my shoulder?
output: Hold the baby upright with his head on your shoulder so you support his head and back, and gently pat his back with your other hand.

instruction: What’s an easy way to burp a baby while I’m sitting down?
output: Sit the baby on your lap, support his chest and head with one hand, and gently pat his back with your other hand.

instruction: Can I lay my baby across my lap to burp him? How do I position him?
output: Yes. Lay the baby on your lap with his back up, support his head so it is higher than his chest, and gently pat or rotate your hand on his back.

instruction: My baby didn’t burp after a few minutes of trying. Should I stop feeding?
output: If he still hasn’t burped after several minutes, continue feeding him and don’t worry; no baby burps every time. When he’s finished, burp him again.

instruction: How long should I keep my baby upright after a feeding to prevent spit-up?
output: When he’s finished, burp him again and keep him in an upright position for ten to fifteen minutes so he doesn’t spit up. Also, as a general tip, hold the baby in an upright position for twenty to thirty minutes after each feeding.

instruction: What’s the difference between normal spit-up and vomiting?
output: Spitting up is usually something most babies don’t even seem to notice. Vomiting is forceful, usually causes great distress and discomfort, generally occurs soon after a meal, and produces a much greater volume than spitting up.

instruction: My baby vomits once a day. Is that something I should worry about?
output: If your baby vomits on a regular basis (one or more times a day), consult your pediatrician.

instruction: I saw bright green color in my baby’s vomit. What should I do?
output: If you notice blood or a bright green color in your baby’s vomit, consult your pediatrician.

instruction: How often should I burp a bottle-fed baby during a feeding?
output: Burp your bottle-fed baby at least every three to five minutes during feedings.

instruction: What can I do to reduce how much my baby spits up during feedings?
output: Make each feeding calm, quiet, and leisurely. Avoid interruptions, sudden noises, bright lights, and other distractions during feedings. Avoid feeding while your infant is lying down. Hold the baby upright for twenty to thirty minutes after each feeding. Do not jostle or play vigorously with the baby immediately after feeding. Try to feed her before she gets frantically hungry.

instruction: How can I tell if a bottle nipple hole is the right size?
output: If the hole is the proper size, a few drops should come out when you invert the bottle, and then stop.

instruction: What should I do if I’m worried about breastfeeding and need help?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Talk to your obstetrician and pediatrician for medical information and support. Attend a breastfeeding class, talk to prenatal instructors, watch online videos, and ask women who have breastfed. Talk to members of La Leche League or other mother-to-mother support groups; their website is llli.org. See if there is a Baby Café in your area at babycafeusa.org/your-nearest-baby-cafe.html. Read recommended resources such as the American Academy of Pediatrics’ New Mother’s Guide to Breastfeeding and visit healthychildren.org for more information.

instruction: Should I feed my baby if she is lying down?
output: Avoid feeding while your infant is lying down.

instruction: Is it okay to play with my baby right after a feeding?
output: Do not jostle or play vigorously with the baby immediately after feeding.

instruction: What if the bottle nipple is too big or too small—how does that affect feeding?
output: A hole that is too big lets formula flow too fast; a hole that is too small frustrates your baby and causes her to gulp air. The proper size lets a few drops come out when inverted, then stop.

instruction: How often should I burp my bottle-fed baby during a feeding?
output: Burp your bottle-fed baby at least every three to five minutes during feedings.

instruction: Is it okay to feed my infant while she is lying down?
output: Avoid feeding while your infant is lying down.

instruction: How long do I need to hold my baby upright after a feeding?
output: Hold the baby in an upright position for twenty to thirty minutes after each feeding.

instruction: Can I jostle or play with my baby right after she finishes a bottle?
output: Do not jostle or play vigorously with the baby immediately after feeding.

instruction: I'm worried my baby gets very upset before I can feed her — when should I try to feed her?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Try to feed her before she gets frantically hungry.

instruction: What signs show the bottle nipple hole is the right size?
output: If the hole is the proper size, a few drops should come out when you invert the bottle, and then stop.

instruction: My baby keeps gulping air during feeds — what might cause that?
output: It makes sense to be concerned. A hole that is too small can frustrate your baby and cause her to gulp air; check the nipple hole size.

instruction: What happens if the nipple hole is too big?
output: A hole that is too big lets the formula flow too fast.

instruction: What happens if the nipple hole is too small?
output: A hole that is too small frustrates your baby and causes her to gulp air; make sure the nipple hole is not too small.

instruction: How can I quickly test whether the nipple hole is correct?
Do these feeding recommendations apply to every baby I meet?
output: The recommendations in this section apply to infants in general. Your child is unique and may have special needs.

instruction: If I still have questions about feeding that aren't answered here, what should I do?
output: If you have questions that these pages have not answered to your satisfaction, ask your pediatrician to help you find the answers that apply specifically to you and your infant.

instruction: Should I burp my baby even if she seems calm during the bottle?
output: Burp your bottle-fed baby at least every three to five minutes during feedings.

instruction: How should I handle feeding when the baby becomes frantic and hard to calm?
output: Try to feed her before she gets frantically hungry.

instruction: My newborn's eyes are blue — will they stay that color?
output: Many Caucasian newborns have blue eyes but this may change over the first year. If a baby’s eyes are going to turn brown, they’ll probably become muddy-looking during the first six months; however, if they’re still blue at that time, they’ll probably remain so. Infants with dark-skinned heritage generally have brown eyes at birth and tend to retain that color.

instruction: There's a blood-red spot in the white of my baby's eye and her face is puffy after birth — is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. A blood-red spot in the whites of one or both eyes and general puffiness of a newborn’s face are most commonly caused by pressures exerted during labor and tend to fade in a few days. If your baby was delivered by C-section, she may not have this puffiness and the whites of her eyes likely will not have any red spots.

instruction: My newborn's skin is peeling — should I treat it?
output: Peeling skin at birth is normal whether your baby was born early, late, or on time and usually requires no treatment.

instruction: My baby has fine hair on her shoulders and back — will that go away?
output: That fine hair is called lanugo and is produced toward the end of pregnancy; it’s usually shed before birth or soon thereafter. If your baby was born before her due date, she is more likely to still have this hair and it may take a couple of weeks to disappear.

instruction: Some patches on my baby's skin look blotchy when I take off her blanket — why does that happen?
output: Mottled or blotchy-looking patches are commonly caused by exposure to cool air and will disappear quickly if you cover her again.

instruction: There are scratches on my newborn's face from her nails — how should I handle that?
output: It’s a good reminder it’s time to trim her fingernails. It can be nerve-racking for new parents, so don’t hesitate to ask advice from a nurse at the hospital nursery, your pediatrician’s office, or anyone else with experience trimming babies’ nails.

instruction: What are stork bites and will they go away?
output: Stork bites, also called angel kisses, are patches light to deep pink in color commonly located on the bridge of the nose, lower forehead, upper eyelids, back of the head, and/or the neck. They are the most common birthmark, especially in light-skinned babies. They typically fade over the first few months to years but may become apparent later in life with flushing.

instruction: My baby's buttocks have a flat grayish spot — should I worry?
output: That sounds like a slate gray macule, which is a flat area of skin containing extra pigment that can look brown, gray, or blue like a bruise. These are most often located on the back or buttocks, are very common especially in dark-skinned babies, usually disappear before school age, and are of no medical significance.

instruction: My newborn protested her wet diaper loudly from day one and seems to cry and eat more than other babies — is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Some infants protest wet diapers from day one and complain loudly until they are changed, fed, and rocked back to sleep. Infants who behave like this tend to spend more time awake than other babies and may cry and eat more. These kinds of differences are normal and can serve as hints of your child’s personality.

instruction: My newborn doesn't seem to care when her diaper is dirty and sleeps a lot — is that okay?
output: Yes. Other newborns won’t seem to notice when their diapers are dirty and may be more likely to object to an exposed bottom during changes. These babies tend to sleep more and eat less frequently than their more sensitive counterparts. These differences are normal and can hint at personality.

instruction: Will my baby's skin color change after birth?
output: All babies, including those with a dark-skinned heritage, have lighter-appearing skin at birth. This gradually darkens as they become older.

instruction: How long do stork bites usually take to fade?
output: Stork bites typically fade over the first few months to years, though they may become apparent later in life with flushing.

instruction: My baby was born by C-section and doesn't have the usual puffiness or red spots in her eyes — is that expected?
output: Yes. If a baby was delivered by C-section, she may not have the puffiness and the whites of her eyes likely will not have any red spots that are commonly seen after vaginal labor.

instruction: I see small spots around the edges of the diaper — what might cause those marks?
output: Some spots appearing around the diaper edges may be due to pressure from the diaper.

instruction: Could the differences in how my newborn reacts to changes hint at her future personality?
output: Yes. Differences such as protesting wet diapers, time spent awake, crying, and how often a baby eats are normal and can serve as hints of your child’s personality. Respecting your baby’s individuality is an important part of being a parent and can help you accept who she will become.

instruction: Why does my newborn have a blood-red spot in the white of one eye and a puffy face?
output: Many parents wonder about this. The blood-red spot and the general puffiness are most commonly caused by pressures exerted during labor. Both the red spot and the puffiness tend to fade in a few days. If the baby was delivered by C-section, she may not have the puffiness or red spots.

instruction: My baby's skin is peeling — is that normal or do I need to treat it?
output: Peeling skin is normal whether the baby is born early, late, or on time, and usually requires no treatment.

instruction: There's fine hair on my newborn's shoulders and back. Will it go away?
output: That fine hair is lanugo, produced toward the end of pregnancy. It is usually shed before birth or soon thereafter. Babies born before their due date are more likely to still have lanugo and it may take a couple of weeks to disappear.

instruction: My baby has tiny white bumps on the nose and cheeks — what are they and how long will they last?
output: Those tiny white bumps are milia, caused by skin gland secretion. This common newborn rash generally disappears on its own within the first two to three weeks after birth.

instruction: My newborn had small blisters at birth that peeled and left dark spots — should I be worried?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. This pattern can be pustular melanosis, a common and harmless newborn rash that often occurs in babies with darker skin; the blisters typically peel and leave dark spots that disappear over several weeks. However, it is always important to have all blister-like rashes evaluated by your baby’s doctor to rule out infection.

instruction: My baby has multiple red splotches with little yellowish-white bumps that come and go — what is that and will it resolve?
output: This description fits erythema toxicum, a very common rash that usually appears within the first few days after birth. It consists of multiple red splotches with yellowish-white bumps in the center that come and go and typically resolve completely in about a week or so.

instruction: My newborn has a swollen area on the scalp where the head was pushed out first during birth — is that serious and will it go away?
output: That swelling is called caput. It is not serious and should disappear in a few days. If you press gently on it, your finger may even leave a small indentation.

instruction: There is firm swelling on one side of my baby's scalp that springs back when pressed — what does that mean and how long will it last?
output: That swelling is likely a cephalohematoma, caused by intense pressure on the head during labor. It represents some bleeding along the outer side of the skull bones (not inside the brain). It is not serious and usually takes six to ten weeks to disappear.

instruction: Can I touch my newborn's soft spot on the head?
output: All babies have two soft spots (fontanelles) where the immature skull bones are still growing together: a larger one toward the front and a smaller one at the back. Parents needn’t be afraid to touch these areas gently, as there is a thick, durable membrane that protects the brain.

instruction: My newborn's breasts are enlarged and sometimes leak a few drops — is this normal and should I squeeze them?
output: Many parents wonder about this. In the first few weeks after birth, babies can be affected by their mother's hormones and their breasts may be temporarily enlarged and may even secrete a few drops of milk. This can occur in boys and girls and normally lasts less than a week, although it can last longer. It is best not to press or squeeze a baby’s breasts, since this won’t reduce swelling and could cause irritation.

instruction: My newborn daughter has a white vaginal discharge and I saw a little blood once — is that okay?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A discharge of white mucus, sometimes with a little blood, is called pseudomenses and is harmless.

instruction: My newborn boy's testicles move in and out of the scrotum and sometimes seem high — is that normal?
output: The scrotum can look small and smooth or large and wrinkled, and the testicles can appear to move in and out of the scrotum and sometimes move as far up as the base of the penis or the crease between the thigh and belly. As long as the testicles are located in the scrotum most of the time, this movement is normal. Some boys have a buildup of fluid called a hydrocele inside the scrotum that will shrink gradually without treatment over several months.

instruction: The scrotum suddenly swelled and seems larger when my baby cries — what should I do?
output: If the scrotum swells suddenly or gets larger when he cries, notify your pediatrician; this could be a sign of an inguinal hernia, which requires treatment.

instruction: My newborn was born with the foreskin attached to the head of the penis — is that normal and can it be retracted?
output: At birth the foreskin is attached to the head (glans) of the penis and cannot be pushed back as it can in older boys. There is a small opening at the tip through which urine flows. If the baby is circumcised, the foreskin is removed and the head of the penis is visible.

instruction: What are 'stork bites' or 'angel kisses' and will they fade?
output: Stork bites (also called angel kisses) are patches light to deep pink in color, most commonly located on the bridge of the nose, lower forehead, upper eyelids, back of the head, and/or the neck. They are the most common birthmark, especially in light-skinned babies, and typically fade over the first few months to years, though they may become apparent later in life with flushing.

instruction: What is a port-wine stain and does it go away on its own?
output: A port-wine stain is a large, flat, irregularly shaped dark red or purple area caused by extra blood vessels under the skin, usually located on the face or neck. Unlike hemangiomas, port-wine stains don’t disappear without treatment. They can sometimes be treated with laser surgery by a plastic surgeon or a pediatric dermatologist.

instruction: My newborn's head has a soft swollen area from delivery. Is it serious and how long will it take to go away?
output: This swelling represents bleeding along the outer side of the skull bones, not inside the brain, and is not serious. It usually takes six to ten weeks to disappear.

instruction: There are two soft spots on my baby's head. Can I touch them?
output: Yes. Babies have two fontanelles (a larger one toward the front and a smaller one at the back). a thick, durable membrane covers them and protects the brain, so parents needn’t be afraid to touch these areas gently.

instruction: My baby was born with a lot of hair. Will it stay the same?
output: Most baby hair falls out during the first six months and is replaced by mature hair. The color and texture of the mature hair may be different from the hair the baby was born with.

instruction: My newborn's breasts are enlarged and a little milk came out. Is that normal?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Exposure to the mother's hormones before birth can cause temporary breast enlargement and a few drops of milk in newborns. This can happen in both boys and girls and normally lasts less than a week, though it can last longer. It’s best not to press or squeeze the breasts because that won’t reduce swelling and could cause irritation.

instruction: My baby girl has a white discharge and a little blood from her vagina. Is this dangerous?
output: Many parents wonder about this. A white mucus discharge and sometimes a little blood (pseudomenses) can occur in infant girls and is harmless.

instruction: I see a small bulge near my baby's belly button when she cries. Could this be a hernia?
output: Yes. A small bulge that appears during crying spells is called a hernia. Small hernias are most commonly seen around the umbilical cord (belly button) but may also appear in a line down the center of the abdomen.

instruction: My baby boy's testicles seem to move up into his groin and back into the scrotum. Should I be worried?
output: This is normal. The testicles can move in and out of the scrotum and sometimes up to the base of the penis or the crease between the thigh and belly. As long as the testicles are located in the scrotum most of the time, this movement is normal.

instruction: The scrotum looks like it has fluid inside and is swollen. Will that go away on its own?
output: Yes. A buildup of fluid in the scrotum (a hydrocele) will usually shrink gradually without treatment over several months as the fluid is reabsorbed by the body.

instruction: When should I notify the pediatrician about my son's scrotum swelling?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If the scrotum swells suddenly or gets larger when he cries, notify your pediatrician, because that could be a sign of an inguinal hernia that requires treatment.

instruction: My son was circumcised and the tip is red with a yellowish discharge. Is that normal?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. The tip of the penis may look red for the first few days and you may notice a yellow secretion; both indicate normal healing and usually disappear gradually within a week. If the redness persists or there is swelling or crusted yellow sores, there may be an infection and you should consult your pediatrician.

instruction: How should I clean my circumcised newborn's penis after the procedure?
output: A light dressing with petroleum jelly may be placed over the head of the penis. The important thing is to keep the area clean. If stool gets on the penis, wipe it gently with soap and water. Some pediatricians recommend keeping a clean dressing until the penis is fully healed; others advise leaving it off. After the circumcision has healed, usually no additional care is required. Occasionally a small piece of foreskin remains; pull that back gently each time the child is bathed and examine the groove around the head to make sure it’s clean.

instruction: My son is not circumcised. How should I care for his penis now?
output: Clean and bathe the uncircumcised penis with soap and water like the rest of the diaper area. Initially the foreskin is attached to the glans, so you should not try to retract it. No cleansing with cotton swabs or antiseptics is necessary. The doctor will tell you when the foreskin has separated and can be retracted safely; this will not be for several months or years and you should never force it. After natural separation occurs, retract the foreskin gently during bathing, rinse the head and inside fold with soap and warm water, then pull the foreskin back over the head.

instruction: When are newborn circumcisions usually done?
output: Circumcision is usually performed in the hospital prior to discharge, but it may be done after discharge during the first weeks at the pediatrician’s discretion. Ritual circumcisions for religious reasons are usually performed in the second week.

instruction: What can make a baby be born larger than average?
output: Common causes of a large baby include being born to large parents, excessive maternal weight gain during pregnancy, a pregnancy that lasts longer than forty-two weeks, overstimulation of the fetus's growth in the uterus, fetal chromosomal abnormalities, the mother's ethnicity, diabetes in the mother before or during pregnancy, and the mother having given birth to other children (often each baby weighs more than the one before).

instruction: Do large babies have any problems after birth?
output: Large babies may have metabolic abnormalities such as low blood sugar and low calcium, traumatic birth injuries, higher hemoglobin levels, or jaundice.

instruction: Why might my baby have been born larger than average?
output: Being born large can be due to several causes listed by doctors: being born to large parents; excessive maternal weight gain during pregnancy; a pregnancy that lasts longer than forty-two weeks; overstimulation of the fetus’s growth in the uterus; fetal chromosomal abnormalities; the mother’s ethnicity; diabetes in the mother before or during pregnancy; and the mother having given birth to other children (often each baby weighs more than the one before it). Large babies may have metabolic abnormalities (such as low blood sugar and calcium), traumatic birth injuries, higher hemoglobin levels, jaundice, various congenital abnormalities, and almost one-third initially have feeding difficulties. Your pediatrician will keep a close watch on these issues.

instruction: What are common reasons a baby is born small?
output: Common causes of a baby being born small include being born early (preterm); being born to small parents; the mother’s ethnicity; fetal chromosomal abnormalities; inadequate weight gain in pregnancy; the mother’s chronic diseases such as high blood pressure or heart or kidney disease; placental issues that lead the baby to be undernourished; the mother’s alcohol or substance use during pregnancy; and the mother’s smoking during pregnancy.

instruction: What measurements will the pediatrician take and how are they used?
output: At every physical exam the pediatrician will measure the baby’s length, weight, and head circumference and will plot them on growth charts to compare with other babies born after the same length of pregnancy; head circumference and length will also be measured and compared to growth charts. In a healthy, well-nourished infant these three measurements should increase at a predictable rate, and plotting them helps the doctor detect and address feeding, developmental, or medical issues.

instruction: My baby was small at birth — will they need tests or extra monitoring?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A small baby may need temperature, blood glucose, and hemoglobin level to be closely monitored. After birth the pediatrician will thoroughly evaluate a small infant and decide when she is ready to go home.

instruction: How often will my baby’s weight, length, and head circumference be checked?
output: The pediatrician will measure the baby’s length, weight, and head circumference at every physical exam and plot them on growth charts.

instruction: My newborn keeps her fists tightly clenched and her feet curve inward — is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Newborns commonly keep their arms and legs close to their bodies and their fists tightly clenched, and their feet naturally curve inward. You should be able to gently straighten the arms and legs. It may take several weeks for her body to unfold from this preferred fetal position.

instruction: Why does my baby make so many grunts, squeaks, and hiccups?
output: Besides crying, newborns commonly make a wide variety of grunts, squeaks, sighs, sneezes, and hiccups. Many of these sounds and sudden movements are reactions to disturbances around her and are signs of how her senses are functioning.

instruction: How close can my newborn see and when will her eyes stop appearing cross-eyed?
output: A newborn’s vision will be best within an 8- to 12-inch (20.3–30.5 cm) range, which lets her see your face as you hold and feed her. When you are farther away her eyes may wander and give a cross-eyed appearance; between two and three months of age her eye muscles will mature, her vision will improve, and both eyes will remain focused on the same thing more of the time. If this does not happen, bring it to the attention of your baby’s pediatrician.

instruction: When should I teach my son to clean his foreskin and how do I do it?
output: After natural separation of the foreskin occurs, retract the foreskin regularly to gently cleanse the end of the penis. As your son gets older you’ll need to teach him how to urinate and wash his penis. Teach him to clean the foreskin by gently pulling it back away from the head of the penis; rinsing the head of the penis and inside fold of the foreskin with soap and warm water; and pulling the foreskin back over the head of the penis. Do not force the foreskin to retract before it is ready, because forcing it could cause painful bleeding and tears in the skin.

instruction: Are feeding problems common in large babies?
output: Yes. Almost one-third of large babies initially have feeding difficulties, and the pediatrician will keep a close watch on feeding issues.

instruction: How do growth charts classify babies as large or small?
output: Growth charts compare a baby’s measurements with other babies born after the same length of pregnancy. Those above the 90th percentile are considered large and those below the 10th percentile are considered small.

instruction: Do the early labels of "large" or "small" predict how big my child will be as an adult?
output: No. Early weight designations as large or small do not predict a child’s size as an adult, but they do help determine whether the baby needs extra attention during the first few days after birth.

instruction: Could being a large baby cause low blood sugar or low calcium?
output: Yes. Large babies may have metabolic abnormalities such as low blood sugar and low calcium.

instruction: If my small baby was born early, will that be listed as a reason for being small?
output: Yes. Being born early (preterm) is listed as one of the common reasons a baby may be born small.

instruction: Will my small infant be allowed to go home right away after the birth?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. After birth the pediatrician will thoroughly evaluate a small infant and decide when she is ready to go home.

instruction: My newborn's fists are tightly clenched — is that normal or a problem?
output: Yes. Newborns often keep their fists tightly clenched, and you should be able to gently straighten them. It may take several weeks for her body to unfold from that fetal position.

instruction: My baby makes lots of grunts, squeaks and hiccups. Should I be worried?
output: No — many newborns make a wide variety of sounds from the beginning, including grunts, squeaks, sighs, sneezes, and hiccups. These sounds and sudden movements are common reactions to disturbances around her.

instruction: Will my newborn recognize my voice right away?
output: Yes. After months in the womb, a newborn will quickly recognize her mother’s voice and possibly her father’s as well. She may quiet down when she listens to soothing music or move gently in time with it.

instruction: My baby seems cross-eyed when I look at her from across the room. Is that normal? I'm nervous about her eyes.
output: Many newborns’ eyes wander and can appear cross-eyed when you are farther away; don’t worry in the first couple of months. Between two and three months her eye muscles usually mature and both eyes focus more. If that does not happen, bring it to the pediatrician's attention. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Can newborns tell the difference between breast milk and other liquids?
output: Yes. By using smell and taste, a newborn can distinguish breast milk from other liquids. Breast milk is naturally sweet and will appeal to a baby’s preference for sweetness.

instruction: We might leave the hospital early. What newborn tests or treatments must still be done?
output: If a newborn leaves early she should still receive appropriate newborn measures such as a hearing screen, newborn metabolic tests, vitamin K administration, antibiotic eye ointment, heart screening, and the hepatitis B vaccine. She should be seen by the pediatrician twenty-four to forty-eight hours after discharge.

instruction: When should I call the doctor after we get home with our baby?
output: Call the doctor sooner than the routine follow-up if the newborn appears listless or feverish, is vomiting, has difficulty feeding, or if her skin develops a yellow color (jaundice).

instruction: How long do most hospitals keep healthy moms and babies after birth?
output: Most hospitals discharge you and your baby within forty-eight hours after a vaginal delivery. After a Cesarean section you may stay three or more days. If born in an alternative birthing center, discharge may occur within twenty-four hours. The decision should be made case by case with the infant’s and mother’s health in mind.

instruction: What kind of car seat do I need to bring home the baby safely?
output: You need a federally approved car safety seat appropriate for your baby’s size, correctly installed rear-facing in the backseat. Follow the car safety seat manufacturer’s instructions and, if possible, have the installation checked by a certified child passenger safety technician.

instruction: I want to do skin-to-skin and nap while holding the baby. Is it okay to fall asleep with her in my bed or chair?
output: Skin-to-skin and breastfeeding time are important, but you should be careful not to fall asleep with the baby in your bed or in a chair. When you or your partner are tired or going to sleep, put the baby back in the bassinet on her back. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What basic things should we have at home before we leave the hospital with our newborn?
output: Before you leave, make sure you have a safe place for the baby to sleep (a crib, bassinet, or portable crib), plenty of diapers, and enough clothing and blankets to keep her warm and protected. Also have a properly installed, rear-facing car safety seat.

instruction: Does holding my newborn really make a difference for growth or development?
output: Yes. Holding your baby gives her a sense of security and comfort and tells her she is loved. Research shows that close emotional bonding actually promotes her growth and development.

instruction: My baby has hiccups a lot, like I did during pregnancy — is that normal?
output: Yes. Newborn hiccups are common; you may even remember similar hiccups from pregnancy. They are one of the many normal sounds and reactions newborns make.

instruction: How can I encourage my baby to sleep apart from me in the bassinet?
output: Swaddling or putting the baby in a sleep wrap may encourage sleeping apart from you. In the hospital, a healthy baby will usually sleep in a hospital-supplied bassinet in your room.

instruction: What are the American Academy of Pediatrics’ minimum criteria for early discharge before forty-eight hours?
output: Minimum criteria mentioned include term delivery, appropriate growth, and a normal physical examination. The Academy notes it often takes forty-eight hours for all criteria to be met and that discharge timing should be decided case by case by physicians and parents.

instruction: When can my newborn be discharged from the hospital?
output: The timing of newborn hospital discharge should be a mutual decision between you and the physician caring for the infant. The American Academy of Pediatrics says the health and well-being of mother and baby should take precedence over financial concerns. The AAP has minimum criteria for discharge before 48 hours after birth: a term delivery, appropriate growth, and a normal physical examination; it often takes 48 hours for all these criteria to be met. The AAP supports legislation based on these guidelines as long as physicians, in consultation with parents, have final authority in determining discharge.

instruction: Is it okay to nap with my healthy newborn in my hospital bed?
output: Be careful not to fall asleep with your baby in your bed or in a chair. If you or your partner are tired or going to sleep, put the baby back in the hospital bassinet on her back. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: How long do the baby blues usually last after birth?
output: About three out of four new mothers experience postpartum blues a few days after birth. These feelings tend to subside as quickly as they develop and typically last no more than several days.

instruction: What is postpartum depression and how common is it? I'm worried I might have it.
output: Postpartum depression involves severe sadness, emptiness, apathy, and despair and may develop a few weeks after birth. It affects about one out of ten new mothers. Symptoms can last many months or more than a year, may worsen with time, and can make a mother feel helpless or incapable of caring for her baby and other children; some may worry about harming themselves or their baby. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What can I do with my time while still in the hospital after giving birth?
output: Take advantage of your time in the hospital to rest, learn from trained professionals, and let your body recover. Maximize breastfeeding and skin-to-skin care with your baby. You can also ask nursery staff to keep an eye on your baby while you rest or tend to personal care. Be careful not to fall asleep with your baby in your bed or in a chair.

instruction: How can I include my older child when a new baby arrives?
output: Including your older child in the new routine can help. Toddlers can retrieve a clean diaper, and older children can take pride in checking for hazards (for example, stray toys) and making sure visitors wash their hands before touching the new baby. As you become comfortable with your routine, be sure to include special times with the older child.

instruction: Can fathers get postpartum depression too? What should they do?
output: Fathers can also suffer from postpartum depression. They should be alert to the signs and seek help early on.

instruction: Why am I crying a lot after giving birth?
output: Many new mothers cry after birth because of hormonal changes associated with birth; these hormonal changes are larger than those experienced in adolescence or during menstrual cycles. These emotional changes can lead to sadness, fear, irritability, anxiety, or even anger toward the baby—this is often called postpartum blues. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: If my newborn is healthy in the hospital, where will she sleep?
output: If you deliver in a hospital and your baby is healthy, she will usually sleep in a hospital-supplied bassinet in your room.

instruction: What should I do if I'm too tired while breastfeeding at night?
output: When you or your partner are tired or going to sleep, put the baby back in the bassinet on her back. You may ask nursery staff to keep an eye on the baby while you rest. Swaddling or a sleep wrap may encourage the baby to sleep apart from you. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Should financial concerns determine how long my baby stays in the hospital?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics believes the health and well-being of the mother and her baby should take precedence over financial considerations. The timing of discharge should be a mutual decision between parents and the physician caring for the infant.

instruction: How common is it for new mothers to feel anxious about caring for a baby at first?
output: It is common for the first few days with a baby to be a mixture of delight, pain, exhaustion, and, especially if this is your first child, apprehension about your capabilities. Once new parents have a few days to get used to routine baby care and get home, things usually start to fall into place. You can rely on friends, family members, and your pediatrician for help and advice.

instruction: What are signs that my emotional state might be more than the baby blues?
output: Postpartum depression involves more severe sadness, emptiness, apathy, and despair than the baby blues. It may develop a few weeks after birth, affects about one out of ten new mothers, and symptoms can last many months or more than a year, worsen with time, and make mothers feel helpless or incapable of caring for their baby and other children; some may worry about harming themselves or their baby.

instruction: I'm worried about juggling multiple children. Will our family settle into new roles?
output: The prospect of taking care of multiple children may worry you, and that makes sense. Given time and patience, every family member will settle into new roles.

instruction: Who should I talk to if my feelings after birth feel too much to handle?
output: Discuss your feelings with your partner, extended family, and close friends. Allow them to relieve you of being the primary caregiver and do not be afraid to ask for help if your concerns seem too great to handle.

instruction: I've been crying a few days after birth — is this normal?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Many new mothers experience emotional changes a few days after birth called postpartum blues or baby blues. About three out of four new mothers have these feelings. They tend to subside quickly, typically lasting no more than several days, and are related to the hormonal shifts that follow birth.

instruction: What's the difference between baby blues and postpartum depression?
output: Baby blues (or postpartum blues) occur a few days after birth, affect about three out of four new mothers, and usually subside within several days. Postpartum depression may develop a few weeks after birth, affects about one out of ten new mothers, and involves more severe symptoms such as intense sadness, emptiness, apathy, despair, withdrawal from family and friends, and may last many months or more than a year and worsen over time; mothers with postpartum depression may feel helpless and may worry about harming themselves or their baby.

instruction: When should I talk to my obstetrician or pediatrician about postpartum feelings?
output: If your feelings are severe and haven’t subsided in about two weeks, talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician or seek help from a mental health professional. Counseling and/or antidepressant medications may be recommended, and if you’re breastfeeding you should check in with your pediatrician.

instruction: Can fathers get postpartum depression too?
output: Yes. Even though they didn’t give birth, fathers can also suffer from postpartum depression; they should be alert to the signs and seek help early on.

instruction: My toddler has started wanting diapers again and has had accidents since the baby came home. Is this normal?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Toddlers may show regression such as insisting on diapers or having accidents after a new baby arrives. These are normal responses to stress and change. The recommended response is to give extra love and reassurance rather than punish, praise appropriate behavior, and help her feel important as a big sister.

instruction: How can my partner become more involved in caring for the baby once we're home?
output: The best approach is to become actively involved. Once home, a partner can and should help feed (if bottle-fed), diaper, bathe, and comfort the baby. Doing these jobs helps bonding and gives the family opportunities to get to know and welcome the new member.

instruction: Is rooming in at the hospital helpful for partners?
output: Depending on the hospital and schedules, rooming in with mother and/or child until going home can help a partner feel less like a bystander, more like a key participant, allow him to get to know the baby from the start, and share an intense emotional experience with the mother.

instruction: What should I tell my older child to help them accept a new baby?
output: Give extra love and reassurance and avoid punishment for stress-related behaviors. Catch and praise appropriate behavior, praise them for "acting like the big sister," tell them there’s plenty of room in your heart to love both them and the baby, let them help with care at times, and supervise them with the baby so they learn to behave safely; over time attachment will build.

instruction: Do pediatricians screen for postpartum depression during baby visits?
output: Many pediatricians now screen for postpartum depression with a questionnaire at each well-child visit in the first six months.

instruction: I feel overwhelmed and I'm afraid I might hurt myself or my baby. What should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Discuss your feelings with your partner, extended family, and close friends and allow them to relieve you of being the primary caregiver when possible. Try reducing stress and anxiety by exercising and getting as much rest as you can. If these feelings are severe and haven’t subsided in about two weeks, talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician or seek help from a mental health professional; counseling and/or antidepressant medications may be recommended (check with your pediatrician if you’re breastfeeding).

instruction: How common are baby blues after birth?
output: About three out of four new mothers experience baby blues a few days after birth.

instruction: How common is postpartum depression and when does it typically start?
output: Postpartum depression affects about one out of ten new mothers and may develop a few weeks after birth.

instruction: What can help reduce stress and anxiety for a new mother?
output: Discuss your feelings with your partner, extended family, and close friends and allow them to help. Reduce stress and anxiety by exercising and getting as much rest as possible.

instruction: If I'm breastfeeding and my doctor recommends antidepressants, what should I do?
output: Counseling and/or antidepressant medications may be recommended for postpartum depression; if you’re breastfeeding, check in with your pediatrician about medications.

instruction: My partner seems distant and conflicted about becoming a parent. What can he do to cope?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A helpful step is to become actively involved in parenting: help feed (if bottle-fed), diaper, bathe, and comfort the baby. These activities help bonding and let him participate fully in caring for the child.

instruction: What should I do if I notice physical problems in my newborn during the first couple of weeks?
output: If you notice any concerning physical conditions in your baby during the first couple of weeks after birth, contact your pediatrician.

instruction: My newborn's hands look a little blue when she is cold. Is that normal and what should I do?
output: It can be normal. Mildly blue or purple hands and feet are usually normal and should return to pink as soon as the baby is warm. It’s common to have questions like this. If skin stays persistently blue, that can mean the heart or lungs are not working properly and the baby is not getting enough oxygen; immediate medical attention is essential.

instruction: Her belly feels hard and swollen and she hasn’t had a bowel movement for two days. What does that mean?
output: If the abdomen feels swollen and hard and the baby has not had a bowel movement for more than one or two days or is vomiting, call your pediatrician. Most likely it is due to gas or constipation, but it could signal a more serious intestinal problem.

instruction: My baby didn’t urinate or poop during the first day after birth. Should I worry?
output: That can be normal. The first urination and bowel movement may be delayed twenty-four hours or more and staff watch for them. However, if the baby does not pass meconium in the first forty-eight hours, further evaluation is required.

instruction: I noticed a little blood in my newborn's stool during the first days. What could cause that?
output: On occasion newborns have a little blood in their bowel movements. In the first few days this usually means a small crack in the anus from stooling and is generally harmless, but let your pediatrician know so the reason can be confirmed since other causes require further evaluation and treatment.

instruction: My baby coughs and sputters when feeding. Is this serious?
output: If the baby drinks very fast, she may cough and sputter; this type of coughing should stop as the feeding routine becomes familiar. It may also relate to how strongly or fast a breastfeeding mother’s milk comes down. If she coughs persistently or routinely gags during feedings, consult the pediatrician because these symptoms could indicate an underlying problem in the lungs or digestive tract.

instruction: My newborn cries a lot and I can’t seem to calm her down. What should I try?
output: All newborns cry often. First make sure she is fed, burped, warm, and in a clean diaper. Hold her and talk or sing until she stops. It makes sense to feel unsure about this. You cannot spoil a baby this age by giving too much attention. If that does not work, wrap her snugly in a blanket or try other soothing approaches. If the crying sounds peculiar, such as shrieks of pain, or persists an unusual length of time, call the pediatrician.

instruction: My partner feels like a bystander after the birth. How can he become more involved?
output: Many partners feel this way. Becoming actively involved helps. Once home he can help feed the baby if bottle-fed, diaper, bathe, and comfort the baby. Rooming in with mother and child in the hospital, when possible, also helps a partner feel less like a bystander and more like a key participant.

instruction: How might a toddler react to a new sibling and how should we respond?
output: A toddler may be confused, frightened by the sight of the mother in bed or attached to tubing, jealous that parents are holding someone else, and may misbehave or regress such as insisting on diapers or having accidents. These are normal responses to stress and change. The best response is to give extra love and reassurance rather than punish, catch her being good, praise her for acting like the big sister, tell her there is plenty of room in your heart for both children, and supervise her with the baby at all times.

instruction: Can a school-age child help with a newborn, or will it be too much for them?
output: A school-age child is often fascinated by pregnancy and childbirth and may be proud and protective once the infant arrives. Let her help take care of the little one at times, but remember she still needs time and attention herself.

instruction: My baby has red marks on the face from forceps. Will these go away?
output: Red marks or superficial scrapes from forceps generally disappear within a few days. Sometimes a firm, flat lump develops in the area from minor tissue damage, but this usually goes away within two months.

instruction: One side of my newborn's shoulder seems weak after a difficult labor. Is that something that will improve?
output: Muscle weakness is a common birth injury caused by pressure or stretching of the nerves and usually affects one side of the face or one shoulder or arm. These muscles generally return to normal after several weeks. Ask your pediatrician to show you how to nurse and hold the baby to promote healing.

instruction: What is newborn jaundice and what causes it?
output: Many normal, healthy newborns have a yellowish tinge to their skin called jaundice. It is caused by a buildup of bilirubin.

instruction: How long might a firm lump from forceps on my baby's head last?
output: Sometimes a firm, flat lump develops where forceps caused minor tissue damage; this lump will usually go away within two months.

instruction: How do pediatricians check for postpartum depression during well-child visits?
output: Many pediatricians now screen for postpartum depression with a questionnaire at each well-child visit in the first six months.

instruction: I’ve been feeling very anxious and low since the baby arrived and rest and exercise haven’t helped for more than two weeks. What should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Reduce stress and anxiety by exercising and getting as much rest as possible. If these feelings are severe and have not subsided in about two weeks, talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician or seek help from a mental health professional. Counseling and/or antidepressant medications may be recommended. If you are breastfeeding, check in with your pediatrician about medications.

instruction: My newborn’s skin looks blue around the lips — what does that mean and what should I do?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Bluing is a sign the heart or lungs are not operating properly and the baby is not getting enough oxygen in the blood. Immediate medical attention is essential.

instruction: The baby still hasn’t passed a bowel movement — when is that a problem?
output: Staff watch for the first urination and bowel movement. Passing stool may be delayed twenty-four hours or more. The first bowel movement or two are black or dark green and very slimy (meconium). If the baby does not pass meconium in the first forty-eight hours, further evaluation is required to make sure no problems exist in the lower bowel.

instruction: There was a little blood in my newborn’s diaper today — should I be worried?
output: Many newborns occasionally have a little blood in their bowel movements. It usually means a small crack in the anus from stooling and is generally harmless. It makes sense to feel unsure about this, and you should let your pediatrician know so they can confirm the reason, since other causes require further evaluation and treatment.

instruction: My baby coughs and sputters while feeding — is that normal?
output: If the baby drinks very fast, she may cough and sputter; this type of coughing should stop as the feeding routine becomes familiar. It may also be related to how strongly or fast a breastfeeding mother’s milk comes down. If she coughs persistently or routinely gags during feedings, consult the pediatrician because these symptoms could indicate an underlying problem in the lungs or digestive tract.

instruction: My newborn cries a lot and nothing seems to help — could it be a medical problem?
output: Many parents wonder about this. All newborns cry, often for no apparent reason. After you’ve made sure she is fed, burped, warm, and in a clean diaper, holding her and talking or singing usually helps. You cannot spoil a baby this age by giving too much attention. If crying sounds peculiar (for example, shrieks of pain) or persists for an unusual length of time, it could mean a medical problem; call the pediatrician and ask for advice.

instruction: My baby has red marks from forceps after delivery — will they heal?
output: When forceps are used during delivery, they can leave red marks or superficial scrapes on a newborn’s face and head; these generally disappear within a few days. Sometimes a firm, flat lump develops in one of these areas because of minor damage to the tissue under the skin, but this usually goes away within two months.

instruction: What does newborn jaundice look like and what causes it?
output: Jaundice is a yellowish tinge to the skin caused by a buildup of bilirubin in the child’s blood. It usually first appears on the face, then the chest and abdomen, and sometimes the arms and legs; the whites of the eyes may also be yellow. Mild jaundice is harmless, but if the bilirubin level continues to rise and is not treated, it can lead to brain injury.

instruction: When do hospitals check newborns for jaundice?
output: Most hospitals now routinely screen newborns for jaundice twenty-four hours after birth using a painless handheld light meter. If the pediatrician suspects jaundice based on skin color, the baby’s age, and other factors, a skin or blood test may be ordered. If jaundice develops before the baby is twenty-four hours old, a bilirubin test is always needed to make an accurate diagnosis.

instruction: We left the hospital before 72 hours — when should our baby be checked for jaundice?
output: Newborns should be checked by a doctor or nurse at three to five days old, since this is when bilirubin levels are highest. Infants discharged before seventy-two hours of age should be seen by a pediatrician within two days of discharge.

instruction: What is phototherapy for newborn jaundice and how is it done?
output: Elevated bilirubin levels can be treated with phototherapy. At the hospital or at home, an undressed infant is placed under special lights or on a specialized light blanket with the eyes covered for protection. This treatment can prevent the harmful effects of jaundice.

instruction: How often should a breastfeeding mother nurse to help keep bilirubin levels down?
output: Breastfeeding mothers should nurse at least eight to twelve times per day; this helps produce enough milk and keeps bilirubin levels low.

instruction: How long can jaundice last in breastfed babies compared with formula-fed babies?
output: In breastfed babies, jaundice may last for more than two to three weeks. In formula-fed babies, most cases go away by two weeks of age.

instruction: My newborn is sleeping a lot and seems hard to wake — when is that a problem?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Newborns spend most of their time sleeping. As long as she wakes every few hours, eats well, seems content, and is alert part of the day, this is normal. If she’s rarely alert, does not wake up on her own for feedings, or seems too tired or uninterested to eat, consult your pediatrician, since such lethargy—especially if it’s a sudden change—may be a symptom of a serious illness.

instruction: My baby seems to be breathing oddly — are there signs that mean I should get help right away?
output: It makes sense to feel worried. If your newborn shows any of these warning signs, notify your pediatrician immediately: fast breathing (more than sixty breaths in one minute), retractions (sucking in the muscles between the ribs so the ribs stick out), flaring of the nose, grunting while breathing, or persistent blue skin coloring.

instruction: My newborn sounds nasal and breathes oddly — is there something simple I can try at home?
output: If your baby’s breathing is unusual, it is most often due to blocked nasal passages. Using saline nasal drops followed by suctioning the mucus from the nose with a bulb syringe may fix the problem; both are available over the counter.

instruction: My baby has yellow skin — what is phototherapy and how is it done at home or the hospital?
output: Phototherapy is light treatment for elevated bilirubin. At the hospital or at home, an undressed infant is placed under special lights or on a specialized light blanket with the eyes covered for protection. This kind of treatment can prevent the harmful effects of jaundice.

instruction: Who is more likely to need treatment for high bilirubin?
output: Babies who are not breastfeeding well, those with considerable bruising and bleeding under the scalp from labor and delivery, and those who have a parent or sibling who had high bilirubin levels and underwent treatment are more likely to need treatment.

instruction: How long can jaundice last in breastfed and formula-fed babies?
output: In breastfed babies, jaundice may last for more than two to three weeks. In formula-fed babies, most cases go away by two weeks of age.

instruction: My newborn sleeps almost all the time and sometimes doesn’t wake for feedings — is that normal?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Every newborn spends most of her time sleeping. As long as she wakes every few hours, eats well, seems content, and is alert part of the day, it’s perfectly normal. But if she’s rarely alert, does not wake up on her own for feedings, or seems too tired or uninterested to eat, you should consult your pediatrician because that change may be a symptom of a serious illness.

instruction: What should I do if my baby sounds congested and is breathing oddly after birth?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If breathing seems unusual, it is most often due to blocked nasal passages. Using saline nasal drops followed by suctioning the mucus from the nose with a bulb syringe may fix the problem; both are available over the counter. However, if you notice fast breathing (more than sixty breaths in one minute), retractions (sucking in the muscles between the ribs so the ribs stick out), flaring of the nose, grunting while breathing, or persistent blue skin coloring, notify your pediatrician immediately.

instruction: What breathing signs should make me call the pediatrician right away?
output: Notify your pediatrician immediately for fast breathing (more than sixty breaths in one minute); retractions (sucking in the muscles between the ribs so the ribs stick out); flaring of the nose; grunting while breathing; or persistent blue skin coloring.

instruction: How should I care for my baby’s umbilical cord stump?
output: Keep the stump clean and dry as it shrivels and falls off. There is no need to use alcohol on the cord; just keep it clean. A quick submersion bath is fine before the cord falls off—dry the stump afterward. Fold the diaper below the cord to keep urine from soaking it.

instruction: I saw a few drops of blood on the diaper when the cord fell off — is that okay?
output: A few drops of blood on the diaper around the time the stump falls off is normal. If the cord actively bleeds, call your baby’s doctor immediately.

instruction: What signs mean the umbilical stump might be infected?
output: Contact your doctor if you notice foul-smelling yellowish discharge from the cord, red skin around the base of the cord, or if the baby cries when you touch the cord or the skin next to it. If the stump becomes infected, it will require medical treatment.

instruction: When should the umbilical cord stump fall off, and when should I see a doctor?
output: The umbilical cord stump should dry and fall off by the time your baby is three weeks old. If it persists beyond that time, a doctor’s visit is needed.

instruction: After the cord falls off there is a small red lump that drains light-yellow fluid — what is that and what happens next?
output: That is an umbilical granuloma, a small reddened mass of scar tissue that can stay on the belly button and drain a light-yellowish fluid. This condition will usually go away in about a week, but if it does not, your pediatrician may need to cauterize the granulomatous tissue.

instruction: My baby’s belly seems to bulge at the navel when she cries — could that be a hernia and is it dangerous?
output: If the umbilical area pushes outward when she cries, it may be an umbilical hernia—a small hole in the muscular part of the abdominal wall that allows tissue to bulge with increased abdominal pressure. This is not a serious condition and it usually heals by itself in the first twelve to eighteen months. Don’t put tape or a coin on the navel; it will not help and may cause a skin rash.

instruction: Will an umbilical hernia always go away on its own?
output: It usually heals by itself in the first twelve to eighteen months. For unknown reasons it often takes longer to heal in African American babies. In the unlikely event it doesn’t heal by three to five years of age, the hole may need surgery.

instruction: Can I use alcohol on the cord stump to keep it clean?
output: There is no need to use alcohol on the cord; just keep it clean. A quick submersion bath is fine before the cord falls off—dry the stump afterward.

instruction: Is it normal for newborns to sleep most of the time and still be healthy?
output: Yes. Every newborn spends most of her time sleeping. As long as she wakes every few hours, eats well, seems content, and is alert part of the day, it’s perfectly normal to sleep the rest of the time.

instruction: My newborn lost some weight after birth — how much weight loss is normal in the first week?
output: It’s normal for a baby to lose excess body fluid after birth. Babies may lose up to one-tenth of their birth weight during the first seven days. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: When should my baby start gaining weight again after the initial loss?
output: Babies should begin gaining steadily on about the fifth day after birth and by about two weeks of age should have returned to their original birth weight.

instruction: How fast do newborns usually gain weight and how much should they weigh at one month?
output: On average a newborn gains about 20–30 grams (about two-thirds ounce) per day and by one month weighs about 10 pounds (4.5 kg).

instruction: My baby had a misshapen skull from birth — will it go back to normal?
output: If your baby’s skull appeared misshapen at birth, it will soon resume its normal shape. Any scalp bruising or eyelid swelling from birth will be gone by the end of the first week or two.

instruction: Is it normal for my newborn to lose the fine hair on the head and have a bald spot?
output: Yes. Fine hair that covered the head at birth may begin falling out and a temporary bald spot on the back of the head from sleeping on the back can occur. This loss is not medically significant and the bare spots will be covered with new hair in a few months.

instruction: My baby has pimples on the face in the third week — what should I do?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Baby “acne” (neonatal cephalic pustulosis) usually appears during the third to fifth week. Place a soft, clean receiving blanket under his head while he’s awake and wash his face gently once a day with a mild baby soap to remove milk or detergent residue. In severe cases your pediatrician may prescribe a face cream.

instruction: Why do my baby’s hands and feet look blue sometimes, and what should I do about it?
output: A newborn’s skin may look blotchy, ranging from pink to blue. Hands and feet in particular may be colder and bluer because the blood vessels are more sensitive to temperature and tend to shrink in response to cold; if you move his arms and legs they quickly turn pink again. Dress him appropriately for the weather, keeping him warm in cool weather and light when hot, using the rule of dressing him in one more layer than you would wear.

instruction: How should I dress my newborn for temperature changes?
output: Your baby’s internal thermostat isn’t fully working yet and he lacks insulating fat, so dress him warmly in cool weather and lightly when hot. A general rule is to dress him in one more layer of clothing than you would wear in the same weather conditions. Don’t automatically bundle him up because he’s a baby.

instruction: When will the umbilical cord stump fall off and what if it oozes a bit?
output: Between ten days and three weeks after birth the umbilical stump should dry and fall off, leaving a clean, well-healed area. Occasionally it may leave a raw spot that oozes a little blood-tinged fluid; keep it dry and clean and it will heal by itself. If it is not completely healed and dry after three weeks, consult your pediatrician.

instruction: My baby’s legs look bowlegged — is that normal and will it fix itself?
output: During the first weeks a baby’s legs and feet may rotate inward, giving a bowlegged look. This condition will usually correct itself over the first year. If the appearance is severe or associated with pronounced curving of the front part of the foot, your pediatrician may suggest a splint or cast to correct it, though those circumstances are extremely unusual.

instruction: What reflexes should my newborn have when trying to feed?
output: Newborns have reflexes that help with feeding. The rooting reflex makes him turn his head toward your hand if you stroke his cheek or mouth, helping him find the nipple. Sucking is present even before birth and has two stages: expression (compressing the breast between tongue and palate to force milk out) and the milking action (tongue moves from areola to nipple). Suction helps secure the breast to the baby’s mouth.

instruction: My newborn seems to shut eyes tightly at bright lights and suck when I put my finger in his mouth — is that normal?
output: Yes. Many parents notice these behaviors. Shutting the eyes tightly to bright light and a reflexive suck when something is placed in the mouth are normal newborn reflexes. Some reflexes last months and others vanish in weeks; sometimes reflexes change into voluntary behavior.

instruction: How much should my baby grow in length during the first month?
output: Most babies grow between 1½ and 2 inches (4.5–5 cm) during the first month.

instruction: Will my baby’s head size change much in the first month?
output: Yes. The average newborn head circumference is about 13¾ inches (35 cm) at birth and grows to about 15 inches (38 cm) by one month. The bones in the skull are growing faster during the first four months than at any other time in life. Boys’ heads tend to be slightly larger by less than ½ inch (1 cm).

instruction: Why are my newborn’s hands and feet bluish and colder than the rest of his body?
output: Many parents worry about this. Newborn blood vessels are more sensitive to temperature and tend to shrink in response to cold, so the hands and feet can look bluer and feel colder. If you move his arms and legs they usually turn pink again. Also, his internal thermostat (the systems that make him sweat or shiver) won’t be working properly for some time, and he lacks the insulating layer of fat in the early weeks, so he is more affected by sudden temperature shifts.

instruction: How should I dress my baby when it’s cool or hot outside?
output: Dress him warmly in cool weather and lightly when hot. A general rule of thumb is to dress him in one more layer of clothing than you would wear in the same weather conditions. Don’t automatically bundle him up just because he’s a baby.

instruction: When will the umbilical cord stump fall off and what should I do if it looks raw?
output: The stump should dry and fall off between ten days and three weeks after birth. Occasionally a raw spot is left and it may ooze a little blood-tinged fluid; keep the area dry and clean and it will heal by itself. If it is not completely healed and dry after three weeks, consult your pediatrician.

instruction: My newborn keeps sucking his hand — is that normal or a sign of hunger?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Sucking and bringing his hand to his mouth are normal feeding cues in the first weeks after birth. Sucking is also a reflex present even before birth and later these movements can be used by the baby to console himself or be comforted by a pacifier or thumb.

instruction: What happens if I stroke my baby’s cheek during feeding?
output: If you stroke his cheek or mouth he will turn his head toward your hand — this is the rooting reflex that helps him find the nipple. At first he’ll root from side to side in decreasing arcs and simply move his head and mouth into position to suck.

instruction: What is the Moro reflex and when does it peak?
output: The Moro reflex is a dramatic startle response: if the baby’s head shifts abruptly, falls backward, or he is startled, he will extend his arms, legs, and neck and then rapidly bring his arms together; he may cry loudly. The Moro reflex peaks during the first month and then disappears after two months.

instruction: I think one side of my baby moves less when he does reflexes. Should I be concerned?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. The Moro and tonic neck reflexes should be present equally on both sides of the body. If you note that a reflex seems different on one side, or that the baby moves one side of the body better than the other, tell your pediatrician.

instruction: Can you explain the tonic neck or 'fencing' reflex and how long it lasts?
output: When your baby’s head turns to one side, the corresponding arm will straighten and the opposite arm will bend, as if he’s fencing. This response is often subtle and may not appear if the baby is disturbed or crying. It disappears at five to seven months of age.

instruction: My newborn grips my finger very strongly — is it safe to let him hold his own weight?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Newborns have a palmar grasp reflex and can grip so strongly in the first days it may seem they could hold their own weight, but don’t try it. He has no control over this response and may let go suddenly.

instruction: When my baby’s feet touch a surface he seems to 'walk' — is that normal and when will it stop?
output: That stepping motion is a normal reflex: if you hold him under the arms and let his soles touch a flat surface he’ll place one foot in front of the other. This reflex helps a newborn crawl to the breast after delivery. It disappears after two months and then can recur toward the end of the first year as the learned behavior of walking.

instruction: What are common feeding cues in the first weeks after birth?
output: Rooting, sucking, and bringing his hand to his mouth are considered feeding cues in the first weeks after birth.

instruction: Will my baby be able to coordinate sucking, breathing, and swallowing right away?
