output: During this period the baby coordinates emerging perceptive abilities—vision, touch, and hearing—with increasing motor skills to develop abilities such as grasping, rolling over, sitting up, and possibly crawling.

instruction: How much weight should my baby gain between four and seven months?
output: Between four and seven months, your baby will gain approximately 1 to 1¼ pounds (0.45–0.56 kg) a month. By his eight-month birthday he will probably weigh about two and a half times his birth weight.

instruction: Is it normal if my baby hasn't started rolling both ways yet?
output: Many parents wonder about this. The time frame varies for different babies. By the end of this period (around four to seven months) he’ll probably be able to roll over in both directions. Most roll from stomach to back before back to stomach, though doing it in reverse is also normal.

instruction: What changes in length and head size happen between four and seven months?
output: Between four and seven months your baby’s length will increase by about 2 inches (5 cm) and his head circumference by about 1 inch (2.5 cm).

instruction: How can I encourage my baby to lift and hold his head during tummy time?
output: Place him on his stomach while he is awake and extend his arms forward. Get his attention with an attractive toy and coax him to hold his head up and look at you. This also is a good way to check his hearing and vision.

instruction: When should my baby be able to sit without leaning on his arms?
output: It will be some time before he can maneuver himself to sitting without your assistance, but by six to eight months, if you position him upright, he’ll remain sitting without leaning forward on his arms.

instruction: What are common movement skills my four-to seven-month-old should show?
output: Movement milestones for a four-to seven-month-old include: rolls both ways (front to back, back to front); sits with, and then without, support of his hands; supports his whole weight on his legs; reaches with one hand; transfers objects from hand to hand; and uses a raking grasp (not a pincer).

instruction: My baby rocks on his stomach and kicks a lot — is that expected?
output: Yes. Around about five months babies may rock on their stomachs, kick their legs, and “swim” with their arms. These abilities help strengthen the upper body and are necessary for rolling over and crawling.

instruction: When will my baby likely develop a pincer grasp with thumb and index finger?
output: He won’t develop the pincer grasp with index finger and thumb until he’s about nine months old. During the fourth to eighth months he will use a mitten- or claw-like grip or raking motion and pick up many things.

instruction: How can I help my baby practice sitting up safely?
output: Once your baby can raise his chest, help him practice sitting up by holding him up or supporting his back with pillows as he learns to balance. Placing interesting toys in front of him gives him a focus as he gains his balance.

instruction: Should I be plotting my baby’s growth measurements, and what if the curve changes?
output: You should have established his position on the growth curve and continue to plot his measurements at regular intervals to make sure he maintains his growth rate. If he starts following a different curve or gains weight or height unusually slowly, discuss it with your pediatrician.

instruction: By when will my baby be able to transfer toys from one hand to the other?
output: By the sixth to eighth month he’ll learn to transfer objects from hand to hand and to turn and twist them.

instruction: What should I do about small objects my baby can reach?
output: Remove any objects from his environment that he could choke on or injure himself with.

instruction: My four-month-old is bringing everything to his mouth — is that normal?
output: Yes. By the fourth month your baby can easily bring interesting objects to his mouth.

instruction: Is it normal for my baby to push up on his arms and arch his back?
output: Yes. Once he can lift his head he will start pushing up on his arms and arching his back to lift his chest. This strengthens his upper body, which is essential for sitting.

instruction: If my baby’s weight gain seems slow, what does the guidance say I should do?
output: If you find he starts following a different growth curve or gains weight or height unusually slowly, discuss it with your pediatrician.

instruction: My 5-month-old still uses a mitten-like raking grasp instead of pinching with thumb and index finger — is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Yes. Using a raking or mitten-like grasp is listed as a movement milestone for the four-to-seven-month period. The pincer grasp with index finger and thumb does not develop until about nine months, and between six and eight months babies learn to transfer objects from hand to hand and turn and twist them.

instruction: What toys are safe and appropriate for a 6-month-old baby?
output: Appropriate toys include an unbreakable plastic or mylar mirror; soft balls (including some that make soft sounds); textured toys that make sounds; toys with fingerholds; musical toys such as bells, maracas, and tambourines; see-through rattles that show the pieces making the noise; old magazines with bright pictures to show him; and baby books with board, cloth, or vinyl pages. Make sure musical and other toys do not have parts that can become loose.

instruction: When can my baby develop the pincer grasp using thumb and index finger?
output: The pincer grasp with index finger and thumb does not develop until about nine months of age.

instruction: How can I help my baby learn to sit up?
output: Once your baby can raise his chest, help him practice sitting up by holding him up or supporting his back with pillows as he learns to balance himself. He’ll soon learn to “tripod,” leaning forward and extending his arms to balance. Placing interesting toys in front of him will give him a focus as he gains balance.

instruction: My baby only rolls from back to front so far. Is that okay?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Rolling from stomach to back often happens before the opposite direction, but doing it in reverse is perfectly normal.

instruction: Should I remove small objects from my baby's environment?
output: Yes. Remove any objects from his environment that he could choke on or injure himself with.

instruction: Is it normal for my 4-month-old to put everything into his mouth?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Yes. By the fourth month your baby can easily bring interesting objects to his mouth.

instruction: At what age will my baby sit without leaning forward on his arms if I position him upright?
output: By six to eight months, if you position him upright, he’ll remain sitting without leaning forward on his arms.

instruction: What vision changes should I expect by four months?
output: By four months your baby’s visual range has increased to several yards or more. He can distinguish more subtle shades of reds, blues, and yellows, and many infants prefer red or blue. Most babies also like increasingly complex patterns and shapes, and they follow faster movements with their eyes and begin to grab moving objects as hand‑eye coordination improves.

instruction: What does “supports his whole weight on his legs” mean and when does that show up?
output: Supporting his whole weight on his legs is listed among the movement milestones for the four-to-seven-month-old.

instruction: My baby lies on his back and grabs his feet and brings them to his mouth — is that normal behavior?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Yes. Lying on his back, your baby can now grab his feet and toes and bring them to his mouth as part of normal exploration.

instruction: Are musical toys okay for my 5-month-old, and any precautions?
output: Musical toys such as bells, maracas, and tambourines are listed as appropriate for a four-to-seven-month-old. Make sure none of the parts can become loose.

instruction: My 7-month-old puts his feet on the floor and bounces — what does that indicate?
output: When you place his feet on the floor he may curl his toes, stroke the surface, use his feet and legs to practice “walking,” or bounce up and down. These are all preparations for the next major milestones: crawling and standing.

instruction: When will my baby be able to transfer objects from one hand to the other?
output: By the sixth to eighth month your baby will learn to transfer objects from hand to hand and to turn and twist them.

instruction: Can my baby follow fast-moving toys and then grab them yet?
output: Yes. By four months he follows faster and faster movements with his eyes, and as his hand‑eye coordination improves he’ll grab those objects as well.

instruction: My 5-month-old seems to prefer red toys. Is it normal for babies to prefer certain colors?
output: Yes. At this stage babies can distinguish subtle shades of reds, blues, and yellows, and many infants prefer red or blue.

instruction: When should I take down the mobile over my baby’s crib?
output: A mobile is good for stimulating vision, but remove mobiles from cribs or play yards as soon as your baby is able to pull or hold himself upright, since by about five months he may sit up and might pull down or tangle himself in it.

instruction: My baby isn’t interested in new toys or pictures. Should I be worried?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Your child’s visual awareness should increase during these months, and if he is not interested in looking at new things, inform your pediatrician.

instruction: My 6-month-old doesn’t babble or imitate sounds. What does that mean?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If he doesn't babble or imitate sounds by his seventh month, it could mean a hearing or speech development problem; if he does not babble or produce a variety of sounds by about six months, alert your pediatrician.

instruction: Our baby was in the NICU after birth. Does that change hearing needs?
output: Infants who were in the neonatal intensive care unit need audiology follow-up at this age. They usually have a screening hearing test at birth and a recommendation to have a behavioral hearing test by six months; you can discuss this with your primary care pediatrician, who may work with an audiologist.

instruction: My baby sometimes turns his eyes in. What should I do?
output: It makes sense to be concerned. If one or both eyes turn in or out, inform your pediatrician.

instruction: How far can a four-month-old see?
output: By four months your baby's visual range has increased to several yards or more, and it will continue to expand.

instruction: Can my baby track a rolling ball now?
output: Yes. In earlier months he might not have tracked a rolling ball, but by this period he will follow moving objects more easily and his ability to track moving objects improves.

instruction: What can I do to encourage my baby's babbling?
output: Talk to him throughout the day. When you recognize a syllable, repeat it back and then say simple words that contain that sound; after six or seven months participation becomes even more important as he begins to imitate sounds.

instruction: My baby had frequent ear infections. Could that affect his hearing now?
output: It makes sense to be concerned. Frequent ear infections can leave fluid trapped in the middle ear space, which could interfere with hearing; if he does not babble or produce a variety of sounds by about six months, alert your pediatrician.

instruction: Is it normal for my baby to be fascinated by a mirror?
output: Yes. A mirror is a source of fascination: the reflected image constantly changes and responds to your child's movements, which can help him begin to realize the person in the mirror is himself during this period.

instruction: When do babies develop full color vision?
output: Developing full color vision is a milestone in the four-to-seven-month period; during this time they can distinguish subtle shades of reds, blues, and yellows.

instruction: Should I remove mobiles from the play yard when my baby can sit up?
output: Yes. By about five months he may be sitting up and might pull down or tangle himself in a mobile, so remove mobiles from cribs or play yards as soon as your baby is able to pull or hold himself upright.

instruction: My 4-month-old curls his toes and bounces when I put his feet on the floor. Is that normal?
output: Yes. When you place his feet on the floor he may curl his toes, stroke the surface, use his feet and legs to practice “walking,” or bounce up and down; these are preparations for crawling and standing.

instruction: If my baby is startled by loud noises but doesn't imitate speech, could he still have partial hearing loss?
output: It makes sense to worry about this. A baby with partial hearing loss can still be startled by loud noises or turn his head toward them and may even respond to your voice, but he will have difficulty imitating speech; if he does not babble or imitate sounds by his seventh month, alert your pediatrician.

instruction: My 4-month-old babbles but I can’t understand him. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Around four months babies start to babble and use rhythms and features of their native language. It often sounds like gibberish, and you may hear them raise and drop their voice as if making statements or questions. It can be up to a year or more before you can interpret the babbling.

instruction: What should I do if my baby isn't babbling or imitating sounds by seven months?
output: It could mean a hearing or speech development problem. It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your child does not babble or produce a variety of sounds by about six months, alert your pediatrician.

instruction: How can I encourage my baby when he repeats the same syllable all day?
output: Talk to him throughout the day. When you recognize a syllable, repeat it back and then say simple words that contain that sound (for example, if he says “bah,” say words like bottle, box, or bonnet).

instruction: My baby was in the NICU. Do we need any special hearing checks?
output: Infants who were in the neonatal intensive care unit usually need audiology follow-up. They typically have a screening at birth and a recommendation for a behavioral hearing test by six months. You can discuss this with your pediatrician, who may work with an audiologist.

instruction: What signs might suggest my baby has partial hearing loss?
output: A baby with partial hearing loss can still be startled by loud noises or will turn his head toward them, and he may even respond to your voice. However, he will have difficulty imitating speech. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: What simple words should I introduce when my baby starts to imitate sounds after six months?
output: Introduce simple syllables and words such as “baby,” “cat,” “dog,” “go,” “hot,” “cold,” “walk,” and familiar names like “Mama” and “Dada.”

instruction: How does a baby learn cause and effect around four to five months?
output: Between about four and five months he begins to notice cause and effect—for example, kicking a mattress and seeing the crib shake or hitting a rattle and hearing a sound. Once he understands he can cause reactions, he experiments more, such as dropping things to see what happens.

instruction: What kinds of toys are safe and interesting for a 5-month-old who likes to drop things?
output: Give unbreakable, lightweight items that are large enough he can’t swallow. Plastic or wooden spoons, unbreakable cups, jar or bowl lids, and boxes are inexpensive and entertaining.

instruction: When does object permanence begin to develop?
output: Sometime after four months, toward the end of the four- to seven-month period, babies begin to realize objects continue to exist when out of sight. Earlier, they may have thought a hidden toy was gone for good.

instruction: My baby had frequent ear infections—could that affect his hearing?
output: Yes. If he had frequent ear infections, he might have fluid trapped in the middle ear space, which could interfere with hearing. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Should all newborns be tested for hearing loss?
output: Yes. All newborns should be tested for hearing loss. Your own observations are an early warning system to know whether further testing is needed, and if you suspect a problem you might ask your pediatrician for a referral to a children’s hearing specialist.

instruction: What does babbling at four months typically sound like?
output: Babbling at about four months uses many rhythms and characteristics of the native language and may include raising and dropping the voice. It often sounds like gibberish even though the baby is practicing speech sounds.

instruction: My 6-month-old finds partially hidden objects but doesn't look for a fully hidden toy. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Finding partially hidden objects is a cognitive milestone for four- to seven-month-olds. Object permanence—understanding that fully hidden objects still exist— develops sometime after four months toward the end of this period.

instruction: How can I tell if my voice is teaching my baby language from birth?
output: From birth babies pay attention to the pitch and level of your voice. A soothing voice can stop crying because the baby hears comfort, while shouting in anger will probably make him cry and show fear because the voice signals something is wrong. By four months he also begins to notice individual sounds like vowels and consonants.

instruction: If my baby isn't producing a variety of sounds by six months, what should I do?
output: If your child does not babble or produce a variety of sounds by about six months, alert your pediatrician. It could indicate a hearing or speech development problem. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: My 5-month-old keeps dropping and banging things — is that normal or is he upset?
output: Many parents wonder about this. This behavior is normal at this age: banging and dropping objects is a way for your baby to learn cause and effect and to test his ability to influence his environment.

instruction: What household items are safe for my baby to experiment with when he gets bored of his toys?
output: Give him items that are unbreakable, lightweight, and large enough that he can’t swallow them. Good choices from around the house include plastic or wooden spoons, unbreakable cups, jar or bowl lids, and boxes.

instruction: When will my baby understand that a toy hidden under a cloth still exists?
output: Sometime after four months your baby will begin to realize that objects continue to exist when out of sight; playing hiding games like peekaboo and observing people and things will help him continue to learn object permanence.

instruction: What cognitive milestones should I expect between four and seven months?
output: Typical milestones in this age range include finding partially hidden objects, exploring things with hands and mouth, and struggling to get objects that are out of reach.

instruction: My baby used to be passive but now yells and bangs for things — why has his personality changed?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Between four and seven months many babies undergo a dramatic change: as they learn to sit up, use their hands, and move, they often become more assertive and attentive, eagerly reaching for things and demanding help by yelling, banging, or dropping objects.

instruction: How should I handle a strong-willed or easily upset baby?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Strongwilled and high-strung babies often need an extra dose of patience and gentle guidance; they don’t adapt easily to changes, so you’ll fare better by recognizing and accommodating his temperament rather than resisting or trying to change it, and talking and cuddling sometimes will calm his nerves while distracting him can refocus his energy.

instruction: My baby is very quiet and doesn’t smile much — does he need less attention?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A shy or sensitive baby often needs personal contact even more than others; give him plenty of time to warm up, have other people approach him slowly, and let him sit on the sidelines before involving him directly so he can become more responsive.

instruction: What signs should make me alert my pediatrician about developmental delay for a four- to seven-month-old?
output: Alert your pediatrician if your baby displays any of the following signs: seems very stiff with tight muscles; seems very floppy like a rag doll; head still flops back when body is pulled up to a sitting position; reaches with one hand only; refuses to cuddle; shows no affection for the person who cares for him; doesn’t seem to enjoy being around people; one or both eyes consistently turn in or out; persistent tearing, eye drainage, or sensitivity to light; does not respond to sounds around him; does not turn his head to locate sounds by four months; or has difficulty getting objects to his mouth.

instruction: My baby does not turn his head to find sounds — should I be concerned?
output: It makes sense to feel concerned. Not turning his head to locate sounds by four months is listed as a sign for which you should alert your pediatrician.

instruction: He reaches with only one hand — is that a problem?
output: It makes sense to feel worried. Reaching with one hand only is listed as a sign you should alert your pediatrician about possible developmental delay.

instruction: Whenever he drops a toy I always pick it up — should I stop doing that?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If he repeatedly drops a toy and screams because you won’t retrieve it, move him to the floor so he can reach it himself rather than always fetching it for him.

instruction: How can I calm my irritable six-month-old who screams when he can’t reach things?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Talking and cuddling sometimes will do wonders to calm an irritable child, and distracting him can refocus his energy.

instruction: Is a baby’s temperament something I can change?
output: Temperament is determined largely by inborn traits such as activity level, persistence, and adaptability; it’s best to recognize and accommodate his natural personality rather than trying to change it, since adapting to his temperament will reduce stress for your family.

instruction: My baby’s head still flops back when I pull him to sit — what does that mean?
output: It makes sense to feel concerned. Head still flopping back when the body is pulled up to a sitting position is listed as a sign for which you should alert your pediatrician.

instruction: My 5-month-old is very quiet and doesn't smile or laugh much. Is this normal or should I worry?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Babies develop at their own pace. A quiet, undemanding baby may still need plenty of personal contact and time to warm up to situations; have others approach him slowly and let him watch from the sidelines before involving him. Also note that not smiling spontaneously by five months is listed as a possible sign of developmental delay, so alert your pediatrician and describe your day-to-day observations.

instruction: How should I introduce a shy or sensitive baby to new people and situations?
output: Give the baby plenty of time to warm up, let other people approach slowly, and allow him to sit on the sidelines before trying to involve him directly. Shy babies can be overwhelmed easily and often need more personal contact; as he feels more secure, he will gradually become more responsive.

instruction: What signs in a young infant under four months should make me alert the pediatrician about possible developmental delay?
output: Alert your pediatrician if the baby seems very stiff or very floppy; if the head still flops back when the body is pulled up to a sitting position; if he reaches with one hand only; refuses to cuddle or shows no affection for his caregiver; doesn’t seem to enjoy being around people; one or both eyes consistently turn in or out; persistent tearing, eye drainage, or sensitivity to light; does not respond to sounds; does not turn his head to locate sounds by four months; or has difficulty getting objects to his mouth.

instruction: My 6-month-old still can't sit with help and doesn't laugh or make squealing sounds. Should I be concerned?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. The inability to sit with help by six months and not laughing or making squealing sounds by six months are listed as possible signs of developmental delay. You should alert your pediatrician, describe your day-to-day observations, and write them down so you don’t forget.

instruction: When can I start giving my baby solid foods?
output: Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended until around six months. You can begin adding solid foods between four and six months. Talk with your pediatrician at the four-month checkup about the timing, especially if there is a strong family history of food allergies or your baby has severe eczema.

instruction: Should I introduce peanuts to my 5-month-old to try to prevent peanut allergy?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Introducing peanut products between four and six months of age may help prevent developing a peanut allergy. If your child has severe eczema or an allergy to egg proteins, talk to your doctor first.

instruction: My baby still has the tongue-thrust reflex and pushes food out. When is it okay to try solids?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Babies are born with a tongue-thrust reflex and most lose it at about four to five months; when that reflex disappears, it’s a good time to begin practicing with solid foods. Also discuss timing at the four-month checkup, especially if there’s a strong family history of food allergies or severe eczema.

instruction: Is it okay to put pureed food in a bottle or use an infant feeder with a nipple?
output: Putting solid foods in a bottle or an infant feeder with a nipple is not recommended. That practice can drastically increase food intake at each feeding, may be a risk for choking, and could lead to excessive weight gain.

instruction: What should I do if my baby turns away or cries when I try to give solids?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Don’t force it; more important than starting solids by a specific date is that you both enjoy mealtimes. Go back to nursing or bottle-feeding for a week or two, then try again, and follow your baby’s cues for hunger and fullness.

instruction: What social and emotional milestones should a four-to seven-month-old baby show?
output: A four-to seven-month-old typically enjoys social play, is interested in mirror images, and responds to other people’s expressions of emotion and appears joyful often.

instruction: My baby does not turn his head to locate sounds and is four months old. What should I do?
output: Many parents worry about this. Not turning his head to locate sounds by four months is listed as a possible sign of developmental delay; you should alert your pediatrician and describe your day-to-day observations, writing them down so you don’t forget.

instruction: Do breastfeeding mothers need to avoid certain foods to prevent allergies in their baby?
output: Mothers who breastfeed do not need to avoid certain foods. Breast milk helps the baby’s body become tolerant of common allergens rather than sensitive to them.

instruction: By seven months, at what distances should my baby be able to follow objects with both eyes?
output: By seven months a baby should be able to follow objects with both eyes at near (about 1 foot or 30 cm) and at far (about 6 feet or 180 cm). If he doesn’t, that is listed as a possible sign of delay.

instruction: I have a strong family history of food allergies and my baby has eczema. When should I discuss introducing solids?
output: Talk with your pediatrician at the four-month checkup about the timing of introducing solid foods, particularly if there is a strong family history of food allergies or your baby has severe eczema.

instruction: What signs suggest possible vision problems in my infant?
output: Alert your pediatrician if one or both eyes consistently turn in or out, if there is persistent tearing, eye drainage, or sensitivity to light, or if the baby does not follow objects with both eyes at the expected near and far ranges by seven months.

instruction: When can I start giving my baby solid foods?
output: Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended until around six months of age. You can begin adding solid foods at four to six months. Many babies lose the tongue-thrust reflex at about four to five months, and when that reflex disappears it’s a good time to begin practicing with solid food. Talk with your pediatrician at the four-month checkup about timing, especially if there is a strong family history of food allergies or your baby has severe eczema.

instruction: Should I avoid peanuts while breastfeeding or delay introducing peanuts to my infant?
output: Introducing peanut products to infants between four and six months of age may prevent a developing peanut allergy. Mothers who breastfeed do not need to avoid certain foods, because breast milk helps the baby become tolerant of common allergens. If your child has severe eczema or an allergy to egg proteins, talk to your doctor first. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: My baby turns away and cries when I try to spoon-feed—what should I do?
output: Don’t force the issue. It’s more important that you both enjoy mealtimes than that he start foods by a specific date. Go back to nursing or bottle-feeding for a week or two, then try again. Follow your baby’s cues for hunger and fullness rather than focusing on specific amounts. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Is it okay to put pureed solid foods in a bottle or an infant feeder with a nipple?
output: Putting solid foods in a bottle or infant feeder with a nipple is not recommended. It can drastically increase food intake at each feeding, may be a risk for choking, and could lead to excessive weight gain. It’s important for your baby to get used to the process of eating—sitting up, tasting new foods, resting between bites, and stopping when full.

instruction: Which foods should I never give my baby because of choking risk?
output: Never offer foods your baby might choke on, such as whole nuts, uncut grapes or cherry tomatoes, or raw carrots or celery. Make sure anything you give is soft, easy to swallow, and breaks down into small pieces he can’t choke on.

instruction: When can my baby start self-feeding with finger foods?
output: Once your baby sits independently, you can begin giving him finger foods to help him learn to feed himself. Most infants can begin learning to self-feed without assistance around eight months. Make sure anything you give is soft, easy to swallow, and breaks down into small pieces he can’t choke on.

instruction: Can I give my infant fruit juice?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends fruit juice not be given to infants, since it offers no nutritional benefit to babies in this age group. Juice should generally be avoided and should definitely not be given as a treatment for dehydration or management of diarrhea. Giving fruit juice to infants and young children may get them used to sweet beverages, which can lead to excessive weight gain and tooth decay.

instruction: Should I introduce new foods one at a time so I can spot reactions?
output: In the past pediatricians recommended starting one new food every few days so you could see if a reaction occurred. New research has shown that it is safe to start multiple foods at once.

instruction: Can I feed my baby rice cereal as the only cereal?
output: Pediatricians now advise parents to choose whole-grain cereals rather than highly processed ones. There is no evidence that one whole-grain cereal is better than another, though some rice cereals have been found to have higher levels of arsenic compared to other grains, so it is important to use a variety of grains rather than sticking with one. Make sure the cereal is very soft or completely pureed until your baby is ready for more texture.

instruction: Do I need to avoid eggs, dairy, soy, fish, or other allergenic foods before six months?
output: There is no evidence that waiting to introduce babysafe (soft), allergy-causing foods such as eggs, dairy, soy, peanuts, or fish beyond four to six months of age prevents food allergy. That is why most allergists and pediatricians recommend early introduction of allergy-causing foods to a baby’s diet. If your child has severe eczema or an allergy to egg proteins, talk to your doctor first. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: Is it okay to give my infant cow’s milk before their first birthday?
output: Cow’s milk is not recommended until after a year of age.

instruction: How should I store homemade baby food and leftovers from a meal?
output: Immediately refrigerate any food not used, and then inspect carefully for signs of spoilage before giving it again. When feeding your baby solid foods, use a small dish instead of the container, jar, or pouch the food came in to prevent contamination with bacteria from the baby’s mouth. Discard the portion left in the dish rather than trying to save it.

instruction: When and how much water should I offer my baby?
output: Hold off on liquids other than breast milk (or formula if you are not breastfeeding) or small amounts of water in a sippy cup. If your infant seems thirsty between feedings, breastfeed him or give him formula, or, after six months, offer small sips of extra water. During hot months when your child is losing fluid through sweat, offer water two or more times a day. If your water is fluoridated, this also will help prevent tooth decay.

instruction: Do I need to add sugar or salt to my baby’s food to make it taste better?
output: Babies do not need the added sugars and salts that adults have grown used to.

instruction: Can I skip purees and try baby-led weaning instead?
output: Parents are often skipping pureed foods in favor of very soft foods that babies can self-feed, a trend called baby-led weaning. Make sure anything you give is soft, easy to swallow, and breaks down into small pieces he can’t choke on. Don’t give any food that truly requires chewing, even if he already has teeth.

instruction: When can I start giving my baby finger foods and what should I give?
output: Once your baby sits independently you can begin giving finger foods. Most infants can begin learning to self-feed without assistance around eight months. Anything you give should be soft, easy to swallow, and break down into small pieces he can’t choke on. Good examples include well-cooked cut-up sweet potatoes, green beans, peas, diced chicken or meat, small pieces of bread, or whole-grain crackers. Don’t give any food that truly requires chewing, even if he already has teeth. After the meal, gently wipe the infant’s mouth with a wet cloth for oral care.

instruction: Which foods should I never give my baby because of choking risk?
output: Never offer foods your baby might choke on. Examples given are whole nuts, uncut grapes or cherry tomatoes, or raw carrots or celery. Also avoid any food that truly requires chewing, even if the baby has teeth.

instruction: Can I skip purees and try baby-led weaning instead?
output: Parents are often skipping pureed foods altogether in favor of very soft foods that babies can self-feed; this trend is called baby-led weaning. Most infants can begin learning to self-feed without assistance around eight months. Start when your baby sits independently and make sure foods are soft, easy to swallow, and break down into small pieces he can’t choke on.

instruction: How should I handle jar or pouch food and leftovers at a meal?
output: When feeding your baby solid foods, use a small dish instead of the container, jar, or pouch the food came in to prevent contamination from the baby’s mouth. Discard the portion left in the dish rather than trying to save it. Immediately refrigerate any food not used, and inspect carefully for signs of spoilage before giving it again.

instruction: Is it okay to give my infant juice, cow's milk, or water?
output: Hold off on liquids other than breast milk (or formula if you are not breastfeeding) or small amounts of water in a sippy cup. The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends that fruit juice not be given to infants; juice should generally be avoided and should definitely not be given as a treatment for dehydration or management of diarrhea. Cow’s milk is not recommended until after a year of age. If your infant seems thirsty between feedings, breastfeed him or give him formula, or after six months offer small sips of extra water. During hot months offer water two or more times a day.

instruction: Do babies need vitamin D supplements, and how much?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends that all infants receive a vitamin D supplement unless they are consuming more than 27 ounces per day of commercial formula that has vitamin D added. Vitamin D supplements of 400 IU (10 mcg) per day are recommended for babies up until age one year, with 600 IU (15 mcg) per day for children over one year. Vitamin D deficiency can produce diseases such as rickets. When children are outdoors for extended periods, they should wear sunscreen, hats, and protective clothing to prevent sunburn and reduce later skin cancer risk.

instruction: When should I start iron supplements for my breastfed baby and what dose?
output: For the first four months, a breastfed baby needs no additional iron because newborn iron stores are sufficient. At four months of age, infants who are partially or completely breastfeeding should be supplemented with 1 mg/kg per day of oral iron until appropriate iron-containing complementary foods (including iron-fortified cereals) are introduced. If there were pregnancy or birth complications such as diabetes, low birth weight, or prematurity, or if your baby was small for gestational age and is taking breast milk, iron supplementation may start in the first month after birth. Once solids are started, the baby will also receive iron from meats, iron-fortified cereals, and green vegetables.

instruction: My baby’s poop changed after starting solids — is that normal or worrying?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Once solid foods are started, stools become more solid and variable in color and have a much stronger odor. Peas and other green vegetables may turn the stool deep green; beets may turn it red (beets can make urine red as well). Stools may contain undigested particles such as hulls of peas or corn and skins of vegetables; this is normal. If the stools are extremely loose, watery, or full of mucus, it may indicate an irritated digestive tract; in that case, consult your pediatrician to determine if there is a digestive problem.

instruction: What should I look for when choosing and using a high chair?
output: By six or seven months your baby will probably sit well enough to use a high chair. Use safety straps, clean the chair seat regularly, and look for a high chair with a detachable tray with raised rims. The raised rims help keep dishes and food in place, and a detachable tray can be cleaned easily.

instruction: Should I make my baby finish a bottle if he doesn’t seem hungry?
output: Some evidence indicates that bottle-fed infants gain weight more rapidly than breastfed babies, perhaps because some parents encourage their infant to finish a bottle. Always let your baby’s hunger cues drive how much he eats, rather than urging him to finish a set amount of food. It is important to get your pediatrician’s advice before making any dietary adjustments.

instruction: Can I feed my baby raw fruits and vegetables, like bananas or apples?
output: You can feed your baby mashed raw bananas, but most other fruits should be cooked until soft. Everything you make should be soft and well-cooked. Cooked fresh vegetables and stewed fruits are easy to prepare. Never offer raw carrots or celery because of choking risk.

instruction: How often should I test well water and what about fluoride?
output: If you are using well water, it is important to have the well water tested on a regular basis for contaminants such as bacteria and toxins. The CDC recommends testing wells used for drinking a minimum of every spring, or more often if there are certain contaminants; another option is adding a well filtration system. You can test your water with home kits to see if your water source is fluoridated. Most city water is fluoridated, and some well water naturally contains fluoride. Your pediatrician or dentist can guide you if additional fluoride is necessary.

instruction: My breastfed baby's stool has bits of corn and tomato skin. Is that normal or should I worry?
output: It’s normal for stools to contain undigested particles like hulls of peas or corn and the skin of tomatoes or other vegetables.

instruction: My baby’s poop is very watery and has mucus. Should I be concerned?
output: It may indicate an irritated digestive tract if the stools are extremely loose, watery, or full of mucus. It’s common to have questions like this. In that case, consult your pediatrician to determine if there is a digestive problem.

instruction: Do breastfed infants need a vitamin D supplement?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends that all infants receive a vitamin D supplement unless they are consuming more than 27 ounces per day of commercial formula that has vitamin D added.

instruction: How much vitamin D should I give my baby?
output: Vitamin D supplements of 400 IU (10 mcg) per day are recommended for babies up until age one year. For children over one year, 600 IU (15 mcg) per day is recommended. An exception is if the infant consumes more than 27 ounces per day of commercial formula that already has vitamin D added.

instruction: Should I let my baby get sun to make vitamin D, or put sunscreen on?
output: Our bodies need sunlight to produce vitamin D, but most people have lower-than-recommended levels partly because of spending more time inside and widespread sunscreen use. All children should wear sunscreen, hats, and protective clothing when outdoors for extended periods to prevent sunburn and reduce the risk of skin cancer.

instruction: When should I start iron supplements for my breastfed baby?
output: For the first four months, a breastfed baby needs no additional iron because birth iron reserves are sufficient. At four months of age, infants who are partially or completely breastfeeding should be supplemented with 1 mg/kg per day of oral iron until appropriate iron-containing complementary foods are introduced.

instruction: My baby was premature. When should I start iron if I'm breastfeeding?
output: If there were pregnancy or birth complications such as diabetes, low birth weight, prematurity, or if your baby was small for gestational age and is taking breast milk, iron supplementation may start in the first month after birth.

instruction: How many naps does a 4–7 month old usually need?
output: Most babies this age still need at least two naps a day, midmorning and midday. Some babies may nap a third time later in the afternoon.

instruction: My baby won’t settle at bedtime. What routine can help him fall asleep?
output: A consistent bedtime routine helps. Try a warm bath, massage, rocking, a story or lullaby, and a breast- or bottle-feeding, and begin these activities before your baby becomes overtired. Settle your baby while he’s drowsy but still awake so he learns to fall asleep on his own.

instruction: Should I put my baby on his back at bedtime even though he rolls over now?
output: Gently place him on his back when settling him to sleep. Many babies at this age are rolling from back to front on their own, so place him drowsy but still awake and then leave the room; if he cries, check and offer a few comforting words and then leave again.

instruction: My baby is teething and has a low fever. Could teething cause a temperature over 101°F?
output: Teething occasionally may cause a low-grade temperature but not over 101 degrees Fahrenheit (38.3 degrees Celsius). If your child has a fever higher than 101°F (38.3°C), it’s probably not from teething and you should consult your pediatrician.

instruction: What can I use to soothe my baby’s sore gums? Are frozen teethers or gels okay?
output: Try gently rubbing or massaging the gums with one of your fingers. Teething rings are helpful and should be made of firm rubber. Frozen teethers tend to get too hard and can cause more harm than good. Never use teething gels to numb the gums; in rare cases they can poison babies’ blood cells and keep them from carrying oxygen.

instruction: Are amber teething necklaces safe and do homeopathic tablets help?
output: Amber teething necklaces do not relieve pain and have caused cases of choking and strangulation; never leave anything around your baby’s neck. Homeopathic teething tablets provide no benefit and in some cases have been made with potentially harmful toxins.

instruction: When should my baby see a dentist and how should I start brushing?
output: Your baby should see a dentist within six months after his first tooth erupts, but no later than twelve months of age. For cleaning when you first start seeing teeth, brush with a soft child’s toothbrush and a smear of fluoridated toothpaste the size of a grain of rice.

instruction: Is it okay to let my baby fall asleep with a bottle or nursing at night?
output: To prevent cavities, never let your baby fall asleep with a bottle or on the breast, either at nap time or at night, because milk can pool around the teeth and create a breeding ground for decay.

instruction: Are mechanical swings safe to calm my baby and how long can I use them?
output: Mechanical swings, especially with cradle attachments, can calm a crying baby. Check the weight limit and age recommendations on the device, use only swings that stand firmly on the floor (not those that hang from door frames), secure your baby with the safety harness at all times, and don’t use a swing for more than half an hour, twice a day.

instruction: When should I use a play yard and are there any product safety checks I should do?
output: A play yard can offer a protected place for your baby to lie or sit before he crawls or walks and once he starts to move about. Be sure the play yard has not been recalled by checking the Consumer Product Safety Commission website for recalled products.

instruction: Is it safe to use teething gels to numb my baby’s gums?
output: No. Never use teething gels to numb the gums. In rare cases they can poison babies’ blood cells and keep them from carrying oxygen.

instruction: Someone suggested homeopathic teething tablets — are those okay to give my baby?
output: Homeopathic teething tablets provide no benefit and in some cases have been made with potentially harmful toxins.

instruction: My friend put an amber teething necklace on her infant. Do amber necklaces help with teething pain?
output: Amber teething necklaces do not relieve pain and have caused cases of choking and strangulation. Never leave anything around your baby’s neck.

instruction: My baby seems really miserable and has a fever of 102°F. Could that be teething?
output: If your child seems particularly miserable or has a fever higher than 101 degrees Fahrenheit (38.3 degrees Celsius), it’s probably not from teething and you should consult your pediatrician. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: When should my baby first see a dentist?
output: Your baby should see a dentist within six months after his first tooth erupts, but no later than twelve months of age, whichever comes first. Pediatric dentists are specially trained to see infants, but going to your family dentist for routine pediatric care is entirely adequate and may be covered by existing health insurance.

instruction: How should I brush my baby’s new teeth?
output: For cleaning when you first start seeing teeth, brush with a soft child’s toothbrush and a smear of fluoridated toothpaste the size of a grain of rice.

instruction: Is it okay to let my baby fall asleep with a bottle or on the breast?
output: Never let your baby fall asleep with a bottle or on the breast, either at nap time or at night. Doing this will keep milk from pooling around the teeth and create a breeding ground for decay.

instruction: We use a cradle attachment swing — any safety tips I should follow?
output: Check the weight limit and age recommendations on the device. Use only swings that stand firmly on the floor, not those that hang from door frames. Don’t use a swing for more than half an hour, twice a day, and secure your baby properly with the safety harness at all times.

instruction: When should I start using a play yard, and are there safety checks I need to do?
output: Once your baby starts to move about, you may need to use a play yard. Even before he crawls or walks, a play yard offers a protected place where he can lie or sit. Be sure the play yard has not been recalled; check the Consumer Product Safety Commission website (cpsc.gov) for recalled products.

instruction: How can I help my four- to seven-month-old baby’s brain development at home?
output: Provide a stimulating, safe environment for exploration. Give consistent, warm physical contact like hugging and skin-to-skin. Be attentive to your baby’s rhythms and moods and respond when he is upset or happy. Talk and sing during daily routines. Engage in face-to-face talk and mimic his sounds. Read books to your baby every day. Use a foreign language at home if you speak one. Engage in rhythmic movement like dancing. Introduce your child to other children and parents when he seems ready. Avoid subjecting your baby to stressful or traumatic experiences.

instruction: My baby isn’t imitating sounds yet. Should I worry about hearing or language?
output: Check with your pediatrician if your baby doesn’t seem to hear sounds or imitate your words.

instruction: How should I handle behavior and discipline for a baby under six months?
output: For the first six months or so, the best way to deal with conflicts is to distract him with an alternative activity or toy. Standard discipline techniques won’t work until his memory span increases around the end of his seventh month.

instruction: I’m frustrated by my baby’s crying for no clear reason. How should I respond?
output: First make sure there’s nothing wrong physically. When he stops, reward him with extra attention, kind words, and hugs. If he starts again, wait longer before turning your attention to him and use a firm tone; this time don’t reward him with extra attention or hugs. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: What kinds of toys should I give to help my baby’s motor skills?
output: Encourage your child to reach for toys. Give him baby blocks and soft toys that can stimulate his hand-eye coordination and fine motor skills. Spend time on the floor playing with your child every day.

instruction: What should I avoid when disciplining my infant?
output: Discipline should never be harsh. Negative disciplinary strategies, including hitting, yelling at, or shaming children, should never be used. The main goal of discipline is to teach limits; discipline means to teach or instruct, not necessarily to punish.

instruction: What should I look for in childcare for my baby?
output: Choose quality childcare that is affectionate, responsive, educational, and safe. Make sure other caregivers understand the importance of forming a loving and comforting relationship with your child. Visit your childcare provider frequently and share your ideas about positive caregiving.

instruction: My 5-month-old keeps grabbing things he shouldn't. How should I handle this behavior now?
output: For the first six months, the best way to deal with conflicts is to distract him with an alternative activity or toy. Discipline techniques won’t work reliably until his memory span increases around the end of his seventh month. Discipline should never be harsh and is meant to teach or instruct rather than punish. Try rewarding desired behavior and withholding rewards for undesired behavior. Negative strategies like hitting, yelling, or shaming should never be used.

instruction: When can I start using standard discipline techniques like time-outs?
output: Standard discipline techniques generally won’t work until his memory span increases, around the end of his seventh month. Before that, distraction with an alternative activity or toy is the best approach.

instruction: My baby cries for no obvious reason and I feel guilty when I don’t pick him up right away—what should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. First make sure there’s nothing wrong physically. When he stops crying, reward him with extra attention, kind words, and hugs. If he starts again, wait longer before turning your attention, use a firm tone when you talk, and this time don’t reward him with extra attention or hugs.

instruction: What kinds of toys should I give to help his hand-eye coordination and fine motor skills?
output: Give him baby blocks and soft toys that can stimulate his hand-eye coordination and his fine motor skills.

instruction: How should I instruct babysitters or other caregivers about bonding and caregiving?
output: Make sure other people who provide care and supervision understand the importance of forming a loving and comforting relationship with your child. Choose quality childcare that is affectionate, responsive, educational, and safe. Visit your childcare provider frequently and share your ideas about positive caregiving.

instruction: I’m nervous about my grandchild starting to sit—what should I watch for and do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Be particularly vigilant as he begins to sit because he will be upright more frequently and likely to tip over. Make your home a safe environment and place all medications and matches out of sight and reach.

instruction: What solids and snacks are appropriate for a 6-month-old grandchild at my house?
output: By the end of this period he will be on solid foods such as infant cereal and pureed vegetables, fruits, and meats. When you’re caring for him, follow the guidance of his parents on what and when to feed him. You can let him explore your versions of "junior foods," such as fruit, soft vegetables, and meats. Stay away from adult canned foods and avoid giving large food chunks that could cause choking. If he is still being breastfed, keep some frozen breast milk in your freezer.

instruction: Is it okay to give him adult canned food if the parents aren’t around?
output: Stay away from adult canned foods. When caring for your grandchild, follow the guidance of his parents on what and when to feed him.

instruction: How often should I play on the floor with my baby?
output: Spend time on the floor playing with your child every day.

instruction: Is it ever acceptable to spank or shame a baby who misbehaves?
output: Negative disciplinary strategies, including hitting, yelling at, or shaming children, should never be used. Discipline should never be harsh and is meant to teach limits and instruct rather than punish.

instruction: What should I have at home if my grandchild visits often?
output: Consider investing in an appropriate crib and other furniture for your home. A high chair will come in handy if he eats meals at your home. A stroller and car safety seat may be useful as well. Keep some everyday medications at your home for a fever or diaper rash, along with a few toys he can enjoy. If he is still breastfed, keep some frozen breast milk in your freezer.

instruction: How can I handle diaper changes when the baby is a "wiggly worm" and won’t stay still?
output: Diaper changing is often a control exercise and you may need all your strength to keep a baby who’s a "wiggly worm" from rolling onto the floor. Switching from the changing table to the bed or floor is often a good idea; remember to keep all diapering supplies close by and within reach.

instruction: How can I encourage my baby to sleep for longer stretches at night?
output: Encourage your child to begin to sleep for extended periods at night. If you need advice about this important step in your infant’s development, ask your pediatrician.

instruction: Are outings like the park or zoo okay for a four- to seven-month-old?
output: Yes. Take him on field trips such as to the park or zoo, and these months are good for interactive outings as his vision, hand transfer, and curiosity are developing.

instruction: What is the main goal of discipline at this age and how should I approach it?
output: The main goal of discipline is to teach limits. Discipline means to teach or instruct, not necessarily to punish. For the first six months use distraction; when you begin disciplining after his memory improves, reward desired behavior and withhold rewards for undesired behavior. Discipline should never be harsh.

instruction: My grandbaby is a wiggly worm during diaper changes. How should I keep him from rolling off the changing table?
output: Switching from the changing table to the bed or the floor is often a good idea. Keep all diapering supplies close by and within reach so you don’t have to step away while changing him. You may need extra strength to hold a very wiggly baby and prevent him from rolling onto the floor.

instruction: The parents and I disagree about discipline. What should I do when I'm caring for my grandchild?
output: Discuss discipline with the baby’s parents and make sure your approach is consistent with their wishes. Your continued role is to assist your child to become an effective parent, so follow the parents’ guidance on discipline.

instruction: What furniture and supplies should I consider having at my home when my grandchild stays over?
output: Consider investing in an appropriate crib and other furniture. A high chair will come in handy if he eats meals at your home. A stroller and car safety seat may be useful as well. Keep some everyday medications for fever or diaper rash and a few toys he can enjoy.

instruction: My daughter says the baby is starting solid foods. What should I feed him when he’s at our house?
output: By the end of this period he will be on solid foods such as infant cereal and pureed vegetables, fruits, and meats. When you’re caring for him, follow the parents’ guidance on what and when to feed. If those foods are on his menu, let him explore your versions of junior foods like fruit, soft vegetables, and meats, and stay away from adult canned foods.

instruction: I’m worried about choking. What should I avoid giving a baby starting solids?
output: It makes sense to feel concerned about choking. Avoid giving large food chunks that could cause choking. Stay away from adult canned foods and offer soft, appropriately sized junior foods such as fruit, soft vegetables, and meats when they are on the parents’ menu.

instruction: My granddaughter is still breastfeeding. Should I keep any breast milk at my house?
output: If your grandchild is still being breastfed, keep some frozen breast milk in your freezer to have on hand when you are caring for him.

instruction: Will overnight stays be hard on my schedule now that the baby is a few months old?
output: Your grandchild should be sleeping through the night, so overnighters will be more enjoyable and less disruptive of your schedule.

instruction: How should I babyproof my home for a visiting grandchild?
output: Make your home a safe environment by placing all medications and matches out of sight and reach. Follow babyproofing guidelines, and store household chemicals out of children’s reach, either high up or in locked cabinets.

instruction: Sometimes both my grandchild and his sibling stay with me. It feels overwhelming. Any tips?
output: It’s common to find this overwhelming. Try caring for one child at a time, especially at first. Doing this allows you to tailor activities and still provide needed relief to your child the parent, who can then focus on the remaining child(ren).

instruction: I want to make memories for my grandchild. What can I do now that will help later?
output: You can promote your grandchild’s development by taking family pictures and videos, creating photo albums, and writing down family stories accompanied by old and new photos.

instruction: My baby keeps pulling my hair. How should I respond so he learns it’s wrong?
output: If you notice him doing something not allowed, such as pulling your hair, let him know it’s wrong by calmly saying “no,” stop him by gently pulling his fingers from your hair, and redirect his attention to something else. Repeat as needed; it may take many repetitions for an infant to learn expected behavior.

instruction: Should I tell the baby 'don’t touch' when he reaches for things he shouldn’t?
output: Avoid saying “Don’t touch.” Use more precise phrases such as “Don’t eat the flowers” or “No eating leaves,” which convey the message without confusing him.

instruction: Where should I keep household soaps and detergents when the baby visits?
output: All household chemicals, such as soaps and detergents, should be stored out of children’s reach, either high up or in locked cabinets.

instruction: How hot should my tap water be to avoid scalding a baby?
output: Household water temperature should be checked at the tap and be no higher than 120 degrees Fahrenheit (48.9 degrees Celsius) to prevent scalding. In many cases, you can adjust your water heater to prevent exceeding this temperature.

instruction: Can I let my 9-year-old watch the baby while I step out briefly?
output: Unless the child is at least twelve, don’t leave him alone with the baby, even if he’s trying to be helpful. Younger children can easily drop or injure an infant without realizing what they’ve done.

instruction: Should I worry about my baby catching a cold after four months? What can I do?
output: Don’t be surprised if your baby catches his first cold or ear infection soon after his four-month birthday, since he now reaches for more things and people. The first line of defense is to keep your child away from anyone known to be sick, and be especially careful around highly contagious diseases such as influenza, RSV, chickenpox, or measles. Remember that people can be contagious a day or so before symptoms, so some exposures are unavoidable.

instruction: Are antibiotics useful for my baby’s common cold or cough?
output: Viruses cause all colds and most coughs, and there are no medicines effective against the common cold. Antibiotics never cure common viral infections, and antibiotics should not be used to treat viral infections.

instruction: Can my three-year-old help give the baby a bath or change diapers by himself?
output: It’s fine for an older child to help at bath or changing time, but don’t leave a child alone with the baby unless he is at least twelve years old. Younger children can easily drop or injure an infant without realizing what they’ve done.

instruction: Why might my baby catch a cold soon after his four-month birthday?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Around four months babies can actively reach for objects and come into contact with more things and people. Also, babies start to lose the immunity they got from their mothers through the placenta around six months, so it’s normal for them to get more viral infections at this age.

instruction: If someone in our play group has measles or chickenpox, what should I do to protect my baby?
output: Keep your child away from anyone known to be sick and be especially careful with highly contagious diseases such as influenza, RSV, chickenpox, or measles. If someone in the play group has one of these diseases, keep your child away from the group until you’re sure no one else is infected. Remember that people can be contagious a day or so before they have symptoms, so it isn’t possible to prevent all exposures.

instruction: Do antibiotics help with the common cold or most coughs?
output: Viruses cause all colds and most coughs, and there are no medicines effective against the common cold. Antibiotics never cure common viral infections and should not be used to treat viral infections.

instruction: My baby might have an ear infection. Do ear infections always need antibiotics?
output: Documented ear infections sometimes require antibiotics. Antibiotics are used to treat bacterial infections, but whether they are needed depends on the specific diagnosis and is best answered by your pediatrician.

instruction: My baby’s mucus turned green—does that mean a sinus or bacterial infection?
output: Sinus infections are very uncommon at this age because the sinuses are so small. Just because mucus is yellow or green does not mean the baby has a bacterial infection; it is normal for mucus to get thick and change color during a viral cold.

instruction: Is bronchitis in babies usually treated with antibiotics?
output: Children rarely need antibiotics for bronchitis, since these infections are almost always caused by viruses.

instruction: Can my four-month-old get a flu shot now, or do we have to wait?
output: Once your child reaches six months, he will be old enough for a seasonal flu shot. Until then, other family members—parents and older siblings—should receive theirs to help protect the baby. There are antiviral medications for influenza, but not all are appropriate for newborns and very young children, and they may not be as effective as prevention via immunization.

instruction: If my pediatrician prescribes an antibiotic, how should I give it to my baby?
output: If an antibiotic is prescribed, your child should take the antibiotic exactly as directed. Never save antibiotics for later use or let other family members use a prescription not intended for them.

instruction: What signs should make me think my four-month-old is getting sick?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Look for signs such as looking pale or having dark circles under the eyes, acting less energetic or more irritable than usual, and fever. The baby may lose weight due to loss of appetite, diarrhea, or vomiting. Some kidney or lung infections can prevent weight gain, and weight loss could also mean a digestive problem such as an allergy to wheat or milk protein or lack of enzymes to digest certain solid foods. If you can’t identify the problem, call your pediatrician and describe the symptoms.

instruction: What common illnesses should I expect at this age?
output: The most common illnesses at this age include bronchiolitis, colds (URIs), conjunctivitis (pinkeye), croup, diarrhea, earache/ear infection, fever, pneumonia, sore throat, and vomiting.

instruction: What vaccines should my baby get at four months?
output: At four months, your baby should receive the second DTaP vaccine, the second polio vaccine, the second Hib (Haemophilus influenzae type b) vaccine, the second pneumococcal vaccine, and the second hepatitis B vaccine (which may be given between one and four months).

instruction: If someone in our play group has chickenpox, can my baby still get exposed even if we avoid them?
output: People can be contagious a day or so before they have symptoms, so it is impossible to prevent some exposures even if you keep your child away from anyone known to be sick. If someone in the play group has chickenpox, keep your child away until you’re sure no one else is infected, but some exposures can still occur before symptoms appear.

instruction: How can improper antibiotic use harm my child long term?
output: It makes sense to worry about this. Repeated or improper use of antibiotics contributes to resistant bacteria and viruses. If a child is infected with resistant bacteria, he might need a different antibiotic or even hospitalization, and a few new strains of bacteria are already untreatable.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby is sick?
output: Look for signs such as paleness or dark circles under the eyes, being less energetic, or more irritable than usual. If the illness is infectious, your baby will probably have a fever. Also watch for weight loss that could come from reduced appetite, diarrhea, or vomiting.

instruction: My four-month-old has lost weight and seems fussy — what might cause poor weight gain?
output: It could be from loss of appetite, diarrhea, or vomiting. Some kidney or lung infections can prevent weight gain, and weight loss at this age could also mean a digestive problem such as an allergy to wheat or milk protein or a lack of digestive enzymes needed to handle certain solid foods. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: When should I call the pediatrician if I think my baby is unwell but I can't figure out why?
output: If you suspect your child may be ill but can’t identify the exact problem, or if you have any concerns about what is happening, call your pediatrician and describe the symptoms. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What vaccines does my baby need at four months?
output: At four months your baby should receive the second DTaP vaccine, the second polio vaccine, the second Hib vaccine, the second pneumococcal vaccine, the second hepatitis B vaccine (may be given between one and four months), and the second rotavirus vaccine (may be given as early as four weeks after the first dose).

instruction: What is the recommended vaccine schedule at six months?
output: At six months: the first dose of influenza vaccine as soon as it’s available in the fall with a second dose one month later; the third DTaP vaccine; the third polio vaccine (which can be given between six and eighteen months); the third pneumococcal vaccine; the third Hib vaccine (depending on the type given for doses one and two); the third hepatitis B vaccine (may be given between six and eighteen months); and the third rotavirus vaccine (depending on vaccine type; one requires two doses and the other three).

instruction: Can I put a rear-facing car seat in the front seat if the baby fits?
output: No. The backseat is the safest place for all children. Never place a rear-facing car safety seat in the front seat of a car with a passenger-side airbag. Always buckle your baby into an approved, properly installed infant car safety seat with a five-point harness before you start the car.

instruction: How do I know when to move my baby from a rear-facing-only car seat to a convertible seat?
output: When your child reaches the top weight or length allowed by his rear-facing-only car safety seat (check the labels or instructions), he will need to use a convertible car safety seat. You may choose a convertible seat that is used rear-facing from birth if it fits your baby properly.

instruction: Is it safe to leave my baby in a bath seat while I step out to get a towel?
output: Never leave a baby alone even for a moment in a bath or near a pool of water. Infants can drown in just a few inches of water, and baby bath seats or supporting rings are not a substitute for adult supervision. Practice touch supervision by staying within arm’s reach anytime your baby is in or near water. Many parents worry about this.

instruction: My baby fell from the changing table but seems a little different — what should I do?
output: Never leave the baby unattended on any place above floor level. If he does fall and seems to be acting abnormally in any way, call the pediatrician immediately. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: How can I prevent scalding from tap water when bathing my baby?
output: Prevent scalding by making sure the water coming out of the faucet is no more than 120 degrees Fahrenheit (48.9 degrees Celsius). Also keep all hot liquids, like coffee and tea, out of baby’s reach and never smoke or eat, drink, or carry anything hot while holding a baby.

instruction: What foods are safe to give a baby so they won't choke?
output: Never give a baby any food or small object that could cause choking. All foods should be mashed, ground, or soft enough to swallow without chewing.

instruction: Could diarrhea and not eating cause my baby to lose weight?
output: Yes. A baby may lose weight due to loss of appetite, diarrhea, or vomiting.

instruction: My car was made after 2002 — should I use the LATCH system for the car seat?
output: If your car is manufactured after 2002, be sure to take advantage of the LATCH system to secure all car seats.

instruction: Are baby bath seats a replacement for watching my baby in the tub?
output: No. Baby bath seats or supporting rings are not a substitute for adult supervision. Never leave a baby alone even for a moment in a bath or near a pool of water and stay within arm’s reach any time your baby is in or near water.

instruction: Can I leave my baby in the bathtub while I step out for just a moment to grab a towel?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Never leave a baby alone even for a moment in a bath or near a pool of water, no matter how shallow. Infants can drown in just a few inches of water. Baby bath seats or supporting rings are not a substitute for adult supervision; stay within arm’s reach anytime your baby is in or near water.

instruction: Are those baby bath seats safe so I can take a quick phone call while the baby is in the tub?
output: No. Baby bath seats or supporting rings are not a substitute for adult supervision. Practice touch supervision by staying within arm’s reach anytime your baby is in or near water.

instruction: How close should I stay to my baby during bath time?
output: Stay within arm’s reach and practice touch supervision anytime your baby is in or near water.

instruction: Can infants drown in very shallow water?
output: Yes. Infants can drown in just a few inches of water.

instruction: Is it okay to leave my baby on the changing table for a minute while I open the front door?
output: Many parents worry about this. Never leave the baby unattended on any place above floor level, such as tabletops, changing tables, or near stairs.

instruction: If my baby falls from a low surface but seems fine, do I have to call the pediatrician?
output: Only if he seems to be acting abnormally. If he does fall and seems to be acting abnormally in any way, call the pediatrician immediately.

instruction: My baby fell off the couch and now seems dazed and not acting right — what should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If he does fall and seems to be acting abnormally in any way, call the pediatrician immediately.

instruction: Can I hold my baby while drinking a hot cup of coffee?
output: Never smoke or eat, drink, or carry anything hot while holding a baby. Keep all hot liquids, like coffee and tea, out of baby’s reach.

instruction: What temperature should I set the faucet to so the bath water won't scald my baby?
output: Prevent scalding by making sure the water coming out of the faucet is no more than 120 degrees Fahrenheit (48.9 degrees Celsius).

instruction: How should I prepare foods so my baby doesn't choke?
output: Never give a baby any food or small object that could cause choking. All foods should be mashed, ground, or soft enough to swallow without chewing.

instruction: Can I leave small toys or pieces of food within my baby's reach on the floor?
output: Never give a baby any food or small object that could cause choking. Make sure all items are not reachable if they could cause choking and that foods are mashed, ground, or soft enough to swallow without chewing.

instruction: Are cords from blinds or electrical cords a hazard for my baby?
output: Make sure he cannot get tangled up in cords such as electrical cords or those on window shades.

instruction: Is it safe to smoke while holding my baby if I step outside on the porch?
output: Never smoke while holding a baby. The guidance is to avoid smoking while holding the baby.

instruction: Can I leave a cup of hot tea on a low table if my baby is crawling nearby?
output: Keep all hot liquids, like coffee and tea, out of baby’s reach. Do not leave hot liquids where the baby can get to them.

instruction: Can I leave my 8-month-old alone for short periods now that she’s mobile?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Mobility brings dangers that must be constantly monitored, and there cannot be any unsupervised time.

instruction: What basic childproofing steps should I do right now?
output: Secure cabinets and drawers, place dangerous and precious objects out of reach, and make perilous rooms such as bathrooms inaccessible unless she’s supervised.

instruction: How can I let my baby explore safely while encouraging her independence?
output: Fill a low kitchen cabinet or drawer with safe objects for her to discover, pad edges of hard tables or replace them with soft-edged ottomans, and equip the home with cushions of different shapes so she can move over and around them.

instruction: My nine-month-old still can't sit up by herself — should I worry?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If she is not sitting by herself by nine months, let your pediatrician know.

instruction: What are typical weights and heights for an eight- to twelve-month-old?
output: A typical eight-month-old boy weighs between 17.5 and 22 pounds (8–10 kg), with girls about a half pound less. By the first birthday the average child has usually tripled birth weight and is 28 to 32 inches (71–81 cm) tall.

instruction: How does head growth change between eight months and one year?
output: Head growth between eight and twelve months slows from the first six months. A typical head circumference is about 17½ inches (44.5 cm) at eight months and about 18 inches (46 cm) at one year.

instruction: She stands with her belly sticking out and her back swaying — is that normal?
output: Yes. When a child first stands her belly may protrude, her rear may stick out, and her back may sway forward; this is perfectly normal until she develops a confident sense of balance sometime in the second year.

instruction: My baby’s toes turn inward when she lies on her back — will that go away?
output: This common condition usually disappears by twenty-four months. If it persists, your pediatrician may show you foot or leg exercises, and if it is severe your pediatrician may refer you to a pediatric orthopedist.

instruction: Her feet turn outward when she starts to walk — should I be concerned?
output: When she takes her first steps her feet may turn outward because the ligaments of her hips are still loose and her legs rotate outward. During the first half of her second year the ligaments will tighten and her feet should point straight.

instruction: When should I step in to help my baby and when should I let her try on her own?
output: Your child will give cues. Intervene when she is acting frustrated rather than challenged. For example, if she is crying because a ball is wedged under the sofa or she has pulled up and can’t get down, she needs your help; at other times let her solve problems on her own.

instruction: Is it okay to feed my nine-month-old because it’s faster and less messy?
output: You may be tempted to feed her for speed, but that deprives her of a chance to learn how to feed herself. Give her opportunities to discover and strengthen her self-feeding skills.
